GPU-accelerated VKS clusters further provide Kubernetes-based deployment for containerized AI workloads with configurable vGPU profiles.

The Model Gallery in Harbor organizes models by project, and catalog items within VCF Automation define specific AI workload patterns available to users.

Operational parameters for conversational AI tasks are defined through configurable agent deployments, including chat history length, session expiration, and linked knowledge bases.

The platform also supports the deployment of PostgreSQL databases for AI workload data storage needs.

While the platform provides the foundational capabilities and tools for defining AI tasks, the organization administrator is responsible for granting permissions before users can request specific AI catalog items, thereby controlling which implementation tasks are available to which teams.

The assignment of user roles, such as Data Scientist or DevOps Engineer, is required for provisioning specific resources like PostgreSQL databases.

The customer must leverage these platform mechanisms to establish and enforce their specific definitions and policies for AI task support and operational parameters.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and document knowledge limits of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) to provide sufficient information to assist relevant stakeholder decision making.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define the criteria as to whether Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) achieved intended purposes and stated objectives to determine whether its development or deployment should proceed.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA implements a comprehensive validate-then-publish workflow that serves as a critical viability checkpoint, gating AI model deployment. MLOps engineers leverage the Triton Inference Server to validate models for inference functionality, rigorously evaluating their performance and safety against defined business use cases. This includes examining inference requests for malicious behavior and conducting thorough functional testing to determine a model's fitness for production deployment.

Deep learning VMs, provisioned from PAIF images, provide an environment for pre-deployment prototyping, fine-tuning, and validation, allowing data scientists to assess whether models achieve their intended objectives before committing to production investment. These DL VMs operate on the same GPU-accelerated infrastructure used in production, ensuring viability assessments are performed under representative conditions.

The Model Gallery functions as the definitive gate between validation and deployment, requiring models to be stored within it before they can be deployed via Model Runtime. MLOps engineers validate models against the organization's security, privacy, and technical requirements prior to upload, and only models that successfully pass this gate become available for production. Model integrity is further verified through hash code validation and unique content digests, confirming that the artifact assessed for viability is precisely the one deployed to production.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms for assessing and gating AI model deployment, the organization remains responsible for defining the specific criteria, intended purposes, and stated objectives against which AI and Autonomous Technologies are evaluated. The customer must establish the organizational security, privacy, and technical requirements that MLOps engineers apply during the validation process.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and document negative, residual risks (defined as the sum of all unmitigated risks) to both downstream acquirers and end users of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define the criteria and responsible party(ies) for superseding, disengaging or deactivating Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) that demonstrate performance or outcomes inconsistent with intended use.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides robust mechanisms for superseding, deactivating, and disengaging Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) workloads.

VI administrators maintain control over the foundational infrastructure, enabling the removal of GPU-accelerated workload domains or modification of Supervisor configurations that underpin AI services. Organization administrators manage namespace imports and catalog item permissions, allowing for the revocation of access to AI workloads by adjusting permissions for AI catalog items.

The platform's REST API facilitates programmatic deactivation of AI workloads and associated resources through namespace deletion endpoints.

For model supersession, the Model Gallery supports versioned revisions stored as immutable OCI artifacts, where each push of changed model data creates a new revision with a unique digest, ensuring an updated model can supersede a previous version while preserving a complete revision history. Model revision tagging supports version management, requiring explicit selection of the intended version rather than defaulting to 'latest'.

Role-based access control, implemented via OIDC groups and namespace-scoped permissions, precisely defines the parties authorized to perform supersession and deactivation actions across all platform levels.

While the platform delivers the technical means and access controls for these actions, the customer is responsible for establishing the specific criteria and operational processes that define when AI/AAT performance or outcomes are deemed inconsistent with intended use. This includes defining the thresholds, monitoring strategies, and decision-making workflows that trigger the supersession, disengagement, or deactivation mechanisms provided by the platform.

Description: Mechanisms exist to monitor the functionality and behavior of the deployed Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides multi-level production monitoring for deployed Artificial Intelligence (AI) workloads. VCF Operations monitors GPU consumption within GPU-enabled workload domains at the cluster, host system, and host properties levels, delivering broad infrastructure-level visibility into AI workload behavior across the deployment. Each deep learning virtual machine includes a pre-installed NVIDIA DCGM Exporter, which monitors GPU metrics and workload behavior. These GPU metrics are collected by Prometheus at configurable scrape intervals and visualized in Grafana dashboards, providing fine-grained, near-real-time telemetry for individual AI workloads. The vSphere Client further provides GPU performance monitoring at both the host and cluster levels through Performance Charts, integrating this view with the broader vSphere management interface.

VMware vDefend contributes to monitoring the functionality and behavior of deployed AI workloads by providing continuous network-level behavioral monitoring. Security Intelligence detects anomalous activity, new traffic flows, and unauthorized communications around AI workloads, alerting administrators and enabling ongoing policy adjustments. Its Segmentation Monitoring offers dedicated views for environment-level traffic flows and can generate policy recommendations. The integrated IDS/IPS provides ongoing threat detection through a dedicated security dashboard, monitoring exploit attempts, command-and-control traffic, lateral movement, and exfiltration patterns targeting or originating from AI workloads. Network Detection and Response applies behavioral analysis to identify suspicious activity, categorizing events by impact type for prioritized response.

While VCF provides comprehensive infrastructure, resource, and network-level monitoring for AI workloads, the platform does not inherently monitor AI system functionality at the application layer. Customers are responsible for implementing mechanisms to track specific AI model performance, output quality, and behavioral drift.

Description: Mechanisms exist to measure Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT)-related risks to deployment context(s) through review and consultation with industry experts, domain specialists and end users.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to regularly assess the effectiveness of existing controls, including reports of errors and potential impacts on affected communities.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and document unmeasurable risks or trustworthiness characteristics.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to gather and assess feedback about the efficacy of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT)-related measurements.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize input from domain experts and relevant stakeholders to validate whether the Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) perform consistently, as intended.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA establishes robust mechanisms for domain expert validation of Artificial Intelligence and Autonomous Technologies. The platform implements a role-based workflow that clearly delineates responsibilities, ensuring that domain-appropriate experts review AI systems at each stage.

MLOps engineers are designated as the primary domain experts for assessing model quality and safety, validating models against organizational security, privacy, and technical requirements before they are uploaded to the Model Gallery. This validation encompasses comprehensive testing of inference functionality using the Triton Inference Server, evaluating performance and safety against defined business use cases, and examining inference requests for malicious behavior.

Deep learning VMs provisioned from Private AI Foundation images offer dedicated testing environments where these domain experts can conduct hands-on evaluations of model behavior, supporting prototyping, fine-tuning, validation, and inference workloads under conditions that mirror production.

The Model Gallery functions as a critical gate, requiring explicit domain expert validation before models can proceed to production deployment via Model Runtime. Access to Private AI Services is rigorously controlled through OIDC groups, with namespace-scoped permissions enforcing these domain expert roles directly within the platform, rather than relying solely on procedural compliance.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific criteria for "intended performance" and consistency, establishing the comprehensive set of security, privacy, and technical requirements, and articulating the business use cases against which models are validated. Furthermore, the customer must define the broader engagement process for all relevant stakeholders to ensure their input is incorporated into the validation lifecycle, beyond the technical domain expert roles enforced by the platform.

Description: Mechanisms exist to evaluate performance improvements or declines with domain experts and relevant stakeholders to define context-relevant risks and trustworthiness issues.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides a comprehensive multi-level monitoring infrastructure to detect and evaluate AI workload performance changes over time. VCF Operations monitors GPU consumption across GPU-enabled workload domains at the cluster, host system, and host properties levels, making performance trends visible across the deployment. Each deep learning VM includes a pre-installed NVIDIA DCGM Exporter, with metrics collected by Prometheus at configurable intervals and visualized in Grafana dashboards, offering time-series data to reveal performance improvements or degradations. The vSphere Client further augments GPU performance monitoring at both host and cluster levels.

The Model Gallery preserves model version history through a revision tracking system, storing revisions as immutable OCI artifacts, which allows domain experts to deploy and test different revisions independently to evaluate performance changes.

Deep learning VMs provisioned from Private AI Foundation images provide the necessary testing environment for domain experts to evaluate performance differences between model versions. These DL VMs support prototyping, fine-tuning, validation, and inference workloads on the same GPU-accelerated infrastructure used in production, ensuring performance comparisons reflect real deployment conditions.

The integrated validate-then-publish workflow mandates that MLOps engineers validate models for inference functionality and evaluate performance and safety against business use cases, including examination of inference requests for malicious behavior and functional testing. This validation gate applies to both new models and updated versions, requiring domain expert review of performance characteristics before any changes are promoted to production.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms and workflow gates for performance evaluation and expert review, the customer is responsible for establishing the specific organizational policies, processes, and criteria for defining context-relevant risks and trustworthiness issues. This includes formalizing the roles and responsibilities of domain experts and relevant stakeholders in interpreting performance data, documenting findings, and implementing risk mitigation strategies.

Description: Mechanisms exist to validate the information source(s) and quality of pre-trained models used in Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) training, maintenance and improvement-related activities.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides comprehensive mechanisms to validate the source and quality of pre-trained models prior to their integration into the deployment pipeline. The platform inherently validates AI workloads leveraging NVIDIA GPU Cloud (NGC) containers, which are a curated, pre-tested, and GPU-optimized catalog for AI and machine learning.

For disconnected environments, a Harbor registry serves as a controlled intermediary, securely storing NGC catalog container images. Access to this registry is secured through validated credentials and requires the Harbor CA certificate to be trusted by Private AI Services.

The Model Gallery within Harbor stores ML models as immutable OCI artifacts, with each revision assigned a unique digest to ensure integrity. OCI metadata provides crucial details on model contents, dependencies, and traceability to their origin.

The platform supports the validation workflow through deep learning VMs provisioned from PAIF images, offering environments for AI prototyping, fine-tuning, validation, and inference workloads, including testing inference functionality with the Triton Inference Server and evaluating performance and safety against business use cases.

While the platform provides the necessary technical infrastructure and tools, the organization is responsible for implementing the specific operational processes. This includes ensuring that MLOps engineers perform the required validation against the organization's security, privacy, and technical requirements before models are published to the Model Gallery. Furthermore, the organization must establish and enforce procedures for checking the hash code of model files to validate integrity before deployment and maintain models within a controlled environment to prevent redundant copies and preserve integrity.

Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides robust event logging capabilities for AI and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) systems, specifically addressing the documentation of data sources and the identification of individuals involved in result verification. The platform links knowledge bases to multiple data sources, documenting the specific databases queried by the AI system and tracking data source activity through document counters and media type visibility. Furthermore, role-based access control, enforced via OIDC groups, establishes clear accountability for AI system operations, separating roles such as VI administrator, organization administrator, project administrator, DevOps, data scientist, and MLOps with namespace-scoped permissions. The MLOps validation workflow explicitly identifies the responsible engineer who validates and publishes models to the Model Gallery, ensuring traceability for verification activities.

While the platform tracks knowledge base indexing operations at a document level, providing aggregate visibility into data processing, it does not record which specific input data matched individual queries. Similarly, although the infrastructure monitoring stack provides time-series telemetry for GPU workload activity and agents support configurable chat history, these mechanisms do not provide per-session start and end timestamps for individual AI agent use events. Customers must implement additional logging or process controls to capture specific input data matches for individual queries and to record precise per-session start and end times for each AI agent use.

Description: Mechanisms exist to report any serious incident involving operational Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) to relevant authorities as to when and where the serious incident occurred, in accordance with mandated reporting timelines.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to perform an investigation when there is a serious incident involving operational Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) that documents a:
 (1) Root Cause Analysis (RCA);
 (2) Risk assessment of the incident; and 
 (3) Description of corrective actions taken, including measures implemented to prevent a recurrence of the incident.
Response:
VMware vDefend provides robust investigative data and tools essential for responding to serious incidents involving operational Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT). Security teams leverage the Security Services Platform interface to investigate individual threat events and correlated campaigns, assessing their scope and impact. Security Intelligence further enhances this by displaying detected suspicious traffic events, categorized by impact type, to prioritize response efforts. Intelligent Assist delivers critical indicators of compromise with context on severity and vulnerability impact derived from IDS/IPS and NDR detections, and can recommend remediation policies through human-guided interaction to facilitate incident triage.

While vDefend furnishes the necessary data and insights to support a Root Cause Analysis, the formal RCA process, comprehensive risk assessment documentation, and the planning and implementation of corrective actions, including measures to prevent recurrence, remain organizational governance activities. The definitive documentation of root cause, risk rating, and corrective actions must be managed and maintained through the organization's established incident response and governance processes.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to analyze anomalous Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) behavior and provide escalation paths for human oversight, including:
 (1) Real-time review; and
 (2) Intervention.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides infrastructure-level monitoring to surface anomalous AI workload behavior, leveraging GPU consumption and resource utilization patterns. Deep learning virtual machines include a pre-installed NVIDIA DCGM Exporter that monitors GPU metrics and workload behavior, with metrics collected by Prometheus at configurable scrape intervals and visualized in Grafana dashboards. This near-real-time telemetry enables operators to detect unexpected GPU utilization patterns, thermal events, or workload failures. The vSphere Client offers additional GPU performance monitoring at the host and cluster levels.

VMware vDefend contributes network-level anomaly detection by continuously monitoring network traffic flows and applying behavioral analysis to identify suspicious activity around AI workloads. The Suspicious Traffic feature uses detectors aligned to MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques, presenting detected events by severity on the Security Overview Dashboard for real-time review. Network Detection and Response (NDR) correlates these events into campaigns for analyst review.

The platform's role-based architecture establishes multiple human oversight points throughout the AI workload lifecycle. VI administrators must add Private AI Services, and organization administrators control user access to AI catalog items. MLOps engineers validate models for inference functionality, performance, and safety against business use cases, including examination of inference requests for malicious behavior, before models are published.

For intervention, the REST API provides endpoints for programmatic deactivation of AI workloads through namespace deletion. Organization administrators can revoke access to AI catalog items, and VI administrators can modify Supervisor configurations. vDefend enables intervention through policy adjustment, allowing administrators to modify firewall policies or deploy microsegmentation rules to isolate or restrict network access when unauthorized or unexpected traffic is detected.

While the platform provides robust infrastructure and network-level anomaly detection, real-time review, and comprehensive human oversight and intervention capabilities, the customer is responsible for implementing specific mechanisms or integrating third-party solutions to analyze anomalous AI behavior at the application or model layer, such as output quality degradation, bias drift, or unexpected decision patterns, and for integrating these findings into the established escalation and intervention workflows.

Description: Mechanisms exist to require human review and clear escalation paths for approval for high-risk or ambiguous Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) actions.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA establishes robust mechanisms for human review and approval throughout the AI workload lifecycle through its comprehensive role-based access control architecture. The platform enforces a clear separation of responsibilities across roles such as VI administrator, organization administrator, project administrator, DevOps engineer, data scientist, and MLOps engineer, each with namespace-scoped permissions. This architecture creates a defined chain of human approvals, requiring a VI administrator to deploy the GPU-accelerated workload domain and add the Private AI Services Supervisor Service, and an organization administrator to grant permissions before users can request AI catalog items.

The validate-then-publish workflow serves as the primary human-in-the-loop gate for AI model deployment, where MLOps engineers are mandated to validate ML models against organizational security, privacy, and technical requirements, including rigorous testing of inference functionality, performance, safety, and examination for malicious behavior, before models can be uploaded to the Model Gallery. Access to the Model Gallery in Harbor is restricted by configured user access per project, ensuring only authorized reviewers can publish models. Furthermore, access to Private AI Services is authorized via OIDC groups, linking human review authority directly to the organization's identity provider, and high-privilege operations like importing namespaces are centralized under the organization administrator's token. The platform's built-in role hierarchy, enforced by namespace-scoped permissions, inherently defines clear escalation paths from data scientists to MLOps engineers to organization administrators for high-risk or ambiguous actions.

While the platform provides the technical framework and enforcement mechanisms for human review, approval, and escalation, the customer is responsible for defining the specific criteria that constitute "high-risk or ambiguous" AI actions. This includes establishing the detailed policies and procedures for model validation, assigning personnel to the platform's defined roles, and ensuring these processes are consistently followed and documented within their operational framework.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to detect and contain emergent or collusive behaviors among multiple Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT), including:
 (1) Automated or human-triggered containment; and 
 (2) Formal investigation to determine the root cause of agentic cascades or collusion.
Response:
VMware vDefend provides robust network-level mechanisms for detecting and containing anomalous inter-workload communication patterns that may indicate emergent or collusive behaviors among Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) workloads. Its Security Intelligence continuously monitors traffic flows, identifying new or unapproved communication paths between AI workloads that deviate from established microsegmentation policies. This network-level visibility serves as an early indicator of coordinated anomalous activity. The Network Detection and Response (NDR) component correlates detected events across the environment, linking suspicious network activity from multiple AI workloads into campaigns and mapping them to MITRE ATT&CK tactics, thereby supporting formal investigations into potential agentic cascades or collusion.

For containment, vDefend offers both automated and human-triggered capabilities. Administrators can rapidly deploy microsegmentation rules to isolate or restrict misbehaving workloads, providing human-triggered containment. Additionally, proactive microsegmentation policies can be pre-defined to automatically block any emergent inter-workload communication that falls outside the expected communication envelopes of AI workloads, ensuring automated containment.

While vDefend delivers essential network-layer detection, correlation, and containment, the comprehensive detection of emergent behavior, collusion, or agentic cascades at the application or model layer requires specialized AI-specific observability tooling. Furthermore, the customer is responsible for establishing and executing the formal investigation processes to determine the root cause of such behaviors, leveraging the network insights provided by vDefend.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to validate AI agent to AI agent communications to:
 (1) Detect poisoning or consensus manipulation; and 
 (2) Identify rogue or compromised AI agents in distributed or multi-agent environments.
Response:
VMware vDefend establishes robust mechanisms to validate AI agent-to-agent communications at the network layer, significantly contributing to the identification of rogue or compromised AI agents in distributed or multi-agent environments. The Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation policies, restricting communication paths between AI agent workloads to an explicit allowlist, thereby achieving a zero-trust environment and limiting the blast radius in the event of a compromise. vDefend IDS/IPS inspects inter-workload traffic for known exploit patterns, command-and-control communications, and lateral movement, while Network Detection and Response applies behavioral analysis to identify suspicious activity, such as unexpected connections or abnormal traffic volumes, indicative of a rogue or compromised agent. Furthermore, Distributed Malware Prevention inspects files exchanged by AI agents, including model files and configuration data, for malicious payloads. Security Intelligence monitors traffic flows to detect new or unapproved communication paths and changes in communication patterns among agents, providing visibility into adherence to expected communication topologies.

While vDefend enforces and monitors the network-layer communication paths and detects compromised agents through network behavioral analysis and threat signature matching, it does not inspect the application-layer semantics of AI agent messages. Detecting poisoning or consensus manipulation at the application layer requires capabilities beyond vDefend's domain.

Description: Mechanisms exist to proactively prevent harm by regularly identifying and tracking existing, unanticipated and emergent Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT)-related risks.
Response:
VMware vDefend provides robust network security capabilities that contribute to the prevention of harm from Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) workloads. It actively detects and contains threats targeting or emanating from AI infrastructure at the network security layer. Through its Intelligent Assist feature, vDefend recommends security policies designed to stop active attacks, such as preventing lateral propagation from compromised AI workloads. These AI-generated remediation rules are subject to a distinct naming convention and require human approval before activation. Furthermore, vDefend's integrated IDS/IPS and Network Detection and Response (NDR) capabilities are instrumental in surfacing anomalous behavior associated with AI infrastructure, delivering critical network-level signals that feed into broader risk identification efforts.

The proactive identification and ongoing tracking of existing, unanticipated, and emergent AI and AAT-related risks constitute an organizational risk management process that extends beyond the technical scope of vDefend. Organizations are responsible for establishing and maintaining separate risk management frameworks and processes to systematically identify, catalog, and track these emergent risks over time, ensuring comprehensive risk governance.

Description: Mechanisms exist to protect human subjects from harm.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to assess and document the environmental impacts and sustainability of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT).
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides comprehensive mechanisms to assess the environmental footprint and sustainability of AI workloads. VCF Operations delivers granular GPU utilization and power monitoring data across GPU-enabled workload domains, including cluster, host system, and host properties levels. This data feeds into VCF Operations' sustainability and green metrics, enabling organizations to track the energy and environmental impact of their AI workloads within the broader VCF platform.

Deep learning virtual machines include a pre-installed NVIDIA DCGM Exporter, which monitors critical GPU metrics such as utilization, memory usage, and thermal data. These metrics are collected by Prometheus at configurable intervals and visualized through Grafana dashboards, providing direct relevance for energy consumption analysis.

Furthermore, the platform enhances resource efficiency through configurable vGPU profiles and VM classes, allowing administrators to right-size GPU resources to workload requirements, thereby optimizing hardware utilization and reducing overall power consumption. The vSphere Client offers additional GPU performance monitoring capabilities at both host and cluster levels through Performance Charts, providing operators with detailed insights into resource utilization patterns.

While the platform furnishes the essential data infrastructure, monitoring tools, and resource optimization capabilities required to assess environmental impact, the organization remains responsible for establishing its specific criteria for environmental impact and sustainability, defining the scope of assessment, and implementing formal processes for documenting these assessments and their findings.

Description: Mechanisms exist to respond to and recover from a previously unknown Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT)-related risk when it is identified.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA, built upon the robust VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) platform, provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for responding to and recovering from previously unknown AI-related risks. VCF's underlying resilience features, such as vSphere High Availability for automatic VM restart, Distributed Resource Scheduler for workload rebalancing, vMotion for live migration, and snapshot-based point-in-time recovery, enable rapid infrastructure-level response by allowing operators to isolate, migrate, or restore AI workloads.

PAIF extends these capabilities with AI-specific response mechanisms, including programmatic deactivation of AI workloads via REST API namespace deletion, immediate prevention of new provisioning by revoking access to AI catalog items, and restricting access to Private AI Services through OIDC group membership modifications.

For recovery, the Model Gallery supports versioned model revisions with immutable manifests, facilitating rollback to known-good model versions, while isolated GPU-accelerated workload domains and namespace-scoped permissions contain the blast radius of incidents.

VMware vDefend further contributes by employing machine-learning-based detection and behavioral analysis to identify anomalous network activity indicative of unknown threats, with Security Intelligence flagging unusual compute entity behavior and Intelligent Assist recommending remediation policies for rapid containment.

While the platform provides robust technical tools, the effective implementation of these mechanisms requires the customer to define and operationalize specific incident response and recovery policies and procedures.

This includes establishing processes for the identification of previously unknown AI-related risks, orchestrating the use of the provided response and recovery capabilities, and conducting post-incident recovery planning.

Recovery activities beyond network-level containment and policy remediation, such as restoring affected AI systems, validating their integrity, or comprehensive post-incident recovery planning, fall outside the direct scope of vDefend and necessitate organizational incident response and recovery processes.
End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure actions to fine-tune Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) do not compromise existing safety and/or security controls.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA establishes robust infrastructure and workflows to ensure that fine-tuning activities for Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) do not compromise existing safety and security controls. Deep learning VMs, provisioned from PAIF images, serve as isolated, GPU-accelerated workload domains, confining all fine-tuning activity to defined infrastructure boundaries. This prevents unintended impacts on other systems.

A critical validate-then-publish workflow functions as a safety gate, requiring MLOps engineers to validate fine-tuned models against organizational security, privacy, and technical requirements before they can be uploaded to the Model Gallery. This validation includes comprehensive testing of inference functionality, performance, and safety against business use cases, including examination for malicious behavior. A fine-tuned model must pass the same rigorous validation gate as any new model, preventing any bypass of established safety checks before reaching production inference endpoints.

The Model Gallery's versioning system provides an immutable rollback path. Model revisions are stored as OCI artifacts with immutable manifests, ensuring that each change creates a new revision with a unique digest. Previous model versions remain readily available, allowing operators to revert to a pre-fine-tuning revision if an updated model exhibits degraded safety or security behavior. Model integrity is further verified through hash code validation, confirming that the specific revision deployed to production is precisely the one that successfully passed validation.

While the platform provides the essential technical mechanisms and enforcement points, the customer is responsible for defining the specific security, privacy, and technical requirements against which models are validated. This also includes establishing the MLOps processes and assigning human accountability for the execution of these validation activities.

Description: Mechanisms exist to track Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT)-related risks are difficult to assess using currently available measurement techniques or where metrics are not yet available.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prioritize, respond to and remediate Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT)-related risks based on assessments and other analytical output.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides operational mechanisms for responding to and remediating AI-related risks at the infrastructure level.

The underlying VMware Cloud Foundation platform includes resilience capabilities such as vSphere High Availability, Distributed Resource Scheduler, vMotion, and snapshot-based recovery, enabling rapid response actions against identified risky AI workloads. These capabilities allow operators to isolate, migrate, snapshot, or restore AI workloads as part of a risk response plan.

PAIF layers AI-specific remediation controls, allowing organization administrators to revoke access to AI catalog items, preventing further provisioning of risky workload types. The REST API provides endpoints for namespace deletion, enabling programmatic deactivation of AI workloads. VI administrators can modify Supervisor configurations, and access to Private AI Services can be restricted by modifying OIDC group membership.

For remediation through model replacement, the Model Gallery supports versioned revisions stored as OCI artifacts with immutable manifests, allowing operators to roll back to a previous verified revision or MLOps engineers to publish corrected models. GPU-accelerated workload domains are deployed as isolated, containing the scope of remediation, and namespace-scoped permissions allow targeted access changes.

VMware vDefend contributes to prioritizing, responding to, and remediating security risks affecting AI workloads running on VMware Cloud Foundation by providing severity-based prioritization, multiple response mechanisms, and policy-based remediation at the network layer.

vDefend categorizes detected threats by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low) and assigns impact scores to correlated campaigns, helping security teams prioritize network-level risks to AI workloads based on analytical output.

When security risks are identified, vDefend offers response options such as deploying microsegmentation rules to isolate affected AI workloads, updating IDS/IPS profiles, or adjusting Gateway Firewall policies. For remediation, vDefend's recommendation engine generates firewall policies based on observed traffic patterns, and the monitor-then-enforce deployment model allows validation of remediation policies against real traffic before full enforcement.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms for responding to and remediating identified AI-related risks, particularly at the infrastructure and network security layers, the broader organizational process for defining, assessing, and prioritizing all categories of AI-related risk—including model performance, fairness, regulatory compliance, and reputational risks—and establishing the overarching risk management framework remains the responsibility of the customer.

The customer must implement the comprehensive risk assessment and management program that leverages these platform capabilities.

Description:
Measures exist to ensure Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) are designed and developed to:
 (1) Achieve an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity; 
 (2) Perform consistently in those respects throughout the AAT system's lifecycle; and
 (3) Be effectively overseen by competent individuals.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides the infrastructure and workflows to achieve and maintain accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity across the AI system lifecycle. The validate-then-publish workflow mandates MLOps engineers validate models for inference functionality using the Triton Inference Server, evaluating performance and safety against defined business use cases, including examination of inference requests for malicious behavior and functional testing.

Deep learning VMs, provisioned from PAIF images, offer dedicated environments for prototyping, fine-tuning, and validation, operating on the same GPU-accelerated infrastructure as production to ensure development-time validation accurately reflects deployment conditions. For consistent performance throughout the lifecycle, model revisions are stored as OCI artifacts with immutable manifests and unique digests, establishing an integrity verification chain that ensures the validated model is cryptographically identical to the artifact deployed to production via Model Runtime.

VCF Operations continuously monitors GPU consumption at the cluster, host system, and host properties levels, with each DL VM including a pre-installed DCGM Exporter for Prometheus collection at 5-second intervals and Grafana visualization, providing continuous performance telemetry. The underlying VCF platform further enhances resilience through vSphere High Availability, Distributed Resource Scheduler, and vMotion, ensuring consistent operation through infrastructure events.

The platform enforces robust role separation across VI administrator, organization administrator, project administrator, DevOps engineer, data scientist, and MLOps engineer roles, each with namespace-scoped permissions. The MLOps role is designed with specific validation proficiency requirements, and access to Private AI Services is authorized through OIDC groups tied to the organization's identity provider. High-privilege oversight is centralized under the organization administrator role, as only their token can be used for namespace API calls.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical capabilities and controls, the customer is responsible for defining the specific "appropriate level" of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity for their AAT systems. This includes establishing the policies, processes, and metrics for validation and ongoing performance evaluation. Furthermore, the customer must define the criteria for "competent individuals," assign personnel to the platform's roles, and implement the overarching governance and oversight processes to ensure the effectiveness of these measures throughout the AAT system's lifecycle.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) are designed and developed so its operation is sufficiently transparent such that output can be easily interpreted by personnel implementing the AAT.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA (PAIF) provides robust platform features and documentation that directly address several requirements for Artificial Intelligence and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) implementation. The platform comprehensively documents computational and hardware resource requirements, detailing system architecture for PAIF deployments, GPU-accelerated workload domain specifications, vGPU profile configurations, VM class definitions tailored for AI workloads, and NVIDIA GPU and driver compatibility. VI administrators are empowered to configure vGPU-based VM classes, defining GPU resource allocations for AI workloads, and the platform specifies the Supervisor Services (Contour, Harbor, Private AI Services) essential for AI workload deployment.

For event logging, PAIF documents multiple capabilities, including cloud-init script logs, vGPU guest driver installation logs, NVIDIA-related errors and license server communication within syslog, and DCGM Exporter metrics collected by Prometheus for Grafana visualization. Knowledge base indexing operations generate detailed document-level tracking data, encompassing deleted, modified, unmodified, skipped, and failed-to-download documents. These logging mechanisms, including specific file paths and configuration parameters, are thoroughly documented in the PAIF administration guide.

Human oversight measures are supported through a documented role-based architecture, defining six distinct roles—VI administrator, organization administrator, project administrator, DevOps engineer, data scientist, and MLOps engineer—along with their respective responsibilities and permission scopes. The platform mandates and documents a validate-then-publish workflow as the required gate for model deployment, assigning MLOps engineers responsibility for validation against organizational requirements. For model characteristics, OCI metadata describes model contents and dependencies, and the Model Gallery tracks revisions with unique digests, providing implementers with essential version and integrity information for each deployed model.

While PAIF delivers the foundational platform and tools, the customer retains responsibility for ensuring that the specific Artificial Intelligence and Autonomous Technologies (AATs) deployed on the platform include comprehensive documentation for all aspects of the control. This includes providing the contact details of the AAT provider, errata from the AAT's initial conformity assessment, detailed information necessary to interpret the specific outputs of the AAT, and the projected usable lifetime of the AAT. The customer must also define and implement the specific policies and procedures for human oversight and model validation within the framework provided by PAIF.

VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA (PAIF) provides the foundational infrastructure and workflows for incorporating human domain knowledge into Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) performance.

For fine-tuning, PAIF offers Deep Learning VMs pre-configured with essential ML frameworks, enabling organizations to fine-tune models. The platform ensures an auditable record of model lineage through unique digests assigned to each revision pushed to the Model Gallery within Harbor, documenting the evolution of models through human-driven improvements.

For Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), PAIF includes the Data Indexing and Retrieval service, which provides a Knowledge Bases interface. This allows domain experts to curate, manage, and update knowledge bases linked to various data sources, thereby augmenting model responses without altering the underlying model.

Furthermore, the Agent Builder within PAIF facilitates the embedding of business rules and domain-specific guidance at the prompt level through its "Add Instructions" setting, allowing human expertise to directly shape AI behavior.

While PAIF provides robust mechanisms for fine-tuning, RAG, and the application of business rules, it does not natively include capabilities for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) or built-in content moderation features. Organizations requiring these specific mechanisms must implement them either on the provided Deep Learning VMs or through external tooling.

Additionally, while the platform offers the technical means for documenting the application of human domain knowledge, the organization remains responsible for defining and enforcing the policies and processes that govern the *extent* of human involvement and the comprehensive documentation of these activities across all employed AI/AAT systems.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure the deployment of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) includes appropriate technical and organizational measures so that AAT are used in accordance with the AAT developer-provided instructions for use.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA establishes a structured deployment workflow that enforces prerequisite gates aligned with developer-provided instructions. Models must be uploaded to the Harbor model gallery using the `vcf pais models push` command before an endpoint can be deployed. Model endpoints are deployed as Kubernetes resources in namespace-scoped environments, configured through the Model Runtime tab in VCF Automation, requiring a model URL and a saved secret. A completion model endpoint must be running in Model Runtime before an agent can be deployed in Agent Builder. This sequenced workflow prevents out-of-order deployments and enforces the operational dependencies defined by model developers.

VCF Automation's Quickstart wizard creates predefined catalog items from Private AI Foundation blueprints, establishing standardized deployment procedures. Custom forms allow administrators to control which inputs users can modify at request time, constraining deployment parameters to values consistent with developer instructions. Deployment automation is supported through YAML code generation for `kubectl` commands and REST API access to deployed model endpoints, enabling reproducible deployments that follow documented procedures. Cloud-init script logs for Deep Learning VM deployments are written to `/var/log/dl.log`, providing an audit trail of deployment activities.

Organizational deployment controls are supported through explicit administrative gates and role separation. Org admins must grant permissions before project members can request AI catalog items, controlling who can deploy AI workloads on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters. MLOps engineers distribute ML models across Deep Learning VMs, Kubernetes clusters, and RAG applications through a central Harbor registry, while data scientists and DevOps engineers deploy workloads from self-service catalog items within their assigned projects and namespaces. Agent Builder provides an "Add Instructions" setting that allows deployers to configure system messages and prefixes, constraining the agent's behavior in accordance with intended use.

While the platform provides robust technical and administrative mechanisms to enforce adherence to developer instructions, the customer is responsible for defining the specific content of those developer-provided instructions, configuring the custom forms with appropriate constraints, and establishing the overarching organizational policies and procedures that govern the creation, review, and management of these instructions and the associated deployment processes.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to assign human oversight of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) to prevent or minimize the risks to:
 (1) Health; 
 (2) Safety; and/or 
 (3) Fundamental rights.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides comprehensive platform mechanisms that enable and enforce human oversight across the Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) lifecycle. The platform implements a mandatory validate-then-publish workflow for machine learning models, establishing a critical human decision point where MLOps engineers must validate models against organizational security, privacy, and technical requirements, including potential risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights, before models are uploaded to the model gallery. Models are stored as immutable OCI artifacts with unique digests, and their integrity must be verified by checking hash codes prior to deployment, ensuring that only approved and verified models proceed to production.

A robust, layered role-based access control model enforces strict separation of duties, requiring multiple levels of human approval from organization administrators, VI administrators, and project administrators before AI catalog items can be requested or AI workloads provisioned. This architecture ensures that no single individual can move an AI workload from model selection through deployment without passing through multiple human approval stages, each representing an opportunity to evaluate potential risks.

Operational visibility is provided through GPU performance monitoring via the vSphere Client, DCGM Exporter, Prometheus, and Grafana, allowing oversight personnel to observe AI workload behavior, detect anomalies, and intervene when AI systems behave unexpectedly. VCF Operations extends this with broader infrastructure metrics.

For agent deployments, Agent Builder requires a running completion model endpoint and allows deployers to configure system messages and prefixes to constrain agent behavior. The VCF Automation catalog item model further reinforces human oversight by requiring explicit organization administrator configuration before any AI workload can be provisioned through self-service, establishing a clear human gate before AI capabilities are made available to end-users.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific policies, procedures, and criteria for assessing risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights. This includes explicitly assigning human oversight roles and responsibilities, establishing the thresholds for intervention, and developing the overarching governance framework that leverages these platform capabilities to ensure effective human accountability and risk mitigation.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to ensure Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) oversight measures are commensurate with the:
 (1) Assessed risk(s); 
 (2) Level of autonomy; and 
 (3) Context of use.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA provides the infrastructure-level mechanisms necessary to scale Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) oversight measures in proportion to assessed risks, the level of autonomy granted to AI workloads, and their specific context of use.

The platform's tiered role-based access control model allows organizations to calibrate the intensity of human oversight based on a workload's risk profile. Organization administrators control the availability of AI catalog items and their assignment to projects, enabling the restriction of high-risk workloads to projects with more stringent approval gates. VI administrators provide an infrastructure-level gate by deciding which Supervisor Services are enabled on each workload domain, while Project administrators manage namespace-scoped permissions and project membership. This layered authorization structure facilitates the imposition of more or fewer approval steps commensurate with the assessed risk of the AI workload.

The VCF Automation catalog and blueprint system supports context-appropriate deployment controls, allowing for customized blueprints and forms to meet specific organizational needs and use cases. Different catalog items can be created with varying levels of pre-configured constraints, such as tightly constrained blueprints for autonomous decision-making versus more flexible options for human-supervised analytics.

Infrastructure policies further enable matching resource allocation and isolation to the risk context of the workload. For workloads with higher autonomy, PAIF's Agent Builder provides the "Add Instructions" setting to configure system messages and prefixes that constrain agent behavior at deployment time, with each agent deployment requiring a running completion model endpoint as a prerequisite.

The validate-then-publish workflow for ML models in the Harbor model gallery provides an additional risk-commensurate gate, ensuring MLOps engineers validate models against organizational requirements and verifying model integrity through hash codes and immutable OCI artifact digests before deployment.

Workload-level observability scales with the context of use through GPU performance monitoring via the vSphere Client, DCGM Exporter, Prometheus, and Grafana, allowing administrators to monitor utilization patterns and detect anomalies. VCF Operations extends this monitoring to broader infrastructure metrics, enabling more intensive observation for higher-risk or more autonomous AI workloads and lighter-touch monitoring for lower-risk, human-supervised workloads.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms, the organization remains responsible for defining its specific risk profiles, levels of autonomy, and contexts of use. It must establish the policies and procedures that dictate how these oversight measures are applied and ensure they are consistently commensurate with the defined criteria. The configuration of role-based access controls, blueprint constraints, agent instructions, and monitoring thresholds must be performed by the customer to align with their established governance framework.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure no action or decision is taken by the deployer of an Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) based solely based on AAT-generated evidence, unless that evidence has been separately verified and confirmed by at least two (2) individuals with the necessary competence, training and authority.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.
End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure the input to Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) is relevant to the intended purpose of the AAT.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA provides comprehensive mechanisms to ensure the relevance of input data to Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) systems. The Data Indexing and Retrieval service enables data scientists to curate knowledge bases, assembling them from deliberately selected data sources. These sources are explicitly added by authorized users who define their purpose and properties, ensuring data is chosen by personnel who understand the AI/AAT system's intended function. During agent deployment, administrators can attach specific knowledge bases and utilize an "Add Instructions" setting to send system messages that constrain the model's scope to topics directly relevant to the intended purpose.

The underlying Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture further reinforces data relevance by employing a semantic retrieval step. This process ensures that at query time, only contextually appropriate chunks from the curated knowledge base are retrieved and passed to the completion model, acting as an inherent relevance filter.

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) at the project and namespace levels restricts the creation, modification, and linking of data sources and knowledge bases to authorized roles, ensuring that decisions regarding data pipelines are made by personnel with appropriate domain knowledge.

While PAIF provides robust technical controls and architectural safeguards, the customer remains responsible for establishing and enforcing organizational policies and procedures that govern the definition of intended purposes for AI/AAT systems. This includes ensuring that authorized personnel possess the necessary domain expertise to accurately curate data sources, configure agent instructions, and manage access controls to align input data with the defined purpose.

Infrastructure provisioning, including GPU-accelerated workload domain configuration and Private AI Services activation, follows a structured, documented sequence.

Model acquisition and validation leverage the Harbor Model Gallery, where MLOps engineers upload models as OCI artifacts, each receiving an immutable manifest with a content digest, creating an auditable record of model availability and provenance. The `vcf pais CLI` further traces models to their gallery, store, and tag.

Compute provisioning supports documented and role-scoped deployment workflows via VCF Automation, direct vSphere clusters, or kubectl commands, ensuring the provisioning method is tied to the performing stakeholder.

Model serving through Model Runtime and the Agent Builder wizard sequences agent configuration, model endpoint selection, instruction definition, and data retrieval settings, establishing a documented chain from the model to the deployed agent.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical capabilities for traceability and event sequencing, customers are responsible for establishing and enforcing organizational policies and procedures to ensure these mechanisms are consistently utilized. This includes defining internal processes for leveraging the platform's documentation features, maintaining records of design decisions, and integrating the platform's auditable outputs into their broader AI governance and compliance framework.

Description: Mechanisms exist to develop and implement fact-checking techniques to verify the accuracy and veracity of information generated by Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT).
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides robust infrastructure capabilities that directly support the development and implementation of fact-checking techniques for AI-generated content. The Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture within Private AI Services is a primary mechanism, grounding large language model responses in organizational data from external knowledge bases rather than relying solely on the model's training data. Data scientists can construct these knowledge bases from diverse data sources, link multiple sources, and configure automatic index refresh policies to maintain data currency, thereby structurally enhancing the accuracy and veracity of AI output.

Furthermore, the validate-then-publish workflow for ML models in Model Gallery facilitates pre-deployment testing, including accuracy verification. MLOps engineers validate models against organizational security, privacy, and technical requirements, utilizing deep learning VMs with Triton Inference Server as dedicated testing environments for inference requests and readiness verification before promotion to production. Model versions in Harbor are assigned unique digests and immutable OCI artifacts, ensuring full traceability of validated and deployed models.

Agent Builder in Private AI Services extends these capabilities by integrating with Data Indexing and Retrieval, enabling RAG-based agents to reference curated, access-controlled organizational knowledge bases, ensuring their generative output is informed and grounded.

While the platform delivers the foundational building blocks and technical mechanisms for implementing fact-checking, the organization deploying these technologies is responsible for defining the specific fact-checking methodologies, establishing acceptance criteria, and integrating these techniques into their operational workflows. This includes determining the appropriate level of verification, the specific data sources for grounding, and the processes for human oversight and intervention.

VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA provides infrastructure-level mechanisms that support output filtering for AI-generated content. Agent Builder in Private AI Services includes an "Add Instructions" setting, which utilizes system messages to define content policies, output restrictions, and behavioral guardrails for large language models. Through this capability, administrators can instruct models to refuse generation of inappropriate, harmful, false, illegal, or violent content, establishing a persistent filtering layer for all model outputs within the deployed application.

The validate-then-publish workflow for ML models further provides a pre-deployment gate where output quality and safety are evaluated. MLOps engineers validate models against organizational security, privacy, and technical requirements, including examination of inference requests for malicious behavior and functional testing within deep learning VMs with Triton Inference Server, ensuring content filtering behaves as expected before promotion to production.

Additionally, the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture available through Private AI Services contributes to preventing false content by grounding model responses in external, authoritative knowledge bases, thereby reducing the likelihood of fabricated or inaccurate information.

While the platform offers these essential building blocks—system message configuration, robust model validation workflows, RAG grounding, and curated model deployment—it does not include a built-in content classification or filtering API that automatically screens model outputs against predefined content categories. The specific content policies, acceptance thresholds, and filtering logic must be defined and implemented by the deploying organization to align with their unique regulatory and business requirements.
