Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure AI models are designed with resilience capabilities that are sufficient to withstand reasonable threats.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides robust infrastructure-level mechanisms that directly support the design and maintenance of resilient AI models.

The platform enforces a validate-then-publish workflow, requiring models to undergo rigorous testing within a sandboxed deep learning VM before promotion. This validation includes verifying model file integrity via hash codes, executing inference tests, and evaluating performance and safety against business use cases, with explicit guidance for examining inference requests for malicious behavior.

Model storage leverages OCI-compliant Harbor registries, where each revision is an immutable OCI artifact identified by a content digest. This immutability prevents silent alteration post-publication and enables reliable rollback to previous, verified versions if a deployed model is compromised.

Furthermore, GPU health and workload behavior are continuously monitored through NVIDIA DCGM Exporter, Prometheus, and Grafana, providing real-time visibility into potential anomalies that could indicate adversarial interference or infrastructure degradation affecting model outputs.

At the foundational infrastructure layer, PAIF workloads inherit the comprehensive resilience capabilities of VMware Cloud Foundation.

vSphere High Availability automatically restarts failed virtual machines on healthy hosts, ensuring continuous operation. Distributed Resource Scheduler dynamically balances compute load and can evacuate workloads from underperforming nodes, optimizing resource utilization and preventing contention. vMotion facilitates live migration of running virtual machines without downtime, and VM snapshots offer point-in-time recovery options.

These platform features collectively protect AI inference workloads and training jobs from hardware failures and resource contention, thereby safeguarding model availability and output quality.

While the platform provides the essential technical mechanisms and tools for resilience, the customer is responsible for defining the specific resilience requirements for their AI models, including threat modeling, establishing acceptable performance and safety thresholds, and implementing the necessary MLOps policies and procedures. This includes actively utilizing the provided validation workflows, monitoring capabilities, and rollback features, as well as incorporating specific adversarial robustness techniques into the AI model's design and training processes to ensure sufficiency against identified reasonable threats.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prevent "model pollution" due to accidental and/or malicious inputs by an AI agent that can negatively alter the AI model.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA (PAIF) provides robust mechanisms to prevent model pollution.

Model integrity is enforced through cryptographic hash codes, where each revision pushed to the model gallery receives a unique digest, and model files must be validated against these hashes prior to deployment. Models are stored as immutable OCI artifacts with immutable manifests in Harbor, ensuring that once a model revision is published, its contents cannot be silently altered. This immutability prevents both accidental overwrites and undetected tampering.

A validate-then-publish workflow governs the model lifecycle, requiring MLOps engineers to validate ML models against security, privacy, and technical requirements before uploading them to the model gallery. Models downloaded from public registries are validated and fine-tuned in deep learning VMs before storage in the controlled gallery environment, and models are scanned for serialization attacks before deployment.

Access controls restrict who can modify models in the gallery, with Harbor project access capabilities limiting read-write permissions to authorized users and OIDC group-based authorization controlling access to Private AI Services.

VMware vDefend further contributes by providing network-level controls that restrict access to AI model training and inference infrastructure running on VMware Cloud Foundation.

The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level, isolating AI training environments, model registries, and inference endpoints into dedicated security segments. Granular firewall rules restrict communication pathways, limiting how accidental or malicious inputs can reach the model. Environment-based segmentation through the Security Services Platform groups AI-related workloads into logical environments, enforcing protection rules that can drop or reject unauthorized traffic between environments.

vDefend IDS/IPS provides signature-based and behavioral detection of malicious traffic patterns, inspecting east-west traffic for exploit attempts and suspicious payloads before they reach model endpoints. Network Traffic Analysis (NTA) for Suspicious Traffic uses machine learning and rule-based analytics to identify anomalous communication patterns targeting AI infrastructure.

Additionally, vDefend Malware Prevention and Advanced Threat Prevention (ATP) inspect files and payloads exchanged between workloads, detecting and blocking poisoned datasets or malicious model updates delivered through the network.

While the platform provides the necessary technical mechanisms, the customer is responsible for defining the specific security, privacy, and technical requirements for model validation, configuring and enforcing access control policies, and establishing the operational processes for MLOps engineers to execute the validate-then-publish workflow. The customer must also define the specific microsegmentation policies and IDS/IPS rules to align with their organizational security posture.

Description: Mechanisms exist to detect and prevent the propagation of false data (e.g., hallucinations) within the AI model or between AI agents.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA provides architectural capabilities designed to reduce the risk of hallucination propagation within deployed AI workloads. The platform's Agent Builder supports Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture, which grounds model outputs in factual enterprise data stored in a vector database, thereby narrowing the LLM's context and reducing reliance on parametric memory, a primary source of hallucinated content. Agent Builder also enables operators to apply system-level instructions through an "Add Instructions" setting, allowing for prompt engineering techniques to constrain model behavior, such as requiring responses only from provided context or flagging uncertainty. Furthermore, the validate-then-publish workflow serves as a pre-deployment gate, requiring MLOps engineers to validate model output quality and accuracy against organizational requirements before models are made available for production inference, effectively catching models prone to hallucination. The platform deploys individual agents and does not support multi-agent orchestration or agent-to-agent communication, rendering the "between AI agents" aspect of this control not applicable as cascading hallucination between agents is not a supported failure mode.

While PAIF offers robust mechanisms for prevention and pre-deployment detection of hallucinations, it does not include a dedicated runtime hallucination detection or filtering engine. The effectiveness of prompt engineering and the validate-then-publish workflow relies on the customer's definition of system-level instructions, validation criteria, and the execution of these processes by MLOps engineers. Organizations must establish and enforce policies and procedures for model validation and prompt engineering to fully address the control's intent.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to monitor and prevent resource overload or Denial of Service (DoS) conditions through:
 (1) Enforcement of quotas;
 (2) Workload controls; and 
 (3) Auto-suspension of runaway AI agent processes.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA contributes to resilience against resource exhaustion and Denial of Service (DoS) conditions through comprehensive resource quotas and workload controls enforced at multiple infrastructure layers. VCF Automation region quotas mandate finite CPU, memory, and GPU limits for each zone within a supervisor, explicitly prohibiting unlimited resource assignments. Namespace creation involves validation of reservation and limit values against available resources, with vCenter provisioning dedicated resource pools that apply these limits to prevent any single namespace from exceeding its allocation. GPU-accelerated AI/ML workloads receive additional controls, requiring CPU and memory reservations and limits to be set to non-zero values, with full memory reservations for all AI/ML workloads utilizing GPU accelerators. GPU time-slicing can be configured to define workload sharing, ensuring controlled resource contention. Infrastructure policies further define resource quality and quantity, complemented by namespace-level storage quotas. At the VMware Cloud Foundation platform level, vSphere DRS manages workload placement and rebalancing, while vSphere HA provides automatic restart capabilities for failed workloads.

PAIF does not inherently provide auto-suspension mechanisms for runaway AI agent processes. While Agent Builder deploys individual agents, it does not include runtime monitoring for runaway behavior or automatic process termination. Organizations requiring auto-suspension capabilities must implement these through external means, such as Kubernetes-level liveness and readiness probes, integration with external monitoring tools, or defined operational procedures.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure AI agents are designed, developed and deployed to securely operate under human oversight.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides robust mechanisms to ensure AI agents are designed, developed, and deployed under explicit human oversight. The Agent Builder within Private AI Services establishes a structured, human-controlled workflow for agent creation. Agents are not self-provisioned; their deployment requires an authorized user to make explicit decisions at each stage, including selecting model endpoints, defining agent behavior through system-level instructions, and attaching knowledge bases. This ensures agents operate within human-authored behavioral frameworks and predefined operational boundaries.

Human oversight is further enforced by a comprehensive role-based access control model spanning the entire PAIF stack. This layered approval chain mandates multiple human decisions across infrastructure, model management, and agent configuration before any agent can be deployed. For instance, organization administrators control AI catalog item requests, VI administrators manage underlying services, and MLOps engineers validate and upload models, ensuring no agent deployment occurs without explicit human intervention and approval.

The architecture inherently limits agent autonomy by restricting agents to single completion model endpoints and knowledge bases, and by not supporting multi-agent orchestration, autonomous agent-to-agent communication, or self-modifying capabilities, thereby maintaining behavior within human-defined parameters.

While VMware Private AI Foundation provides the foundational technical controls and mechanisms for human oversight throughout the AI agent lifecycle, the customer is responsible for establishing and enforcing the organizational policies, processes, and human accountability necessary to fully leverage these capabilities and ensure continuous secure operation under human governance.

VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides comprehensive infrastructure hardening and isolation capabilities for AI workloads. Resource allocation is managed through namespace classes, which define precise CPU, memory, and GPU reservations and limits, validated against available resources during provisioning. Role-based access control is enforced through multiple layers, including OIDC group authorization, distinct VCF Automation roles (VI administrator, organization administrator, project administrator, DevOps engineer, data scientist, MLOps engineer), and Kubernetes privileged pod security policies. Network segmentation is achieved via NSX Edge clusters, isolated workload domains with Tier-0 gateways, and dedicated pod/service CIDR blocks for GPU-accelerated VKS clusters, ensuring AI workload traffic isolation. Workload isolation is further enforced through namespace scoping, GPU reservations, and Harbor project access controls, restricting access to sensitive data and model artifacts.

VMware vDefend directly enhances network segmentation and workload isolation through its Distributed Firewall and Security Services Platform. The Distributed Firewall enables environment isolation by segmenting development, staging, and production environments and configuring block policies to prevent unauthorized traffic. Security Intelligence Segmentation Monitoring supports infrastructure hardening by enabling lockdown rules and recommending segmentation policies for critical infrastructure services. The underlying VCF platform also contributes to overall infrastructure hardening.

While the platform provides the necessary technical mechanisms, customers are responsible for defining specific resource allocation policies, configuring and assigning roles for privilege management, establishing detailed network segmentation rules, and implementing workload isolation policies tailored to their operational requirements. Furthermore, the application of specific host-level hardening benchmarks, such as CIS or STIG profiles, and the operationalization of intrusion detection at the infrastructure layer, remain customer responsibilities.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to implement limitations for AI agents according to:
 (1) Least privileges, where the AI agent operates with the minimal permissions necessary to perform designated tasks; and
 (2) Least functionality, where the AI agent is restricted to communicate with the minimal Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) and networks necessary to perform designated tasks.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA implements AI agent limitations aligning with least privilege and least functionality principles through its structured agent deployment model, role-based access controls, namespace scoping, and constrained agent architecture.

For least privilege, PAIF enforces a layered access control model that restricts each user and role to the minimum permissions needed for their designated function, utilizing OIDC groups, distinct VCF Automation roles (VI administrator, organization administrator, project administrator, DevOps engineer, data scientist, and MLOps engineer), and namespace scoping to control access to resources. Namespace class configurations specify available storage and VM classes, and API calls for importing namespaces or adding Private AI Services are restricted to specific administrative roles, ensuring robust role separation.

For least functionality, PAIF's Agent Builder constrains each AI agent to communicate with only the specific services required for its task, typically a single completion model endpoint and an optional knowledge base, preventing communication with arbitrary external services. At the infrastructure level, GPU-accelerated VKS clusters configure dedicated pod and service CIDR blocks for network segmentation, and Kubernetes namespaces enforce pod security policies. VMware vDefend further contributes to AI agent least-functionality requirements by enabling network-level restrictions. Security Intelligence generates policy recommendations for groups of VMs to define and enforce the minimal set of required network communications, which the Distributed Firewall then enforces through microsegmentation policies to prevent AI agents from communicating with unauthorized systems or networks.

While these controls establish a secure environment for AI agent operation, the platform does not provide application-layer agent sandboxing, fine-grained tool-call permission policies, or runtime monitoring of which APIs an agent invokes during execution.

The least privilege and least functionality constraints primarily operate at the infrastructure, namespace, and agent configuration layers rather than within the agent's runtime behavior. Organizations must define and implement specific policies for AI agent runtime behavior, including restricting file system, API, and runtime permissions for AI agents through separate identity, access management, and platform hardening mechanisms to fully meet the least privilege requirements at the agent's operational level.

VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides comprehensive authentication and authorization mechanisms for API invocations by AI agents deployed via Agent Builder. The platform mandates Bearer token authentication for API requests, with tokens secured through the VCF Automation OAuth token endpoint. JWTAuthenticator is configured to authenticate users interacting with deployed agents and model endpoints using issuer, audience, username, and groups claims. Private AI Services enforces authorization through OIDC groups specified in the authorizedGroups configuration, while Data Indexing and Retrieval utilizes OIDC authentication with configurable issuer URL, client ID, and authorized groups to control access to knowledge bases consumed by agents. Agent Builder deploys agents that interact with model endpoints, which are Kubernetes resources within VCF Automation, inheriting namespace-scoped access controls and project-level permissions. Harbor project access capabilities further restrict access to the model artifacts used by agents. VMware vDefend enhances these controls at the network access layer, enabling microsegmentation to enforce which systems can reach AI agent APIs.

However, Private AI Foundation does not include built-in schema validation for tool inputs, rate limiting on agent API invocations, or output validation and guardrails for agent-generated responses. These critical application-layer functions must be implemented by the customer through additional tooling, API gateway infrastructure layered in front of PAIF endpoints, or other application frameworks and identity management systems.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to validate, secure and restrict AI agent orchestration protocols to prevent:
 (1) Unauthorized tool chaining;
 (2) Context manipulation; and/or
 (3) Protocol-based escalation.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA implements robust architectural and design-time safeguards to secure and restrict AI agent orchestration protocols. The platform's constrained single-agent architecture inherently limits orchestration-level risks by preventing multi-agent orchestration, agent-to-agent communication, and agent-to-agent invocation or chaining. This design eliminates a significant class of orchestration-level attacks, including unauthorized tool chaining between agents or protocol-based escalation across agent boundaries. Each agent is bound to a single completion model endpoint and, optionally, a single knowledge base, with its fixed set of backend services determined at creation time. Agents cannot dynamically discover or invoke additional tools, endpoints, or services beyond what was explicitly configured by the human operator, thereby preventing runtime tool chaining.

Context manipulation is addressed through Agent Builder's "Add Instructions" setting, which sends operator-defined instructions as system messages or prefixes to user queries, establishing immutable behavioral boundaries at agent creation. Knowledge base attachments further narrow the context window by scoping retrieved data to specific, access-controlled data sources. OIDC groups restrict user interaction with agents, and namespace-scoped permissions prevent unauthorized users from reconfiguring agents or their backing services. Protocol-based escalation is mitigated by a layered role separation across VI administrator, organization administrator, project administrator, DevOps engineer, data scientist, and MLOps engineer, ensuring distinct credentials and privilege levels for agent creation, model deployment, and infrastructure management. Bearer token authentication, validated by JWTAuthenticator, is required for all API requests, ensuring that compromising agent-level access does not grant access to model management, infrastructure administration, or namespace configuration.

While PAIF provides significant controls, it does not include runtime monitoring of agent protocol interactions, dynamic tool authorization policies, or prompt injection detection at the orchestration layer. Organizations deploying agents with complex tool integrations beyond the built-in knowledge base and model endpoint pattern must implement additional protocol validation and monitoring at the application layer to achieve comprehensive coverage.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to validate, sanitize and monitor all data inputs and retrieval pipelines for AI agents to:
 (1) Ensure data provenance; and 
 (2) Prevent unauthorized access risks (e.g., injection, manipulation or exfiltration).
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA establishes robust mechanisms for data provenance and input integrity within AI agent retrieval and model pipelines.

The Data Indexing and Retrieval service maintains a clear chain from source data to agent context by explicitly defining data source objects that reference original locations and store necessary credentials for retrieval. Supported data source types, including S3-compatible object storage, SharePoint, and Confluence, enforce specific authentication requirements, such as S3 access keys with list/read permissions or SharePoint username/password authentication. Document-level indexing status, tracking new, deleted, modified, skipped, and failed documents, provides an auditable trail of data entering the retrieval pipeline.

Input validation in the retrieval pipeline ensures only supported file formats (PDF, HTML, PowerPoint, Markdown, DOCX, TXT, CSV) are processed, with unsupported types logged as skipped. A sequential workflow mandates that knowledge bases are created and indexed before linking to an agent, preventing unprocessed data from reaching the agent and narrowing the context window to access-controlled data sources.

Unauthorized access to the retrieval pipeline is prevented through OIDC authentication with configurable issuer URLs, client IDs, and authorized groups, along with bearer token authentication for all Private AI Services REST API requests. Trusted connections to content management systems and Harbor registries require CA certificates to prevent man-in-the-middle interception.

On the model pipeline side, PAIF enforces input integrity via a validate-then-publish workflow, requiring MLOps engineers to validate models against organizational requirements, scan for serialization attacks, and test in sandbox environments. Each model revision receives a unique digest, and file integrity is validated through hash code checks.

VMware vDefend contributes to the monitoring and prevention of unauthorized access and potential exfiltration for AI agent data pipelines at the network layer.

Security Intelligence analyzes flow data to establish exception policies for authorized communications between environment pairs, thereby enforcing legitimate data pipeline paths and detecting anomalous data flows. Filtering logic, such as retaining only flows with non-zero application IDs, maintains the integrity of flow data used for security analysis. These network-layer controls are instrumental in detecting and containing exfiltration attempts.

While PAIF and vDefend provide foundational controls, the organization is responsible for implementing additional measures to fully address runtime prompt injection detection, real-time input sanitization filters at the agent query layer, and automated anomaly detection on retrieval pipeline data flows.

Preventing injection or manipulation of AI training data and retrieval pipelines beyond the platform's inherent validation requires application-layer controls or supplementary tooling.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to prevent privilege escalation or unauthorized delegation by AI agents by:
 (1) Monitoring and enforcing dynamic roles; and
 (2) Establishing cross-agent delegation boundaries and privileged actions.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to restrict agent access to sensitive/regulated data so that AI agents cannot ingest, generate or act on unauthorized data.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA (PAIF) provides robust mechanisms to restrict AI agent access to sensitive and regulated data. Agents created via Agent Builder integrate with Data Indexing & Retrieval, accessing business data through knowledge bases explicitly linked to a maximum of ten specific data sources. This design ensures agents only retrieve content from deliberately attached and authorized data sources, preventing open-ended access to all organizational data. Data source access is further secured through credential-based authentication, where specific permissions (e.g., Amazon S3 access keys, SharePoint credentials) scope the agent's reach to only the data authorized by those credentials. Access to Private AI Services itself is controlled via OIDC authentication with configurable issuer URL, client ID, and authorized groups, ensuring only authorized personnel can configure data sources, create knowledge bases, and deploy agents. Harbor project access capabilities provide an additional layer of restriction for sensitive data used in ML model training and tuning, scoping model and training data visibility to authorized project members. At the infrastructure level, VCF Automation creates namespace-specific contexts, confining AI workloads and their associated resources to defined organizational boundaries and preventing cross-project data access.

VMware vDefend further contributes to restricting AI agent access through comprehensive network-level controls. The Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level, allowing granular rules to confine AI agent workloads to communicating solely with authorized data sources. This prevents agents from reaching unauthorized sensitive, regulated, or classified data systems at the network layer. Layer 7 application identification and URL filtering provide additional control over protocols and destinations, preventing agents from accessing unauthorized external data sources or APIs. Security Intelligence traffic flow analysis identifies unauthorized access attempts and offers policy recommendations for least-privilege network access. Gateway Firewall rules control north-south traffic, restricting external data source access, while TLS inspection verifies authorized endpoints and prevents data exfiltration.

While these platforms provide the necessary technical controls, the customer is responsible for defining specific data classification policies, configuring the precise data sources and access credentials within Private AI Foundation, and establishing appropriate OIDC groups and administrative permissions. Furthermore, customers must define and implement granular microsegmentation policies, Layer 7 rules, URL filters, and Gateway Firewall rules within vDefend to align with their specific data access requirements. The broader aspects of restricting what an AI agent *generates* or *acts on* at the application level, such as implementing prompt-level guardrails or enforcing data classification within the agent's logic, remain a customer responsibility, requiring careful agent design and operational oversight.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prevent AI agents from extracting sensitive/regulated data from volatile memory that can be exploited at a later point.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, through its vDefend capabilities, provides robust network-level mechanisms to prevent the unauthorized exfiltration of sensitive or regulated data by AI agents. The Distributed Firewall enforces granular microsegmentation policies, restricting AI agent workloads to communicate only with authorized network destinations. This significantly limits the ability of a compromised AI agent to transfer data scraped from memory to external or unauthorized internal systems. Security Intelligence continuously monitors east-west traffic flows within the environment, detecting anomalous communication patterns that could indicate unauthorized data staging or exfiltration attempts.

The direct prevention of AI agents extracting sensitive data from volatile memory is a host-level security function. This requires the implementation of host-based security controls, endpoint protection platforms, and runtime application security mechanisms that operate within the guest operating system or hypervisor to protect process memory. These specific capabilities fall outside the scope of VMware Cloud Foundation's network security offerings.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to ensure user identification and authentication methods are capable of preventing AI agents from 
 (1) Spoofing;
 (2) Mimicry; and/or
 (3) Impersonation attacks.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prevent AI agent logic from being subverted or manipulated.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides robust technical mechanisms to prevent AI agent logic subversion or manipulation.

The Agent Builder serves as the sole interface for agent deployment, enforcing a controlled UI workflow that limits the attack surface to authorized users. Agents are configured to specific model endpoints and knowledge bases, with administrators defining behavioral boundaries through system messages.

Access to Private AI Services is governed by OIDC-based authentication and authorized groups, restricting agent creation, modification, and deployment. A namespace-scoped deployment model with role separation further limits component modification.

Model integrity is ensured through a validate-then-publish workflow, where MLOps engineers validate models against organizational requirements before uploading them to an access-controlled Harbor gallery. Each model revision receives a unique digest, and integrity is validated via hash code checks before deployment, preventing the substitution of compromised models.

Knowledge bases, which provide retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) context, are similarly access-controlled, requiring credentials for data retrieval and restricting creation to authorized users, ensuring a bounded and auditable data pipeline.

VMware vDefend further strengthens the protection of AI agent logic by providing comprehensive network-layer security.

The Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation at the vNIC level, isolating AI agent workloads and restricting communication to authorized systems, thereby limiting lateral movement. The Gateway Firewall controls north-south traffic to and from AI agent services.

vDefend IDS/IPS inspects both east-west and north-south traffic for known attack signatures and behavioral anomalies, alerting administrators to exploit attempts or injection attacks targeting AI agent endpoints. URL filtering and TLS inspection provide visibility into encrypted communications, aiding in the detection of command-and-control channels or data exfiltration. Malware Prevention scans incoming files, intercepting malicious payloads before they can reach AI agent workloads.

Security Intelligence provides traffic flow analysis and visualization, enabling the establishment of baseline communication patterns and the detection of deviations indicative of unauthorized access or manipulation attempts.

While the platform provides the foundational technical mechanisms, customers are responsible for defining and implementing specific security policies, configuring access controls, and establishing operational processes.

This includes specifying authorized identity groups, defining granular firewall rules, configuring agent instructions, and ensuring MLOps engineers adhere to validation procedures for models and knowledge bases.

The effectiveness of these mechanisms relies on diligent customer configuration and ongoing management of access, policies, and operational workflows.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize a "sandbox" capability to restrict AI agents from unrestricted access to both local and remote resources.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA, leveraging VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend, provides robust mechanisms for sandboxing AI agents, restricting their access to both local and remote resources.

PAIF deploys AI agents within Kubernetes namespaces on GPU-accelerated VKS clusters, which operate within vSphere namespaces scoped to specific workload domains. The Supervisor enforces namespace-level resource boundaries, preventing agents from accessing resources in other namespaces or workload domains. Kubernetes pod security admission is enforced on agent workload namespaces, controlling the security profile of agent containers.

Access to AI catalog items, agent deployments, and model artifacts is governed by OIDC group-based authorization, VCF Automation project-level permissions, and Harbor project access controls.

At the infrastructure layer, VCF provides workload domain isolation with dedicated vCenter, ESX hosts, and networking, while VKS clusters establish network boundaries through defined pod and service CIDR blocks. VM classes further define compute and GPU resource constraints for agents.

VMware vDefend complements these capabilities by providing network-level isolation and access restriction.

The Distributed Firewall operates at the vNIC level, applying microsegmentation policies to AI agent virtual machines. Administrators can define granular Layer 2-7 firewall rules to confine agent communication to designated data stores and API endpoints, enforcing a zero-trust posture.

Security Services Platform extends this with environment-pair policies to block traffic between sensitive environments, and Security Intelligence provides visibility into agent traffic flows.

For remote access, Gateway Firewall and URL filtering allow administrators to define allowlists for external destinations, with TLS inspection verifying outbound connections and IDS/IPS detecting exploitation attempts.

These combined capabilities create a comprehensive infrastructure and network-level sandbox that constrains AI agent access.

While the platform provides extensive infrastructure, network, and access control mechanisms for sandboxing, customers are responsible for defining and implementing the specific policies, configurations, and rules for Kubernetes namespaces, pod security, network microsegmentation, and access controls to effectively utilize these capabilities.

It is important to note that VMware vDefend does not provide application-level sandboxing of AI agent execution, such as restricting file system access, limiting API calls, or constraining code execution within a runtime environment.

Description: Mechanisms exist to detect and mitigate prompt injection / input attacks that seek to manipulate AI agent instructions, bypass controls or result in unauthorized actions.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to allow authorized users or operators to immediately halt or disable AI agent activity in case of unexpected behavior or harm.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides robust, multi-layered mechanisms for authorized operators to immediately halt or disable AI agent activity. Agents deployed via Private AI Services are Kubernetes-managed resources, allowing authorized users to undeploy or delete them directly through the interface or REST API, instantly terminating their availability. Stopping the underlying model endpoint in Model Runtime also serves as an effective kill switch, as agents lose their inference backend. At the Kubernetes layer, operators with `kubectl` access can delete agent pods, scale deployments to zero, or apply resource quotas, with actions taking effect within seconds. The namespace-scoped architecture ensures targeted intervention.

VCF Automation enforces role-based access control across these actions, enabling organization, VI, and project administrators to manage resources at their respective scopes, including revoking catalog item permissions or intervening at the Supervisor level. The VCF Automation REST API further supports programmatic kill-switch workflows.

The underlying VCF platform offers additional containment, allowing vSphere administrators to power off or suspend virtual machines hosting Kubernetes worker nodes, immediately halting all containerized workloads, including agents.

VMware vDefend complements these capabilities by providing critical network-level mechanisms that contribute to the immediate halting or disabling of AI agent activity.

The vDefend Distributed Firewall operates at the vNIC level, enabling administrators to instantly block all network traffic to and from a specific virtual machine or container running an AI agent. Rules are enforced in the hypervisor kernel, taking effect immediately and preventing circumvention. Pre-staged "emergency isolation" policies can rapidly cut off misbehaving AI agents from the network. Microsegmentation policies allow for fine-grained security groups, enabling administrators to apply deny-all rules to relevant groups across the environment.

The vDefend Gateway Firewall provides a control point at network boundaries for agents communicating with external services. Security Intelligence continuously monitors traffic for anomalies, providing visibility and policy recommendations, while vDefend IDS/IPS offers automated real-time blocking of malicious or anomalous network patterns.

These network controls provide a reliable infrastructure-level mechanism to sever an AI agent's connectivity, forming a necessary component of a defense-in-depth strategy.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for immediate halting and disabling, the customer is responsible for establishing the organizational policies, processes, and procedures for detecting unexpected behavior or harm, defining the specific conditions under which these mechanisms are to be invoked, and formally assigning human accountability and authorization roles for operators to execute these actions.

Description: Mechanisms exist to regularly conduct adversarial testing that simulates attacks against AI agents to identify and mitigate vulnerabilities.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA provides the essential infrastructure and workflow mechanisms that enable adversarial testing of AI models and agents. The platform's validate-then-publish workflow incorporates a mandatory validation step where MLOps engineers must test models against organizational security, privacy, and technical requirements before deployment to the model gallery. This includes scanning for serialization attacks and malware, with platform documentation explicitly requiring examination of inference requests for malicious behavior and functional testing as part of the pre-deployment validation process.

Deep Learning VMs offer isolated, GPU-accelerated environments, preconfigured with ML frameworks and toolkits, and the Triton Inference Server, providing the necessary tooling for data scientists and MLOps engineers to simulate adversarial inputs, test model robustness, and evaluate agent behavior under attack conditions. For agents deployed via Agent Builder, which are exposed through REST APIs, standard API testing tools and techniques can be employed for adversarial testing, further supported by the platform's robust RBAC controls, including OIDC groups, namespace-scoped permissions, and project-level Harbor access, to establish dedicated, secure testing environments for red team activities.

The organization retains responsibility for defining the specific scheduling, methodology, and execution of its adversarial and red team testing programs.

Description: Mechanisms exist to control, restrict and log AI agent self-modification.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA establishes a robust architecture that fundamentally prevents AI agent self-modification. Agents deployed through Private AI Services are declaratively configured resources, not self-modifying programs. They are created with specific model endpoint bindings, knowledge bases, and system instructions, none of which can be altered by the agent at runtime.

All modifications to agent configurations require explicit human action through the Private AI Services console or REST API. Access to these modification capabilities is strictly governed by OIDC-based authentication, requiring users to belong to authorized identity groups. Role separation further restricts which users can modify specific components of the agent pipeline, and namespace-scoped deployment boundaries contain agents within their designated organizational scope.

The underlying models invoked by agents are also protected through a validate-then-publish workflow, where MLOps engineers validate models before they are stored as immutable OCI artifacts in Harbor, ensuring agents cannot trigger changes to the models they utilize.

Changes to agent workloads, which run as Kubernetes-managed resources within VKS clusters, are comprehensively logged through the Kubernetes API audit log, capturing details of who made changes and what was modified. VCF Automation provides an additional layer of visibility by tracking deployment and lifecycle events for agent-related resources within its own logging infrastructure.

While the platform provides the foundational technical mechanisms to prevent, control, restrict, and log modifications, customers are responsible for defining and managing their OIDC-based identity groups, establishing appropriate role-based access control policies, implementing MLOps validation processes for models, and ensuring regular review and action upon the generated audit and lifecycle logs.

Description: Mechanisms exist to restrict purging of any persistent memory or long-term data used by AI agents.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation (PAIF) with NVIDIA provides robust mechanisms to restrict the purging of persistent memory and long-term data utilized by AI agents. This is achieved through a multi-layered approach encompassing role-based access controls (RBAC), namespace-scoped resource boundaries, and immutable artifact storage. For AI agents deployed via Agent Builder in Private AI Services, knowledge bases, which contain indexed embeddings in the vector database, are protected by OIDC-based authentication. Deletion or modification of these knowledge bases requires specific access to the Private AI Services console or API, granted only to users within authorized identity groups. Similarly, ML models stored in Harbor model galleries benefit from immutable artifact storage, where each revision creates a new, unique artifact rather than overwriting existing data. Deleting model artifacts or entire projects necessitates Harbor administrator privileges, distinct from standard PAIF operational roles.

At the infrastructure layer, PAIF resources are deployed within Kubernetes namespace-scoped boundaries. Namespace deletion, which would purge all contained agent data, knowledge bases, and configuration, is a highly privileged operation requiring either the organization administrator role in VCF Automation or provider administrator access. The platform enforces a specific teardown sequence, including the prior deletion of identity providers and region quotas, preventing accidental or unauthorized bulk data removal. Furthermore, role separation across the platform ensures that routine agent users or operators, such as data scientists, do not possess the elevated administrative privileges required to purge critical agent data like knowledge bases or model galleries.

While the platform provides these foundational technical controls, the customer is responsible for defining and assigning appropriate identity groups, configuring Harbor project access controls, and establishing organizational roles to effectively leverage these mechanisms and enforce the desired level of restriction on data purging.

Description: Mechanisms exist to restrict agentic delegation, chaining and multi-agent communication to prevent unauthorized task escalation or emergent risks.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to continuously monitor for behavioral drift or deviation from established AI agent baselines.
Response:
VMware vDefend provides robust mechanisms for continuously monitoring AI agent behavioral drift at the network level. Security Intelligence continuously monitors network traffic flows between environments, detecting segmentation drift and alerting administrators to new, unauthorized, or unexpected communications. This capability ensures that if an AI agent workload running on VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) begins communicating with unexpected services, accesses data stores outside its normal pattern, or generates anomalous traffic volumes, these deviations are immediately flagged. Segmentation drift monitoring tracks changes against established policy baselines, presenting change-detected indicators in the Security Services Platform UI for review when observed traffic departs from the approved segmentation model.

Further enhancing this monitoring, Network Traffic Analysis (NTA) for Suspicious Traffic leverages machine learning-driven behavior modeling and rule-based analytics to build time-series profiles of host behavior and identify anomalies. When an AI agent's network activity deviates from its historical baseline, NTA detects and categorizes the suspicious traffic event, with detection results available across configurable time windows from one hour to one month, supporting both immediate alerting and long-term trend analysis crucial for identifying gradual behavioral drift.

Additionally, vDefend IDS/IPS includes behavior-based detection that identifies anomalous events by comparing observed activity against a baseline of normal traffic. This provides an additional layer of drift detection at the network level, flagging deviations even when no specific known-threat signature matches, should an AI agent's behavior change in ways that alter its network signature.

While these capabilities provide a critical network security dimension for identifying AI agent behavioral drift, VMware Cloud Foundation does not directly monitor the logical or functional behavior of AI agents. Detecting changes in network communication patterns serves as a meaningful, though partial, signal for identifying overall behavioral drift. Organizations must complement vDefend's network-level monitoring with application-level AI observability tools that track model outputs, decision quality, and functional metrics to achieve comprehensive coverage of AI agent behavioral drift.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure that all AI agent-initiated actions are properly mapped to authenticated user or system identities, with enforced authorization checks.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA establishes a robust, layered authentication and authorization architecture to ensure AI agent-initiated actions are mapped to authenticated identities.

The platform leverages an OIDC-based authentication framework, where agents deployed through Agent Builder in Private AI Services utilize a JWTAuthenticator configured with essential details such as issuer URL, audience, username, and groups claims for user authentication. All API interactions with deployed agents are routed through the Private AI Services REST API, which mandates Bearer token authentication in the Authorization header, thereby linking each agent interaction directly to an authenticated user session.

Authorization is enforced through multiple integrated controls, including OIDC groups specified in the authorizedGroups configuration to restrict user interaction with the platform and its agents. Organization administrators are required to explicitly grant permissions for requesting AI catalog items before users can deploy AI workloads, and VI administrators maintain control over the availability of Supervisor Services within each namespace. This hierarchical permission structure ensures that agents deployed within a given namespace inherit the access controls of that namespace, effectively bounding agent-initiated actions by the permissions of the authenticated identity that invoked them.

The JWTAuthenticator configuration further mandates TLS certificate authority data for trusted communication with the OIDC issuer, and the platform validates registry credentials for Harbor registries used for model storage, completing a comprehensive chain of authenticated identity from the OIDC provider through JWT validation to namespace-level authorization, making all actions performed by or through agents fully traceable to the initiating authenticated user or system identity.

While the platform provides the foundational mechanisms for authentication, authorization, and traceability, the customer is responsible for the configuration and management of the external OIDC provider, defining and maintaining the authorized OIDC groups, establishing specific permissions for AI catalog items, and managing the namespace-scoped access controls that govern agent deployments and their operational boundaries.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to provide comprehensive audit trails of AI agent actions including:
 (1) Rationales; and 
 (2) User/trigger mappings.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to:
 (1) Provide human-understandable explanations for significant AI agent actions or decisions; and 
 (2) Enable users to contest outcomes.

Mechanisms exist to validate AI agent-generated outputs through the use of:
 (1) Content scanning; and
 (2) Output vetting through human approvals, where appropriate.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure AI agent actions offer non-repudiation and enable forensic examination to determine accountability.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to generate event logs for Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) actions to ensure transparency and auditability.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, through its Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA and vDefend components, provides extensive logging and monitoring infrastructure that supports the auditability of AI and Autonomous Technologies (AAT) environments. Private AI Foundation deploys DCGM Exporters within Deep Learning VMs to expose GPU metrics, which are collected by Prometheus and visualized in Grafana, providing a continuous record of GPU utilization, memory consumption, and compute activity. Deep Learning VM deployment activities are logged to `/var/log/dl.log`, establishing an audit trail for provisioning events. At the platform level, VCF Operations monitors GPU consumption across workload domains, and the vSphere Client offers advanced GPU performance monitoring.

VMware vDefend generates comprehensive event logs across its security services, capturing network-level activity for all workloads, including AI and AAT. The vDefend Distributed Firewall logs security events at the vNIC level, recording firewall rule evaluations and actions on traffic to and from AI workloads, providing visibility into communication patterns. Distributed IDS/IPS generates detailed event logs for detected threats, and the Malware Prevention Service logs file analysis performed on content processed by AI workloads. Gateway Firewall logs north-south traffic, and logging levels across vDefend components are configurable, with structured log messages from the host agent forwardable to centralized log management platforms.

While the platform provides robust operational, resource, network, and security logging for the environment hosting AI and AAT, it does not inherently generate event logs for the specific actions, decisions, or outputs of the AI or autonomous technology itself. Private AI Foundation does not provide built-in request/response logging for inference calls, and application-level logging of agent decisions and outputs is dependent on the consuming application's configuration. Similarly, vDefend's logging capabilities do not extend to AI-specific actions such as model inference decisions, training iterations, or autonomous decision outputs. Therefore, the implementation of logging mechanisms for the direct actions and internal decisions of AI and AAT systems remains a customer responsibility, requiring configuration within the AI applications or models themselves.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to control AI agent sessions by:
 (1) Embedding session IDs into the requests to the AI model;
 (2) Implementing capabilities to correlate sessions; and
 (3) Terminating sessions after a defined time period.
Response:
VMware Private AI Foundation with NVIDIA (PAIF) provides robust session management capabilities for AI agent interactions. Agent Builder within PAIF allows administrators to configure a session expiration period, ensuring agent sessions are terminated after a defined time. At the API level, all inference calls to Private AI Services utilize Bearer token authentication, which embeds an authentication identifier in each request to the AI model endpoints, thereby providing a mechanism for attributing API requests to authenticated sessions.

While PAIF facilitates session termination and request attribution, the platform does not inherently provide full session correlation across multiple agent interactions to link related sessions to a common user journey or investigation thread. Customers are responsible for implementing external observability tooling to achieve comprehensive session correlation and for defining and configuring appropriate session expiration policies within Agent Builder to align with their security and operational requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prevent cognitive overload or decision fatigue for humans-in-the-loop (HITL) through risk-based prioritization.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement Robotic Process Automation (RPA) to improve efficiency, accuracy and speed for high-volume, repetitive and rules-based business processes.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust, scalable, and secure virtualized infrastructure that serves as the foundational platform for deploying and operating Robotic Process Automation (RPA) solutions. It delivers the necessary compute, storage, and networking resources to host RPA software platforms and their associated bot execution environments, ensuring high availability and performance for automated business processes.

While VCF furnishes the underlying infrastructure, it does not natively incorporate RPA mechanisms or provide built-in capabilities for defining, orchestrating, or executing robotic processes. The deployment, configuration, and management of specific RPA software, the design of automated workflows, and the establishment of operational policies for RPA fall entirely within the customer's responsibility.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and enumerate business process task activities that can be executed both manually and in an automated fashion.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to facilitate an IT Asset Management (ITAM) program to implement and manage asset management controls.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms that directly facilitate IT Asset Management programs. It includes built-in inventory and discovery capabilities, offering detailed lists of compute, storage, and networking resources. VCF Operations logically groups discovered objects, and the Service Discovery adapter automatically identifies applications, services, and their associated metrics running on the managed infrastructure. This discovered application data can be exported in CSV format for integration with external asset tracking systems.

Furthermore, VCF offers seamless integration with ServiceNow, populating the ServiceNow CMDB with discovered resources through the Management Pack for ServiceNow in VCF Operations and maintaining synchronization via extensibility action scripts and a dedicated ServiceNow ITSM Plug-in in VCF Automation. Within VCF Automation, governance is enhanced through projects that link users, catalog items, and IaaS resources, along with policies that govern provisioned resources such as VMs and Kubernetes clusters. Tags and Day 2 Action policies further control access and management actions for these assets.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the essential technical foundation and integration points for asset discovery, inventory, and internal governance, the broader responsibilities of establishing comprehensive asset governance policies, defining asset ownership lifecycles, and maintaining organizational accountability for the overall IT Asset Management program remain within the customer's purview.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and assess the security of Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS), Applications and/or Services (TAAS) that support more than one critical business function.
Response:
VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform provides robust mechanisms for asset identification and classification. It organizes inventory assets into a defined hierarchy spanning region, zone, environment, application, and application tier, enabling the classification of assets as infrastructure or applications, including designations for critical or regular status. This classification directly supports understanding which assets underpin critical business functions.

The platform's Security Intelligence capabilities, through traffic flow analysis and visualization, offer deep visibility into asset interdependencies and communication patterns at the network layer, which is crucial for identifying service dependencies.

Furthermore, the Security Services Platform inventory facilitates importing asset information via CSV and allows for filtering infrastructure assets by name and status, thereby supporting comprehensive asset discovery and lifecycle tracking within the network layer.

While vDefend significantly contributes to the identification and dependency mapping of critical technology assets, applications, and services, the ultimate responsibility for defining critical business functions and mapping assets to those functions rests with the customer. The platform provides the technical foundation for network-layer asset inventory and dependency mapping but does not replace the need for a comprehensive enterprise asset management system or the customer's processes for formally assessing the security posture of these identified critical assets.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and involve pertinent stakeholders of critical Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD) to support the ongoing secure management of those assets.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement a scalable, standardized naming convention for Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD) that avoids asset naming conflicts.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to maintain a current list of approved technologies (hardware and software).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive mechanisms for maintaining a detailed, real-time inventory of technology assets across compute, storage, and networking layers. vCenter serves as the authoritative inventory for all managed objects, including ESX hosts, clusters, virtual machines, datastores, and networking constructs, with programmatic access via the vSphere Web Services API. VCF Operations retrieves and logically groups these inventory details, offering interactive dashboards and a Service Discovery adapter that identifies running applications and services with CPU and memory metrics, allowing for grouping and export. VCF Automation tracks provisioned resources through its project model, linking users, catalog items, and IaaS resources, with inventory views scoped by rights and project roles to support audit and review. For software inventory, vSphere Lifecycle Manager maintains a depot with metadata about installed components and available updates, tracking versions across the environment. Integration with external asset registries is supported through the Management Pack for ServiceNow, which populates the ServiceNow CMDB, and VCF Operations for Networks, which can fetch configuration items and relationships, with VCF Automation supporting CMDB reconciliation. VMware vDefend further contributes by maintaining an inventory of network-visible workloads, organized into asset collections spanning groups, infrastructure assets, environments, applications, and application tiers, supporting import of inventory data and filtering for reporting.

While VMware Cloud Foundation tracks software versions and provides extensive technical inventory, the identification of authorized software products with associated business justification details, along with enterprise asset classification and property accountability processes, remains an organizational responsibility. vDefend's inventory scope is limited to network-visible workloads and does not replace enterprise-wide TAASD inventory tools, authorized software catalogs with business justification records, or comprehensive data asset registries required for full compliance.

Description: Mechanisms exist to update asset inventories as part of component installations, removals and asset upgrades.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to detect and alert upon the detection of unauthorized hardware, software and firmware components.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a comprehensive suite of automated mechanisms to detect and alert upon unauthorized hardware, software, and firmware components across the infrastructure stack.

At the hardware and firmware level, ESX hosts leverage Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Modules (TPM), and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT) to validate boot-time measurements and ensure hosts boot into known-good configurations, with vCenter retrieving TPM event logs. The hypervisor enforces code signing to block unsigned software.

For host software, vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) establishes a desired-state software image for clusters, automatically flagging and remediating hosts that deviate. vSphere Configuration Profiles extend this to host settings, detecting and remediating configuration drift. VCF Operations provides configuration drift detection for vCenter and vSphere clusters against defined templates, displaying deviations in a dedicated dashboard, and offers compliance monitoring against regulatory and custom benchmarks, generating alerts for violations.

For container workloads, Harbor integration provides image signing, vulnerability scanning, and content trust, blocking unauthorized or vulnerable images.

VMware vDefend further enhances detection through its network-layer capabilities.

vDefend IDS/IPS inspects east-west and north-south traffic for signatures indicative of known malware, exploit kits, or command-and-control communication, automatically detecting and alerting or blocking unauthorized software activity. Malware Prevention extends this to file-level analysis, inspecting files traversing the network for known and unknown malware.

vDefend Network Detection and Response (NDR) and Security Intelligence analyze network traffic flows to identify anomalous communication patterns, correlating events to surface indicators of compromise related to unauthorized software or devices. The Security Services Platform provides configurable detectors for suspicious network traffic, identifying activity from unauthorized components.

Additionally, the vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation, blocking unauthorized communication attempts and logging violations, thereby providing a deny-by-default posture that surfaces attempts by unauthorized components to communicate within the environment.

While these automated mechanisms are robustly provided, the customer is responsible for defining the specific authorized states, such as desired software images, configuration templates, and compliance benchmarks, against which unauthorized components are identified. This includes activating, configuring, and tuning the various detection capabilities, as well as establishing operational processes for monitoring the generated alerts and responding to detected unauthorized components.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to employ Network Access Control (NAC), or a similar technology, which is capable of detecting unauthorized devices and disable network access to those unauthorized devices.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, through NSX SpoofGuard, automatically enforces device identity at the virtual network layer by maintaining a reference table of VM names, MAC addresses, and IP addresses. This mechanism blocks traffic from devices whose source addresses do not match expected bindings on the corresponding logical port or segment, thereby detecting unauthorized address changes and disabling network access.

NSX also drops forged MAC packets by default, providing an additional layer of source address validation.

Network segmentation capabilities, including VLANs, private VLANs, overlay-based segments, and VPC subnets with various access modes, restrict connectivity and isolate workloads, limiting the reach of unauthorized devices.

VCF Operations for Networks provides automated network device discovery, identifying devices and flagging those with default manufacturer passwords.

VMware vDefend further enhances these capabilities.

The Distributed Firewall enforces security policy at the vNIC level, ensuring every virtual machine's network interface is subject to firewall rules. This allows administrators to define rules based on IP sets, MAC sets, security groups, and tags, restricting network access to only those workloads matching authorized criteria. Any workload not covered by an allow rule is blocked by the default deny posture, acting as a form of network access control.

The Identity Firewall extends this by integrating with Active Directory for user identities and using event log scraping for physical devices, enabling access decisions to be tied to authenticated identities. Devices lacking proper identity bindings can be denied network access at the firewall layer.

The Gateway Firewall provides North-South traffic control, allowing administrators to control which devices can communicate across network boundaries.

Security Intelligence provides traffic flow analysis and visualization, supporting the identification of unauthorized or anomalous devices through unexpected traffic patterns.

While these platform features provide robust automated mechanisms for detecting unauthorized devices based on network identity, traffic patterns, and defined policies, and for disabling their network access, a dedicated Network Access Control (NAC) solution that performs 802.1X port-based authentication, comprehensive endpoint posture assessment, and automated quarantine of non-compliant endpoints remains outside the native scope of the platform.

Customers are responsible for defining and configuring the specific security policies, firewall rules, and identity integrations that drive these automated detection and enforcement capabilities.

Description: Mechanisms exist to enable Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server logging to improve asset inventories and assist in detecting unknown systems.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, through NSX, provides robust DHCP server services with integrated audit logging capabilities. NSX generates comprehensive audit events for all DHCP operations, including the creation, modification, and deletion of DHCP profiles, static bindings, and IP pools, effectively capturing the full lifecycle of DHCP address management within the virtual network. NSX IP Discovery Segment Profiles further enhance visibility by supporting DHCP Snooping for IPv4 environments, which tracks address assignments to virtual machines on network segments.

These DHCP-related events can be forwarded to a centralized logging platform via configurable remote logging server settings, allowing administrators to specify the destination, protocol, log level, and facility for seamless integration with broader infrastructure logging. Additionally, NSX offers API endpoints to programmatically query the realized state of DHCP server configurations and static bindings, providing essential data for inventory reconciliation.

VMware vDefend complements these capabilities by offering network visibility through Security Intelligence flow analysis and Distributed Firewall logging, which can assist in detecting unknown or rogue systems communicating on the network. IDS/IPS events and Distributed Firewall logs, including Log Labels, are also forwarded to a central log repository via syslog, establishing centralized audit trails of network activity.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational technical mechanisms for DHCP server logging and network visibility, the effective utilization of these logs to improve asset inventories and detect unknown systems necessitates customer-defined operational processes and tooling. This includes correlating DHCP lease data with authoritative asset records and integrating with external asset management systems for comprehensive analysis and reconciliation.

Description: Mechanisms exist to protect Intellectual Property (IP) rights with software licensing restrictions.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive mechanisms to enforce software licensing restrictions for both the platform itself and the workloads it hosts. VCF includes integrated license management capabilities that enforce subscription capacity limits across the environment, tracking usage to ensure it does not exceed purchased entitlements. This system supports both connected and disconnected operational modes and facilitates license splitting and sharing to align with deployment topologies. The vSphere LicenseManager API offers programmatic access to license state, enabling robust reporting and compliance verification.

For applications running as virtual machines, vSphere within VCF offers several controls to aid in compliance with software licensing terms. DRS affinity and anti-affinity rules restrict VM placement to specific hosts, which is critical for software licensed on a per-host or per-socket basis. VM hardware configuration limits, such as assigned vCPUs and memory, directly support compliance with per-core or per-vCPU licensing models. Resource pools further establish boundaries on compute resource consumption. Additionally, vSphere Lifecycle Manager tracks software component versions deployed across clusters and hosts, providing administrators with essential visibility for software license reconciliation.

Establishing organizational policies that define authorized software, maintaining accurate records of entitlements against procurement, and conducting regular license compliance audits remain responsibilities of the customer.

Description: Mechanisms exist to create and maintain a map of Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) where sensitive/regulated data is stored, transmitted or processed.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for discovering and mapping technology assets and their interactions.

VCF Operations for Networks collects and analyzes network flow data, identifying communication patterns between workloads and applications, which can be exported for documentation. The VCF Operations network map offers an end-to-end interactive topology of physical and virtual devices, illustrating data sources and network paths.

vCenter provides comprehensive inventory visibility, including logical and physical infrastructure components, and supports storage tagging for classification. The Property Collector API enables programmatic inventory traversal to enumerate objects and their properties.

VMware Live Recovery further contributes by documenting where replicated copies of data are stored across protected and recovery sites, providing visibility into protection groups, inventory mappings, and storage policy translations.

DR IP Customizer and IP address map reporter generate documentation of networking configurations for protected virtual machines, mapping network identity changes during recovery.

VMware vDefend enhances these capabilities through Security Intelligence, which collects and visualizes network traffic flows between compute entities, and Security Services Platform, which maintains an asset inventory with hierarchy mapping. Assets can be organized into collections representing security zones and tagged for classification by data sensitivity or regulatory scope, allowing for tracing data movement paths across the environment.

While these platforms provide extensive technical mechanisms for discovering, visualizing, and tracking assets, network flows, and storage locations, the classification of data as sensitive or regulated remains an organizational responsibility. The formal creation and ongoing maintenance of a comprehensive data action map, which defines the scope of sensitive data and integrates these technical insights, is a customer-driven process that leverages the platform's capabilities.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement and manage a Configuration Management Database (CMDB), or similar technology, to monitor and govern technology asset-specific information.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust configuration management capabilities that function as a CMDB-like technology for the virtual infrastructure, and it is designed to integrate seamlessly with external CMDBs. VCF Operations maintains a comprehensive inventory of configuration items across the environment, tracking objects such as compute hosts, virtual machines, datastores, switches, services, and applications. Each configuration item is associated with a class that defines its properties, and VCF Operations tracks relationships between items, such as dependencies, execution environments, and data exchange. Historical inventory metrics capture configuration property changes over time, providing an auditable record of how assets evolve within the VCF domain.

For organizations utilizing ServiceNow, the Management Pack for ServiceNow in VCF Operations populates the ServiceNow CMDB with discovered resources, leveraging a `cmdbSync` adapter that supports configurable synchronization methods, object identification sources, custom field mappings, and name filters to control resource synchronization and appearance. VCF Operations for Networks further enhances this by integrating with ServiceNow CMDB as a data source, fetching application and tier details and retrieving CMDB statistics via the ServiceNow API. Additionally, VCF Automation offers a separate CMDB integration path through its ServiceNow ITSM Plug-in, which supports CMDB reconciliation to ensure configuration items remain current as resources are provisioned and modified.

While VCF provides the technical mechanisms for asset discovery, tracking, and integration, the overarching organizational decisions regarding CMDB governance remain the responsibility of the customer. This includes defining which specific assets to track beyond the virtual infrastructure, establishing classification schemes for configuration items, and implementing processes to maintain data quality and integrity within the CMDB.

Description: Mechanisms exist to track the geographic location of system components.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to bind components to a specific system.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms to bind various components to specific systems within its managed infrastructure. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) establishes a desired-state software specification for each cluster, explicitly binding specific software components, firmware, and driver versions to the hosts. Compliance checks ensure hosts adhere to this specification, flagging any deviations. This is further extended by vSphere Configuration Profiles, which manage host configuration settings against a cluster-level specification, automatically detecting and remediating drift.

Additionally, DRS affinity and anti-affinity rules bind virtual machines to specific hosts or host groups, enforcing placement and migration decisions. VCF Operations object tags create trackable associations between components and systems, while its relationship mapping capabilities define dependencies across the environment.

While VCF provides these robust technical bindings, broader organizational processes for component assignment, including tracking physical asset ownership or binding components to systems outside the VCF-managed infrastructure, fall outside the platform's direct scope. These aspects require customer-defined policies and procedures to ensure comprehensive coverage.
