Description: Mechanisms exist to conduct tests and/or exercises to evaluate the contingency plan's effectiveness and the organization's readiness to execute the plan.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational infrastructure and capabilities for exercising contingency plans. vSAN stretched clusters can be exercised by simulating site failures through host maintenance mode operations, validating that the surviving availability zone continues storage and compute operations. vSphere HA can be tested by deliberately failing hosts to observe VM restart behavior and confirm that admission control policies reserve sufficient capacity. VMware HCX supports test workload migrations between sites, allowing organizations to exercise mobility procedures and validate that workloads can be moved to alternate processing locations. Management component backup and restore procedures can be tested by performing restore operations for SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX, and VCF Operations components. VCF Operations capacity planning scenarios allow teams to model infrastructure changes without implementing them, supporting planning for test exercises, and Changed Block Tracking enables incremental backups through VADP-integrated solutions that can be tested independently.

VMware Live Site Recovery provides non-disruptive testing of disaster recovery plans as a core operational capability. Recovery plans organize protected workloads into sequenced, dependency-aware execution groups, and these plans can be tested without affecting production operations on either the protected or recovery site. Test recovery simulates an unplanned site failure to verify that configured RTO and RPO targets can be met, while planned migration validates orderly site evacuation, confirming that workloads can be moved between sites in a controlled manner. Both modes exercise the full recovery sequence, including VM power-on ordering, network reconfiguration, and IP address customization, while making concessions to avoid disrupting ongoing operations. Testing a recovery plan has no lasting effects on either site, which means exercises can be repeated at any frequency without risk to production workloads. Recovery plan testing can also be automated through the VMware Live Site Recovery API and PowerCLI, with the Initiate Test Recovery Plan workflow verifying that the recovery plan is in a ready state before allowing a test to proceed. VMware vSphere Replication contributes to test readiness by maintaining current replica copies at the recovery site through continuous host-based replication with configurable RPO intervals, with Changed Block Tracking reducing the data transfer required to keep replicas current.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms for conducting tests and exercises, the organizational elements of contingency plan testing, such as exercise scheduling, scenario design, evaluation criteria, after-action reviews, and the overarching policy for evaluating effectiveness and organizational readiness, remain the customer's responsibility.

Description: Mechanisms exist to coordinate contingency plan testing with internal and external elements responsible for related plans.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational technical platform and integration points necessary to validate recovery capabilities across internal and external elements. This includes support for third-party VADP-based backup solutions deployed on management clusters, allowing contingency plan testing to exercise the full chain of dependencies, including external backup partners.

VMware HCX facilitates coordinated workload mobility exercises through test migrations between sites. Management component backup and restore can be tested across SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX, and VCF Operations, validating the end-to-end recovery chain.

VMware Live Site Recovery further enhances coordinated testing by providing technical mechanisms such as recovery plans built from protection groups, which track state to prevent conflicts when shared across multiple plans. Recovery plans support dependencies between workload groups, enabling the sequencing of recovery steps during test runs. VMware vSphere Replication supports connectivity testing between compute resources, validating replication infrastructure. The VCF Operations orchestrator plug-in for VMware Live Site Recovery allows programmatic initiation of test recovery workflows, integrating DR test exercises into broader infrastructure automation.

While these technical capabilities are robust, the formal coordination of contingency plan testing schedules, stakeholder communication, alignment with related organizational plans, communication of test windows, alignment of test objectives, and joint after-action reviews remain organizational responsibilities.

Description: Mechanisms exist to test contingency plans at alternate storage & processing sites to both familiarize contingency personnel with the facility and evaluate the capabilities of the alternate processing site to support contingency operations.
Response:
VMware Live Recovery provides robust orchestration and testing mechanisms essential for validating contingency operations at alternate processing and storage sites. Recovery plans precisely define workload failover sequences from a protected site to a designated recovery site. The test recovery mode executes the complete recovery sequence at the alternate site without impacting production operations at either location. This allows organizations to thoroughly evaluate the recovery site's ability to host protected workloads, validate storage availability, and confirm network connectivity. VMware Live Recovery supports bidirectional protection, enabling test exercises in either direction, and utilizes multiple replication methods, including VMware vSphere Replication and array-based replication, to maintain data synchronization. Recovery plan tests can be automated through the VMware Live Recovery API and the VCF Operations orchestrator plug-in, facilitating periodic alternate-site validation exercises.

VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) further contributes by providing the foundational infrastructure. vSAN stretched clusters extend a single logical cluster across two availability zones, keeping both sites active simultaneously, which inherently familiarizes personnel at both sites with the infrastructure. Administrators can simulate site failures by placing hosts in maintenance mode to observe continued operations. VMware HCX enables workload mobility between sites, allowing test migrations to evaluate the alternate site's readiness to host production workloads through various migration methods. Additionally, management component backup and restore operations can be tested at the alternate site by performing restore operations for SDDC Manager, vCenter, and VCF Operations on freshly deployed appliances.

While VMware Cloud Foundation and VMware Live Recovery provide the critical technical mechanisms for testing contingency plans and evaluating alternate site capabilities, the comprehensive, automated, non-disruptive DR testing with orchestrated failover of entire protection groups requires the customer to define the structured test framework and implement organizational processes. The alternate processing and storage facilities themselves, including site selection, provisioning, and ongoing facility management, remain organizational responsibilities.

Description: Mechanisms exist to conduct a Root Cause Analysis (RCA) and "lessons learned" activity every time the contingency plan is activated.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.
End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical capabilities to identify infrastructure components and changes relevant to contingency planning. VCF Operations delivers topology mapping, infrastructure visualization, and real-time inventory of managed objects including hosts, virtual machines, clusters, datastores, and networks. These features make it straightforward to identify deployed technologies and their interdependencies, reflecting changes in topology views to keep documentation current.

VCF Operations for Networks supports diagramming of live network paths and data flows, which helps identify data repositories, data movement patterns, and the supporting network infrastructure. Capacity planning features allow modeling of what-if scenarios to understand the impact of infrastructure changes before implementation, informing contingency planning around capacity constraints.

VMware Live Recovery tracks technology and data-flow components that directly affect disaster recovery plan execution. Protection groups define virtual machines for contingency plans, and recovery plans organize these into sequenced execution workflows with defined dependencies and priority. Recovery plan state management provides continuous visibility into plan executability, reflecting impacts when deployed technologies change, such as replication failures, unavailable protection groups, or offline datastores. VMware vSphere Replication tracks replication status for each protected VM, surfacing changes to the replication infrastructure like target datastore capacity or network path availability, which helps identify data repository and data flow changes affecting contingency plan execution. Recovery plan customization steps document the specific order and context for operations, aiding in evaluating how infrastructure changes might affect plan execution.

VMware vDefend Security Intelligence Segmentation Planning supports identification of deployed technologies and data flows by cataloging infrastructure assets, validating policy scope hierarchies, and tracking publish status of segmentation policies, which helps surface changes to deployed technologies and data repositories or flows that could affect contingency plan execution.

The VMware Cloud Foundation platform, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend do not provide mechanisms for tracking personnel roles, business processes (including third-party service dependencies), or physical infrastructure changes. These aspects are organizational governance responsibilities and facility components that fall outside the scope of the platform and require additional organizational processes and tooling for comprehensive coverage.

Description: Mechanisms exist to keep stakeholders informed of changes to contingency plans.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement alternative or compensating controls to satisfy security functions when the primary means of implementing the security function is unavailable or compromised.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements a robust, layered security architecture where multiple independent controls inherently address the same security objectives, providing natural compensating coverage when any single control is unavailable or compromised.

For network isolation, VCF provides NSX segments for logical separation, VLANs and Private VLANs for Layer 2 isolation, and gateway firewalling to control traffic at network boundaries. These mechanisms are complementary; if one isolation method is bypassed, the others continue to restrict lateral movement. VMware vDefend Distributed Firewall further extends this with microsegmentation, adding another independent layer of enforcement at the workload vNIC.

For access control, VCF implements role-based access control at multiple levels, with vCenter enforcing granular permissions. If a primary identity provider is temporarily unavailable, local fallback accounts can maintain administrative access, and VCF SSO supports identity federation, allowing organizations to use multiple identity sources as alternatives.

Data protection is achieved through TLS encryption for management interfaces and API endpoints, certificate-based authentication between components, and encrypted vSAN datastores for data at rest, all operating independently at different layers of the stack.

VCF Operations provides continuous compliance monitoring, surfacing the security posture of the environment and helping administrators detect when a primary security control has drifted from its baseline configuration, thereby supporting the timely activation of compensating measures.

VMware vDefend further enhances this capability with its layered network security architecture, where independent controls address overlapping security objectives.

The Gateway Firewall enforces policy at NSX Tier-0 and Tier-1 gateway boundaries, while the Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation rules at the vNIC level on every workload, operating independently. These enforcement points are architecturally separate; a misconfiguration or bypass at the gateway level does not negate the Distributed Firewall's ability to restrict lateral movement, and vice versa.

Beyond firewalling, vDefend layers additional security functions that serve as compensating controls, including IDS/IPS for traffic inspection, Malware Prevention and sandboxing (ATP) for file content analysis, and Network Detection and Response (NDR) for behavioral anomaly detection. These controls operate at different layers of the network security stack and employ distinct detection methodologies, ensuring that a failure or gap in one does not eliminate the protection provided by the others.

Security Services Platform contributes to compensating control readiness by continuously analyzing the deployed rule base for weaknesses such as duplicate, redundant, or overly permissive rules, helping administrators maintain the effectiveness of each independent security layer.

Security Intelligence further supports this by generating microsegmentation policy recommendations based on observed traffic patterns, enabling the implementation of more granular controls that tighten the overall security posture.

While the platform provides extensive technical mechanisms for compensating controls, the selection and activation of specific compensating controls when a primary control fails, along with the organizational processes for documenting and approving these measures, remain operational decisions and responsibilities of the customer.

Description: Mechanisms exist to establish an alternate storage site that includes both the assets and necessary agreements to permit the storage and recovery of system backup information.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the technical mechanisms for establishing and managing alternate storage sites. vSAN stretched clusters extend storage across physically separated data sites, maintaining synchronized data copies at both locations and providing continuous storage operations even if one site goes offline. VMware HCX facilitates workload mobility and replication to remote sites, enabling data movement to alternate locations. For Kubernetes workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) cluster management directs backup data to specified target locations, including S3-compatible storage, separate from the primary VCF environment. VCF also supports Changed Block Tracking (CBT) for efficient incremental backups to alternate sites via VADP-integrated solutions.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further enhances these capabilities by providing comprehensive technical mechanisms for replicating and managing data at an alternate storage site. VLR orchestrates data replication between protected and recovery sites, supporting methods such as vSphere Replication for individual VMs, array-based replication for datastores, and policy-driven replication for VMware Virtual Volumes environments. VLR utilizes placeholder datastores at the recovery site to reserve capacity for protected virtual machines, ensuring readiness for recovery operations.

While VCF and VLR provide the robust technical foundation and mechanisms for data replication, storage management, and recovery at alternate sites, the establishment of the physical alternate storage facility, including site selection, provisioning of underlying hardware and network resources, and the negotiation and execution of necessary contractual agreements for storage and recovery permissions, remains an organizational responsibility. Organizations must ensure sufficient storage capacity and stable network connectivity at the target location.

Description: Mechanisms exist to separate the alternate storage site from the primary storage site to reduce susceptibility to similar threats.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for separating primary and alternate storage sites to mitigate shared threats. vSAN stretched clusters distribute data across geographically distinct sites, utilizing a three-site topology with a witness host to ensure no single site failure results in data loss. Layer 3 networking between data sites reinforces physical separation with network-level independence.

Within a single site, vSAN fault domains provide rack-level separation, distributing data replicas across different physical racks to reduce susceptibility to localized hardware failures. Storage policies with site disaster tolerance settings control data placement across sites and fault domains, allowing administrators to align data resilience with specific availability requirements.

VMware HCX extends workload mobility to remote sites, and VKS cluster management supports directing backup data to S3-compatible storage at separate locations, further enabling distributed storage architectures.

VMware Live Recovery inherently operates across geographically separated sites, with distinct protected and recovery infrastructure environments. This two-site architecture orchestrates data replication and recovery operations, directly supporting the separation of alternate storage from the primary site.

VLR facilitates storage-level isolation through various mechanisms, including array-based replication with separate LUNs or dedicated storage arrays for full logical isolation. Vendor-specific stretched storage isolation behaviors prevent failure propagation by making devices inaccessible or breaking communication post-failover. VMware vSphere Replication provides host-based replication independent of the underlying storage array, creating replicas at the recovery site without shared storage infrastructure, thereby eliminating shared storage failure domains.

The operational model ensures site independence, requiring explicit reprotection after a failover. Network isolation is further supported by recommending isolated VLANs for test recovery, preventing unintended connectivity between sites.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and mitigate potential accessibility problems to the alternate storage site in the event of an area-wide disruption or disaster.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to establish an alternate processing site that provides security measures equivalent to that of the primary site.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the foundational infrastructure platform for establishing alternate processing sites, ensuring consistent security and operational capabilities across locations. This includes vSAN stretched clusters that extend logical clusters across two availability zones, mirroring data and enabling site failure tolerance while maintaining data accessibility. NSX Federation delivers centralized network policy configuration and enforcement across multiple locations, ensuring consistent network security between primary and alternate sites, with NSX disaster recovery deployments allowing secondary sites to assume networking functions during catastrophic failures. VCF Operations further supports single-source configuration for identity and access management, security baselines, configuration profiles, and access controls across multiple geolocations, thereby maintaining equivalent security configurations at the alternate site. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) then provides the orchestration platform for operating these alternate processing sites, facilitating both planned migrations and unplanned disaster recovery by migrating business-critical services to the recovery site, which must possess equivalent hardware, network, and storage resources. VMware vDefend, deployable on vSAN stretched clusters, contributes the network security policy layer, ensuring distributed firewall, IDS/IPS, and other security capabilities operate with equivalent policy enforcement at the alternate site when scaled appropriately.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for establishing and operating an alternate processing site with equivalent security capabilities, the establishment of the physical alternate processing facility itself and its associated agreements remains an organizational responsibility. Organizations must also ensure adequate capacity is reserved at the alternate site for failover scenarios and are responsible for defining and consistently applying specific security baselines, configuration profiles, and access controls across all managed sites using the VCF Operations capabilities.

Description: Mechanisms exist to separate the alternate processing site from the primary processing site to reduce susceptibility to similar threats.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides inherent mechanisms for separating compute and storage across geographically distinct sites, significantly reducing susceptibility to shared threats. This is achieved through vSAN stretched clusters, which distribute data across two physically separated data sites with a witness host deployed at a third independent site, establishing a three-location topology that prevents data loss from a single site failure. NSX disaster recovery deployments inherently position a secondary site to assume networking and workload operations following a catastrophic primary site failure, leveraging the physical separation of multi-site models. Within a single site, vSAN fault domains provide rack-level separation, and storage policies with site disaster tolerance settings control data placement across sites and fault domains.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further reinforces this by building its disaster recovery model on the fundamental separation of protected and recovery sites, typically involving a business-critical datacenter and an alternative infrastructure at a separate location. VLR supports full logical isolation at the storage layer through array-based replication using distinct LUNs or separate storage arrays, and vSphere Replication provides host-based replication independent of storage arrays, preventing shared storage failure domains. Bidirectional protection configurations enhance operational independence, and network isolation is supported via isolated VLANs for test recovery, preventing unintended connectivity.

VMware vDefend contributes network-layer isolation controls, such as deploying Network Detection and Response (NDR) in isolated networks for standalone operation at a secondary site, and isolating VDI server environments to prevent threat propagation through shared network segments.

While these robust mechanisms are provided by the platform, the customer is responsible for the strategic decisions regarding geographic site selection, the physical deployment of infrastructure across distinct locations, and the configuration of network routing to ensure complete isolation and fault tolerance between primary and alternate processing sites.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and mitigate potential accessibility problems to the alternate processing site and possible mitigation actions, in the event of an area-wide disruption or disaster.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides architectural options for establishing and maintaining alternate processing sites, including vSAN stretched clusters that extend storage across availability zones and monitor connectivity to identify accessibility issues. NSX supports multisite networking deployments with Federation, allowing stretched gateways to maintain network connectivity for recovered workloads during catastrophic failures. VMware HCX facilitates workload mobility to remote targets, supporting migrations with minimal disruption.

VCF Operations offers what-if analysis and committed scenarios, serving as planning tools to identify potential accessibility problems before a disruption occurs.

VMware Live Recovery further provides mechanisms to identify and mitigate accessibility problems both before and during area-wide disruptions. Its planned migration capabilities enable regular testing of the recovery path, validating site accessibility, replication infrastructure, and successful recovery plan execution. VLR supports multiple replication methods, including vSphere Replication and array-based replication, offering options to mitigate accessibility risks based on infrastructure. Bidirectional protection provides an additional mitigation path, ensuring recovery options even if one direction is inaccessible, and VLR's failover continues without interruption even if the protected site experiences errors during a disaster recovery operation.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms and tools, broader accessibility planning, including network path redundancy, comprehensive capacity modeling for recovery site resources, and detailed area-wide disruption scenario analysis, remains an organizational planning responsibility.

Administrators must also be aware of specific accessibility considerations, such as the impact on array-based replication mirroring if the protected site's storage array is offline. Furthermore, ensuring the recovery site has adequate hardware, network, and storage resources, and that users with appropriate permissions can access the site to create, test, and run recovery plans, falls under customer operational management.

Description: Mechanisms exist to address priority-of-service provisions in alternate processing and storage sites that support availability requirements, including Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation and VMware Live Recovery provide robust technical mechanisms to establish and enforce priority-of-service provisions across primary and alternate processing and storage sites, directly supporting availability requirements and Recovery Time Objectives.

VCF enables granular resource allocation through vSphere resource pools, allowing hierarchical assignment of CPU and memory with reservations, limits, and shares that can be consistently applied across sites. Network traffic prioritization is managed via NSX QoS segment profiles, supporting Class of Service (CoS) and Differentiated Services Code Point (DSCP) marking and shaping, alongside Network I/O Control (NIOC) on the vSphere Distributed Switch for bandwidth allocation among predefined traffic categories. vSAN storage policies define availability and performance requirements per VM, including failures to tolerate and site disaster tolerance for stretched clusters, ensuring data resilience and performance consistency.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities specifically for disaster recovery operations, allowing administrators to assign priority levels (1-5) to virtual machines within recovery plans, ensuring business-critical workloads are brought online first, with configurable dependencies to enforce startup sequencing. Resource pool mappings between paired sites ensure that recovered workloads receive appropriate CPU and memory allocations at the alternate site, consistent with their service priority. VLR also manages replication requirements through VM storage policies, governing replication frequency and RPO, and performs storage synchronization to minimize data loss during recovery, further supported by site-specific IP customization to reduce network restoration time.

While the platform provides the technical controls for resource prioritization and availability at alternate sites, establishing and enforcing service-level agreements with external providers remains an organizational responsibility. The broader definition and ongoing management of priority-of-service provisions, including their integration with organizational policies and external agreements, are customer duties.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prepare the alternate processing alternate to support essential missions and business functions so that the alternate site is capable of being used as the primary site.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust infrastructure mechanisms to prepare and maintain an alternate processing site capable of assuming primary site functions. vSAN stretched clusters extend storage across two sites with synchronous data mirroring, ensuring both sites are continuously ready with identical data copies. NSX disaster recovery deployments enable a secondary site to take over networking functions, with NSX Federation maintaining consistent policies across locations. VMware HCX facilitates workload mobility and migrations between sites, while vMotion supports live migration of running workloads. Proactive HA monitors hardware health, automatically addressing degraded components to maintain readiness, and vSphere HA admission control reserves capacity for VM restart after host failures.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further enhances site preparation by creating placeholder virtual machines at the recovery site, preserving identity and configuration for rapid instantiation. Continuous data replication, through vSphere Replication, array-based replication, and Virtual Volumes policy-driven replication, ensures the recovery site always holds a current copy of protected workloads. Test recovery and cleanup cycles validate the alternate site's capability to serve as the primary, returning it to a ready state without impacting its operational potential. Planned migration workflows enable orderly workload transfers, making the alternate site the new primary, and VLR actions can be combined with vCenter actions in VCF Operations orchestrator workflows to automate ongoing site preparation. VMware vDefend contributes by allowing segmentation planning within the Security Services Platform to validate and publish infrastructure asset inventories and firewall policies, confirming security policy coverage at the alternate site prior to activation.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for infrastructure, data, and network readiness, the customer is responsible for defining essential missions and business functions, mapping these to specific workloads, and establishing the broader organizational processes required for full business function readiness at the alternate site.

Description: Mechanisms exist to plan and prepare for both natural and manmade circumstances that preclude returning to the primary processing site.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical capabilities to support continued operations from an alternate site when the primary processing site is unavailable. NSX disaster recovery deployments enable a secondary site to seamlessly take over networking, with tier-0 and tier-1 standby gateways automatically becoming active and NSX Manager recovery completing rapidly, allowing the secondary site to function as the permanent primary without manual networking reconfiguration. vSAN stretched clusters deliver site-level storage resilience, ensuring continuous storage operations at the secondary site if the preferred site goes offline or becomes isolated, with automatic failover to functional sites. VCF Operations capacity planning tools further assist administrators in modeling and saving workload planning scenarios, which can be utilized for permanent relocation of workloads to an alternate site. VMware Live Recovery supports continued operations at the recovery site following a primary site failure, with virtual machines starting and running at the recovery site, which then becomes the operational primary. VLR also facilitates reprotection, allowing for the reversal of site roles to re-establish DR protection from the new operational primary to the former primary (if recovered) or a new site. Recovery plans within VLR support both planned migrations and disaster recovery execution types, offering flexibility for various relocation and failover scenarios.

While the platform provides essential technical mechanisms for operating from an alternate site, the comprehensive planning and preparation for permanent site loss necessitates significant customer involvement. This includes the organizational aspects of procuring replacement infrastructure, establishing contractual arrangements for alternate facilities, and defining long-term business process continuity, all of which remain governance responsibilities outside the scope of VCF and VLR. Furthermore, if a physical catastrophe completely destroys the original site, VMware Live Recovery cannot unpair and recreate the site pairing because both sites must be available for that operation. In such a scenario, while workloads continue running at the recovery site, establishing new DR protection to a replacement third site requires a new VLR deployment and configuration rather than reconfiguring the existing pairing.

Description: Mechanisms exist to reduce the likelihood of a single point of failure with primary telecommunications services.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) inherently reduces the likelihood of single points of failure in primary telecommunications services through multiple layers of integrated redundancy. At the physical network layer, vSphere Distributed Switches support multiple uplinks per host, enabling active and standby configurations for teaming and failover. VCF workload domain host configurations map physical NICs to distributed switch uplinks, leveraging NIC teaming options such as route-based load balancing and Link Aggregation Control Protocol (LACP) for both static and dynamic configurations. This design ensures that traffic automatically fails over to remaining active uplinks upon a physical network interface failure and rebalances upon recovery.

For storage connectivity, vSphere provides native multipathing with configurable path selection policies, exposing multipath management capabilities and runtime state information to monitor and configure redundant storage paths.

Further enhancing resilience, Network Extension High Availability in HCX creates redundant pairs of connections for network extension appliances, safeguarding extended networks from disruptions. VPN redundancy is achievable through the deployment of primary and secondary autonomous Edge L2 VPN clients. VCF Operations supports a High Availability model designed to prevent service interruption during a single availability zone failure, complemented by a High Availability for Networks model that supports scale-up and scale-out for enhanced application performance and resilience.

The combination of vSAN network uplink redundancy, NIC teaming with failover policies, and vSphere Distributed Switch centralized management collectively delivers consistent, redundant network connectivity across all hosts within a VCF deployment.

While VCF provides these robust, built-in mechanisms, the customer is responsible for the proper implementation, configuration, and ongoing management of these features to ensure that the desired level of redundancy is achieved and maintained in their specific environment, including the provisioning of adequate physical uplinks and adherence to best practices.

Description: Mechanisms exist to formalize primary and alternate telecommunications service agreements contain priority-of-service provisions that support availability requirements, including Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to obtain alternate telecommunications services from providers that are separated from primary service providers to reduce susceptibility to the same threats.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust internal mechanisms that reduce susceptibility to shared threats through comprehensive network and storage redundancy. At the physical network layer, vSphere Distributed Switches support multiple uplinks per host, leveraging active and standby configurations for teaming and failover. NIC teaming options, including route-based load balancing and Link Aggregation Control Protocol (LACP), ensure automatic traffic failover and rebalancing upon physical network interface failure. Critical infrastructure traffic, such as vMotion, NFS, and vSAN, can be segmented on dedicated networks for enhanced performance isolation and fault containment. For storage connectivity, vSphere offers native multipathing with configurable path selection policies, and the HostStorageSystem managed object provides capabilities to monitor and configure redundant storage paths. Furthermore, Network Extension High Availability in HCX establishes redundant pairs of connections for network extension appliances, safeguarding extended networks from disruptions, while vSAN Stretched Clusters deliver site-level resilience with support for asymmetrical network connectivity.

While VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a resilient internal infrastructure, the procurement and policy decisions related to obtaining alternate telecommunications services from providers physically separated from primary service providers remain an organizational responsibility. VCF's technical capabilities provide the essential internal foundation that organizations must combine with diverse external telecom providers to achieve comprehensive end-to-end resilience.

Description: Mechanisms exist to contractually-require external service providers to have contingency plans that meet organizational contingency requirements.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to maintain command and control capabilities via alternate communications channels and designating alternative decision makers if primary decision makers are unavailable.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to create recurring backups of data, software and/or system images, as well as verify the integrity of these backups, to ensure the availability of the data to satisfy Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for creating recurring backups of data, software, and system images across its management and workload layers. This includes scheduled file-based and image-based backups for VCF management components such as SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, and vCenter, designed to capture a consistent state for restoration. For workload data, Changed Block Tracking (CBT) enables incremental backups, and VMware vStorage APIs for Data Protection (VADP) offer a standardized interface for third-party backup solutions. vSAN Data Protection and vSAN Express Storage Architecture (ESA) deliver advanced snapshot capabilities with automated scheduling, retention policies, and improved performance for point-in-time recovery. VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) further extends these capabilities to cluster management workloads, supporting recurring schedules and CSI volume snapshots. VMware Live Recovery enhances data protection through continuous host-based replication via VMware vSphere Replication, allowing configurable RPO intervals and the creation of multiple point-in-time snapshots. vSAN Data Protection protection groups within VLR enable the scheduling and management of vSAN snapshots, supporting up to 200 recovery points on a peer site, alongside array-based replication. VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) provides recurring backup and restore capabilities specifically for its control plane, protecting firewall rules, microsegmentation policies, and IDS/IPS configurations with configurable schedules and encrypted transmission to an SFTP server. Integrity verification is supported through VCF's explicit capability for restore testing and VLR's recovery plan testing, which confirms data usability and RPO attainment. vDefend's SSP maintains a backup history to confirm successful operations and supports file-based restoration from a known good state. These integrated capabilities collectively ensure the availability of data to satisfy defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs).

While the platform provides the necessary technical mechanisms, the customer is responsible for defining and configuring specific RTOs and RPOs based on business requirements. This includes establishing and implementing backup schedules, retention policies, and snapshot configurations for all relevant components and workloads. Sufficient storage capacity must be provisioned for backup data, and periodic cleanup of backups, such as NSX Global Manager backups, must be performed. The customer must also manage the integration with third-party backup providers via VADP, provide and manage the SFTP server for vDefend backups, and ensure certificate consistency during vDefend recovery operations. Regular execution of restore testing and recovery plan testing is critical to validate backup integrity and confirm that RTO and RPO targets are consistently met.

Description: Mechanisms exist to routinely test backups that verify the reliability of the backup process, as well as the integrity and availability of the data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for testing backup reliability and verifying data integrity across its management and workload layers. NSX backup monitoring tracks configuration updates, successes, and failures, offering direct visibility into the reliability of the backup process. Administrators can perform on-demand manual backups to verify process functionality and configure recurring scheduled backup jobs. Management component restore operations for SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX, and VCF Operations can be tested to validate backup data integrity.

For workload data protection, vSAN Data Protection enables external replication with automated scheduling, and snapshot restore operations can be tested to validate data integrity. VMware vStorage APIs for Data Protection (VADP) offer a standardized interface for third-party backup solutions, which typically include their own verification capabilities.

VMware Live Site Recovery (VLR) further enhances this by providing non-disruptive testing mechanisms that verify the reliability of the replication process and the integrity and availability of replicated data at the recovery site. Recovery plan testing uses temporary copies to exercise the full recovery sequence without disrupting operations, allowing for frequent validation of recovery plans. Enhanced Replication Mappings testing validates the replication infrastructure, and quiescing improves data consistency. The VCF Operations Management Pack for vSphere Replication tracks virtual machine error states, providing visibility into replication health. Testing a recovery plan creates snapshots on the recovery site, concretely verifying that replicated data can be instantiated as running virtual machines.

VMware vDefend Security Services Platform contributes by providing backup history tracking and monitoring capabilities, offering visibility into the successful completion of scheduled backup operations for the security platform itself, and ensures platform state reliability through accurate time synchronization, certificate consistency, and Machine Health Check.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical capabilities for backup monitoring, scheduling, and the ability to perform restore and recovery tests, the routine execution of these tests, the definition of specific testing procedures, frequency, scope, and the validation of backup content integrity and availability ultimately require customer-defined policies, processes, and human accountability. Active backup restoration testing and integrity validation of backup content, particularly for vDefend components, falls outside the automated scope of the platform and necessitates additional organizational testing procedures.

Description: Mechanisms exist to store backup copies of critical software and other security-related information in a separate facility or in a fire-rated container that is not collocated with the system being backed up.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for storing backup copies of critical software and configuration data at locations separate from the systems being backed up. vSAN stretched clusters inherently distribute data across two physically separated data sites with a witness host at a third location, maintaining copies of data at a geographically distinct facility by design. vSAN Data Protection allows snapshots to be replicated and stored externally to the datastore. Integration with third-party backup providers through VMware vStorage APIs for Data Protection (VADP) and Changed Block Tracking (CBT) enables directing backup data to separate storage infrastructure. VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) cluster management supports specifying a target backup location separate from the cluster storage, including AWS S3 or S3-compatible storage, ensuring backup data can reside on a completely different storage platform. VCF also supports exporting configuration backups for components like vSphere Distributed Switches, with guidance to copy these files to a secure location separate from the appliance.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further strengthens this capability through VMware Live Site Recovery and VMware vSphere Replication, which store replicated copies of protected workloads at a physically separate recovery site, a fundamental design principle of disaster recovery architecture. vSphere Replication maintains replicas at the remote recovery site through continuous host-based replication, with point-in-time snapshots providing historical recovery points stored separately from production data. Live Site Recovery supports array-based and Virtual Volumes policy-driven replication to a remote site, and placeholder datastores at the recovery site are non-replicated, ensuring they reside on independent storage. Bidirectional protection configurations mean each site stores replicated copies of the other site's workloads, ensuring critical information is stored at a separate facility in both directions.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) includes backup and restore capabilities for its critical configuration and security policy data. SSP backup captures platform configuration, feature configurations, NSX Manager connection information, user accounts and roles, security policies, and feature states. This backup data is written to a designated backup server, which can be provisioned on infrastructure separate from the SSP deployment itself. The backup server target is configurable, allowing organizations to direct SSP backups to storage infrastructure located in a separate facility or on a physically distinct storage system from the SSP cluster nodes.

While the platforms provide the technical mechanisms and capabilities to store backup copies in separate facilities or on non-collocated storage, the ultimate responsibility for provisioning the separate facility or fire-rated container, configuring the backup targets to leverage this separation, and establishing the operational processes to ensure compliance rests with the customer. The platforms enable the separation but do not enforce the physical characteristics or geographic placement of the backup media or infrastructure.

Description: Mechanisms exist to reimage assets from configuration-controlled and integrity-protected images that represent a secure, operational state.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms to reimage and restore assets from configuration-controlled and integrity-protected images that represent a secure, operational state.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) delivers image-based lifecycle management for ESXi hosts, utilizing desired software specifications to define approved baseline images inclusive of firmware, drivers, and patches. vLCM actively remediates hosts to return them to this approved baseline upon detecting configuration drift. This capability is further enhanced by vSphere Configuration Profiles, which extend desired-state configuration management with automated drift detection and remediation, ensuring hosts consistently maintain a known-good, secure operational state.

For VCF management components, image-based backup and restore capabilities are provided for critical services such as VCF Operations, fleet management, Identity Broker, Logs, and Networks, with vCenter Server supporting restoration from file-based backups.

The integrity of images is maintained through VCF's robust supply chain security, which includes signed binaries, verified updates, and secure software distribution. Furthermore, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) enforces image registry policies to restrict container image deployments to authorized sources, and content libraries apply OVF security policies to virtual machine images prior to deployment, while Secure Boot for virtual machines verifies guest operating system integrity.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) complements these capabilities by protecting and recovering VM templates, which serve as configuration-controlled images for deploying workloads in a known-good state across sites. VLR ensures these golden images are available at the recovery site, enabling consistent workload deployment post-recovery. Additionally, point-in-time snapshots retained at the recovery site offer granular recovery options for virtual machines.

While VMware vDefend provides mechanisms for capturing and restoring its own platform configuration and persistent volumes via CSI snapshots and installer data backups, its scope is specifically limited to its own components.

The overarching reimaging capabilities for hosts, management components, and the integrity assurance for various image types are foundational elements of the VMware Cloud Foundation platform.

Description: Cryptographic mechanisms exist to prevent the unauthorized disclosure and/or modification of backup information.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust cryptographic mechanisms to protect backup information from unauthorized disclosure and modification across its components. SDDC Manager backup files are encrypted using AES-256-CBC with SHA256 message digest, while VCF Operations and VCF Fleet Management backup data utilizes an encryption passphrase configured during backup. vCenter backup supports encryption with a user-specified password, and NSX backup data is also encrypted, requiring a passphrase for decryption during restore. During management domain recovery, VCF backup files necessitate the encryption password to extract data, ensuring backups remain protected at rest.

VCF's underlying cryptographic libraries are validated against FIPS 140-3 standards, with components like VCF Operations for Networks capable of enabling FIPS mode to restrict operations to validated cryptographic modules. Cryptographic operations within the environment are governed by fine-grained privileges, including a "No Cryptography Administrator" role to prevent unauthorized encryption or decryption, and custom roles can further restrict these capabilities.

VMware Live Recovery extends this protection to replicated data, providing cryptographic safeguards both in transit and at rest. vSphere Replication supports data encryption for replication traffic, securing workload data during network traversal. VLR also supports the protection and recovery of encrypted virtual machines through both array-based and vSphere Replication protection groups, leveraging a trusted connection between vCenter and a Key Management Server (KMS). To maintain protection and recoverability, the recovery and protected sites must utilize a common KMS or KMS clusters with shared encryption keys. vSphere Replication further allows enabling VM encryption for already replicated VMs and enforces a fail-closed posture, preventing unencrypted replication of protected VMs if Secure LWD support is unavailable.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) independently applies cryptographic protection to its own backup data. SSP backup files are encrypted using a passphrase prior to being uploaded to the configured SFTP backup server, ensuring protection at rest and in transit to the server. This passphrase, configured during backup setup, is distinct from other server communication credentials.

Restoring from an SSP backup requires the correct passphrase, preventing unauthorized access to sensitive configuration data such as firewall rules, microsegmentation policies, IDS/IPS profiles, and malware prevention configurations. This encryption prevents attackers from reading or modifying security policies, which could otherwise be exploited for evasion strategies or tampering before a restore operation.

While the platform provides these comprehensive technical capabilities, customers are responsible for securely managing encryption passphrases, passwords, and Key Management Server (KMS) infrastructure, as well as defining and enforcing organizational policies for cryptographic key lifecycle management and access control to ensure the effective utilization of these mechanisms.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize sampling of available backups to test recovery capabilities as part of business continuity plan testing.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for sampling available backups to test recovery capabilities. This includes comprehensive backup and restore functionalities for management components such as SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX Manager, and VCF Operations. Individual components can be selected for test restore operations, directly supporting a sampling approach. vSAN Data Protection offers snapshot capabilities with automated scheduling and retention, allowing individual snapshot restores to be tested for integrity validation. Furthermore, Changed Block Tracking (CBT) facilitates frequent backup samples, and VMware vStorage APIs for Data Protection (VADP) integrate with third-party solutions for extended test restore capabilities. For container workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service supports backup and restore operations, including CSI volume snapshots. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) complements these capabilities by providing non-disruptive test recovery, enabling organizations to sample and validate replicated data as an integral part of business continuity plan testing. Recovery plan testing utilizes temporary copies of replicated data, allowing for frequent validation without impacting production operations. Organizations can select specific recovery plans or enhanced replication mappings to test, effectively sampling protected workloads. VMware vDefend further contributes by enforcing specific prerequisites for restoration, such as consistent node counts and FQDNs, which must be validated during recovery tests, thereby providing a structured framework for sampling backup restores of the security platform's operational state.

While the platform provides the essential technical mechanisms and frameworks for sampling and testing backup restoration across management components, workload snapshots, replicated data, and the security platform, the organization remains responsible for defining the comprehensive sampling strategy, orchestrating the broader business continuity plan testing, selecting which specific backups to test, and validating the recovery of all non-security workloads.

Description: Mechanisms exist to transfer backup data to the alternate storage site at a rate that is capable of meeting both Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for transferring backup and protection data to alternate storage sites at rates capable of meeting diverse recovery objectives. vSAN stretched clusters offer synchronous data mirroring between availability zones, ensuring near-zero Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) for site-level events by committing all writes to both sites before acknowledgment, thereby providing continuous data transfer. VMware HCX facilitates workload mobility with delta synchronization during migration, maintaining replication while awaiting scheduled switchover and supporting various migration methods tailored to specific transfer requirements. Changed Block Tracking (CBT) optimizes incremental backups through VADP-integrated solutions, significantly reducing data transfer volumes and enabling tighter RPO targets without requiring proportionally more bandwidth. vSAN Data Protection extends these capabilities with automated snapshot scheduling and retention, allowing external replication and storage, further enhanced by vSAN Express Storage Architecture (ESA)'s metadata-based snapshots for improved performance and faster remote replication. For Kubernetes workloads, VKS supports directing backup data to S3 or S3-compatible storage at alternate locations, complete with flexible scheduling options. VMware Live Site Recovery and VMware vSphere Replication further enhance these capabilities by transferring replicated data to the alternate storage site at configurable rates, specifically designed to meet RPOs and support Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs). Administrators can set RPOs as low as one minute using enhanced replication, with granular control over replication frequency per protected workload via the RPO slider. Multiple replication methods, including host-based vSphere Replication, array-based replication, and Virtual Volumes policy-driven replication, ensure data transfer occurs according to configured RPO periods, with incremental changes keeping transfer volumes proportional to the data change rate. Non-disruptive test recovery and planned migration exercises validate that configured replication rates and RPO settings meet their targets.

While the platform provides the comprehensive technical mechanisms and capabilities for data transfer, the organization is responsible for defining its specific Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) based on business requirements. Administrators must then configure the replication settings, RPO periods, and transfer schedules within VMware Cloud Foundation and VMware Live Recovery to align with these defined objectives and available network capacity.

Description: Mechanisms exist to maintain a failover capability, which is not collocated with the primary Technology Asset, Application and/or Service (TAAS), which can be activated with little-to-no loss of information or disruption to operations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive, non-collocated failover capabilities across all critical infrastructure layers, ensuring minimal information loss and operational disruption. At the compute layer, vSphere High Availability (HA) and Fault Tolerance (FT) enable rapid VM restarts and zero-downtime failover, respectively, with Proactive HA preventing failures by evacuating VMs from degrading hardware. Storage resilience is delivered through vSAN stretched clusters, which extend a single cluster across geographically distinct sites with synchronous data mirroring, guaranteeing site-level failover with no data loss. Network connectivity is maintained via multi-NIC teaming, LACP, NSX active-standby gateways, and HCX Network Extension High Availability, ensuring continuous network services during link or appliance failures. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further enhances these capabilities by orchestrating cross-site disaster recovery and planned migrations, supporting Recovery Point Objectives (RPO) as low as one minute through various replication methods and automating failover sequences with recovery plans. VMware vDefend integrates high availability for its security services, including the Security Services Platform, Gateway Firewall, and Distributed Firewall, ensuring that security enforcement remains continuous and resilient during infrastructure failovers, thereby maintaining the overall security posture at the recovery site.

The platform provides the necessary technical mechanisms to establish and maintain non-collocated failover capabilities with minimal disruption. Customers are responsible for designing, configuring, and implementing these capabilities according to their specific business continuity and disaster recovery requirements, including defining appropriate RPO/RTO targets, establishing recovery plans, and regularly validating the failover functionality through non-disruptive testing.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement and enforce dual authorization for the deletion or destruction of sensitive backup media and data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical capabilities that facilitate the implementation of dual authorization for the deletion or destruction of sensitive backup media and data. Role-based access control (RBAC) restricts backup and restore operations, allowing organizations to define roles such that no single administrator possesses the authority to both initiate and confirm the destruction of backup media.

Furthermore, VCF's backup infrastructure incorporates multiple layers of protection. SDDC Manager file-based backups are encrypted with AES-256-CBC, requiring both the root user password and the VCF user password for restoration, effectively splitting credentials across different roles. Similarly, vCenter backups necessitate a separate encryption password, and VCF Operations backup data is encrypted with its own passphrase. vSAN data protection further enhances security through an immutability mode, which creates read-only snapshots that are protected from modification or deletion, even by administrators with elevated privileges, thereby providing a technical safeguard against unauthorized data destruction.

While VCF offers these foundational technical controls, it does not include a dedicated, built-in dual-authorization workflow for backup media destruction. Organizations are responsible for layering their own formal approval processes and procedural workflows on top of VCF's RBAC and encryption controls to fully meet the dual-authorization requirement for sensitive backup media and data destruction.

Description: Mechanisms exist to restrict access to backups to privileged users with assigned roles for data backup and recovery operations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements robust role-based access control (RBAC) across its constituent products to restrict access to backup and restore operations. At the platform level, only Admin and Console users can configure these operations through public APIs and CLI. SDDC Manager further limits backup schedule configuration to users with the Admin role. NSX secures backup authentication to the file server using separate credentials, such as password or SSH private key, ensuring independent management from NSX administrative access. VCF Operations enforces its own RBAC for backup and restore, with the administrators role providing comprehensive environment access. VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) requires the Organization Administrator role for data protection target location creation. vCenter mandates that backup operators possess read and write permissions to the backup server, establishing a critical checkpoint for access restriction.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) provides granular role-based access control specifically for replication and recovery operations, featuring multiple predefined role levels designed for least-privilege access. These include the VRM Virtual Machine Recovery User role, which permits VM recovery and low-level file operations without granting broader administrative access, and the Site Recovery Test Administrator role, which allows testing recovery plans but prohibits configuration of protection, creation of protection groups or recovery plans, or actual recovery operations. The Protection Groups Administrator role manages remote sites and protection groups. Protection configuration is a cross-site operation, requiring permissions on both primary and secondary vCenter instances, thereby gating access to recovery site data through dual authorization.

VMware vDefend (SSP) restricts backup and restore operations by requiring authenticated access to an SFTP server, including the provision of the SFTP server's SSH public host key. This ensures that only individuals with valid SFTP credentials can initiate or access backup data. Within SSP itself, only users with sufficient administrative privileges can initiate backup or restore operations, adding a layer of privileged-user access control.

While VCF, VLR, and vDefend provide the necessary technical mechanisms for role-based access control, organizations are responsible for defining specific backup and recovery roles that align with their operational policies. This includes assigning these roles to appropriate personnel through the respective RBAC frameworks. For VLR, administrators must manage permissions in shared recovery site configurations to ensure users have sufficient privileges without accessing resources belonging to others, prevent users from changing roles or assigning permissions for objects not dedicated to their use, and consistently assign roles on both sites to maintain uniform access restrictions. For vDefend, the broader assignment of roles specifically scoped to backup and recovery operations, and their enforcement across the wider IT environment, must be managed through organizational RBAC processes and the SFTP server's own access controls.

Description: Mechanisms exist to restrict access to modify and/or delete backups to privileged users with assigned data backup and recovery operations roles.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust role-based access control (RBAC) to restrict backup and restore operations to privileged users. Only designated Admin and Console users can configure these operations via public APIs and CLI commands, requiring administrator permissions across VCF platform components. SDDC Manager's Access Control settings enable granular configuration of user roles, differentiating between read-only and modify permissions for backup configurations. Within VCF Operations, the administrators role governs environment access, and the Organization Administrator role is necessary for establishing data protection target locations. NSX Manager backup configuration is similarly restricted to administrative users. VCF supports separation of duties through its role model, allowing custom roles with limited privileges to be created by cloning built-in roles, with only a System Administrator capable of modifying predefined role rights. The No Cryptography Administrator role further segregates encryption operations. For enhanced protection, vSAN offers immutable snapshots, which are read-only and cannot be modified or deleted, even by administrators.

VMware Live Recovery enforces granular RBAC to restrict modification and deletion of replicated data, protection groups, and recovery plans. Its role model ensures separation between users who can view and test DR configurations and those authorized to modify or delete them. For instance, the Test Administrator role is prevented from configuring protection, creating/removing protection groups or recovery plans, or performing recoveries. Similarly, the Recovery Plans Administrator role cannot configure protection or create/remove recovery plans. The VRM Administrator role in vSphere Replication can be customized by deselecting specific privileges, such as Manage VR Server, to create roles with tailored restrictions. The VLR Administrator cannot edit inherited permissions, preventing privilege escalation.

VMware vDefend restricts backup and restore operations to users with the necessary administrative privileges, limiting modification or deletion of backup configurations and initiation of restore operations to authorized personnel. The platform supports backup and restore of configuration data and enforces version consistency requirements, ensuring that the SSP version at restore time matches the backup version, thereby preventing unauthorized or incompatible modifications to backup assets.

While these platforms provide comprehensive technical mechanisms for access restriction, organizations retain the responsibility for configuring roles to properly segregate backup and recovery duties and for assigning backup privileges solely to designated personnel. This includes managing permissions in shared recovery site configurations within VMware Live Recovery to ensure users have appropriate access to their own resources without infringing on others, and consistently assigning roles across sites. For VMware vDefend, access controls governing connections to the underlying SFTP backup server reside outside the Security Services Platform and must be managed through the organization's own access control processes. The effective implementation of these mechanisms relies on robust organizational policies and diligent assignment of human accountability.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure the secure recovery and reconstitution of Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) to a known state after a disruption, compromise or failure.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for the secure recovery and reconstitution of technology assets, applications, and services to a known state following disruption, compromise, or failure. This includes vSphere HA for automatic VM restarts, vSphere Fault Tolerance for zero-downtime failover, and Proactive HA for pre-failure evacuation. vSAN stretched clusters enable site-level recovery, while vSAN Data Protection offers snapshot-based point-in-time recovery with automated scheduling and retention. The platform management layer supports file-based and image-based restore operations for vCenter and VCF Operations, with a reconciliation API to ensure topology and infrastructure state consistency. HCX further enhances data mobility and replication for workload recovery scenarios.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities by providing comprehensive mechanisms for orchestrated virtual machine workload recovery. This is managed through protection groups and recovery plans, which define protected VMs and orchestrate recovery steps for both planned migrations and disaster recovery. Multiple replication methods, including vSphere Replication and array-based replication, are supported, with integrated recovery plan testing to validate the recovery process non-disruptively. All communications within VMware Live Recovery are secured via TLS, and appliance management verifies Platform Services Controller certificates, ensuring the integrity of recovery operations. VMware vDefend contributes to this posture by providing backup and restore functionality for its own security infrastructure, including Security Intelligence, Network Detection and Response, and Malware Prevention Service configurations. NSX Federation ensures the continuity of established security frameworks, such as Distributed Firewall rules and microsegmentation policies, across multiple sites during failover events, maintaining the security posture of workloads at the recovery site.

While the platform provides the comprehensive technical mechanisms for secure recovery and reconstitution, the customer is responsible for the proper configuration, implementation, and regular testing of these capabilities. This includes defining specific recovery point objectives (RPOs) and recovery time objectives (RTOs) within recovery plans, establishing appropriate backup schedules and retention policies, and ensuring sufficient resources are reserved for recovery operations.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize specialized backup mechanisms that will allow transaction recovery for transaction-based Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) in accordance with Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides specialized mechanisms for transaction recovery aligned with Recovery Point Objectives. vCenter includes a transaction manager that utilizes workflow journals to reconcile interrupted transactions and ensure transaction-level consistency for vCenter-managed operations. vSAN Data Protection offers snapshot-based point-in-time recovery with configurable scheduling and retention, including metadata-based snapshots from vSAN ESA, which deliver fine-grained recovery points for transaction-based workloads. Changed Block Tracking (CBT) enables incremental backups through VADP-integrated solutions, reducing backup windows and supporting tighter RPO targets.

VMware Live Recovery further enhances these capabilities by providing specialized replication mechanisms, such as vSphere Replication, which allows administrators to set RPO targets at the individual virtual machine level, aligning replication frequency with workload transaction sensitivity. Recovery plans within VMware Live Recovery support custom command recovery steps, enabling the execution of scripts on recovered virtual machines for application-specific transaction recovery operations, such as database consistency checks or transaction log replay, as part of an automated recovery workflow.

While the platform provides these robust technical mechanisms, customers are responsible for defining and implementing specific backup jobs to capture the consistent state of related components at a common point in time. For application-level transaction recovery, administrators must customize recovery plans, understanding the order and context of recovery steps to integrate application-specific transaction recovery operations effectively. The selection and configuration of third-party backup solutions leveraging VADP for application-aware backup capabilities also remain a customer responsibility to fully realize transaction recovery for all transaction-based applications.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement real-time or near-real-time failover capability to maintain availability of critical Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive real-time and near-real-time failover capabilities across all infrastructure layers.

vSphere High Availability automatically restarts virtual machines on surviving hosts, while vSphere Fault Tolerance maintains a live shadow instance for zero-downtime failover. Proactive HA integrates with hardware monitoring to evacuate workloads before component failure. Live migration of running workloads is achieved through vMotion, extending to remote sites via VMware HCX.

Network resilience is ensured through multi-NIC teaming, LACP, and failover policies, with NSX Tier-0 and Tier-1 gateways supporting active-standby high availability.

Storage multipathing provides automatic failover to maintain access, and vSAN delivers storage-level resilience, including stretched clusters for site-level failover with synchronous data mirroring, fault domains for rack-level resilience, and automated recovery with vSAN Express Storage Architecture.

VMware Live Site Recovery further implements site-level failover for critical virtual machine workloads through automated recovery plans, supporting planned migrations and disaster recovery. It leverages multiple replication methods, including vSphere Replication, array-based replication, and Virtual Volumes policy-driven replication, with IP customization and inventory mappings applied during failover.

VMware vDefend ensures continuous network security enforcement by operating its Gateway Firewall in a primary-passive high availability mode with flow state synchronization, preserving existing sessions across failover events. The Security Services Platform, hosting advanced threat detection functions, has built-in high availability and can leverage vSphere HA and vSAN stretched clusters for additional resilience. The Distributed Firewall, operating at the hypervisor kernel level, is inherently distributed, ensuring policy enforcement continues across hosts.

While these robust mechanisms are inherent to the platform, customers are responsible for defining their critical Technology Assets, Applications, Services, and Data (TAASD), configuring appropriate failover policies such as RPOs and admission control, and establishing recovery plans to align with their specific availability requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize electronic discovery (eDiscovery) that covers current and archived communication transactions.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to restore Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD) within organization-defined restoration time-periods from configuration-controlled and integrity-protected information; representing a known, operational state for the asset.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide robust technical mechanisms for restoring technology assets, applications, services, and data from configuration-controlled and integrity-protected information to a known, operational state.

VMware Cloud Foundation offers file-based backup and restore for management components such as SDDC Manager, vCenter, VCF Operations, and VCF Operations for Networks, with capabilities like the reconciliation API for state consistency. For workload recovery, vSAN provides snapshot capabilities with configurable retention and scheduling, enhanced by vSAN Express Storage Architecture and vSAN Data Protection for external replication.

VMware Live Recovery further extends these capabilities by providing automated recovery plans for virtual machine workloads, leveraging vSphere Replication, array-based replication, and Virtual Volumes with configurable Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) and multiple point-in-time instances.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform supports backup and restore of its configuration data, including Distributed Firewall policies and Network Detection and Response settings, with configurable scheduling to align with organizational RPOs.

These platforms provide the necessary infrastructure and configurable settings, such as backup scheduling, retention policies, and RPOs, to enable restoration within defined time periods.

While the platforms deliver the technical infrastructure and configurable parameters essential for recovery, the explicit definition of restoration time-period targets, the comprehensive testing of recovery processes against these targets, and the establishment and maintenance of operational runbooks and procedures remain organizational responsibilities.
