Automation network profiles further extend this capability to workloads, allowing for isolation through subnets, external subnets, and security groups, enabling granular control over network segmentation for security tools.

VMware vDefend reinforces these capabilities by providing network segmentation and security enforcement. Its core Security Services Platform, which hosts analytics and threat detection components, is deployed by design into a dedicated subnet, requiring specific IP pools and CIDR assignment. This inherently places security-specific technologies like Security Intelligence, Network Detection and Response, and Malware Prevention on a purpose-built network segment.

vDefend's Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level, creating security boundaries around these dedicated subnets and restricting unauthorized lateral movement. Gateway Firewall provides north-south protection, securing traffic entering and exiting these isolated security zones.

Security Intelligence can be configured to collect data from specific segments, further supporting the use of dedicated subnets for security monitoring. The platform fully provides the technical mechanisms required to host security-specific technologies in dedicated subnets.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure support for secure interoperability between components with Application Programming Interfaces (APIs).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust framework for secure API interoperability through encrypted communications, certificate-based authentication, and standardized authorization models. All management interfaces and API endpoints utilize Transport Layer Security (TLS) encryption, with TLS 1.3 as the default for internal communications. Service-to-service communication within the platform is secured via certificate-based authentication using VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA) issued certificates, while session identifiers establish trust with API endpoints. vCenter provides RESTful APIs with a consistent authorization model, enforcing role-based access control at the API layer, including project-level roles. Modern authentication standards such as OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type are supported via VMware Identity Broker, and API access tokens are available for applications.

VMware Live Recovery further enhances secure API interoperability by employing TLS connections for all communications between Live Site Recovery and vCenter instances, and its site pairing workflow includes certificate handling to establish trusted inter-site API communications. Live Recovery also incorporates a DR REST API Rate Limiter to mitigate risks from API resource exhaustion and brute force attacks, alongside a granular permission model that enforces authorization at the API layer for protection and recovery operations.

VMware vDefend contributes by providing authenticated and authorized API access to configure and manage security services, inheriting the VCF SSO and vCenter authentication and authorization framework. The vDefend API surface uses TLS-encrypted communication patterns and CA bundles to build trust for external service communication, ensuring that security configuration APIs are protected by the same identity and access controls governing the VCF platform, with additional vDefend-specific authorization checks.

API audit logging captures REST API calls, SDK usage, and PowerCLI commands with user attribution across the platform, and tenant isolation is supported for environments requiring strict separation.

While the platform provides extensive mechanisms for secure API interoperability, broader API security concerns such as comprehensive API gateway management, advanced API-specific threat detection, and cross-platform API governance may require the implementation of supplemental API management solutions by the customer.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement an Application Programming Interface (API) Gateway, or similar technology, to serve as a controlled entry point that manages interactions between client-facing requests and backend services.
Response:
VMware vDefend Gateway Firewall establishes a controlled network entry point for north-south traffic, inspecting and monitoring API-related traffic flows. Gateway IDS/IPS capabilities are enabled on tier-0 and tier-1 gateways, applying broad enforcement of inspection policies across all uplink and service interfaces. Malware Prevention on the Gateway Firewall further enhances the inspection of traffic transiting tier-1 gateways, contributing to the security of API traffic by enforcing network entry-point policies and deep packet inspection.

While vDefend provides robust network-layer access control and deep packet inspection, it is not a dedicated API gateway product. It does not provide application-layer API schema validation or authentication, which are critical functions for comprehensive API interaction management. Customers are responsible for implementing a dedicated API gateway solution or other compensating controls to address these application-layer requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure the integrity of virtual machine images at all times.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms that contribute to the integrity of virtual machine images. VCF ensures guest operating system integrity through Unified Extensible Firmware Interface (UEFI) Secure Boot support, allowing virtual machines to boot exclusively with signed and trusted code. Virtual Trusted Platform Module (vTPM) 2.0 further enhances this by providing cryptographic functions for guest operating systems, enabling measured boot and attestation capabilities. VMware Tools packages are cryptographically signed and verified during both installation and subsequent updates. Virtual machine images are managed through vSphere Supervisor content libraries, which are associated with namespaces. Administrators can apply an OVF default security policy to these content libraries, enforcing security controls on all stored virtual machine images. VCF Automation cloud templates standardize and repeat image selection by specifying operating system images for deployment, either hard-coded or derived from user input. For Kubernetes workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) cluster management supports image registry policies that restrict which registries images can be pulled from for deployment within managed namespaces.

While VCF provides these foundational controls, the continuous integrity of virtual machine images at all times requires additional organizational processes and tooling. VCF does not inherently provide continuous at-rest integrity scanning of VM images residing in datastores; this capability necessitates the integration of organizational processes or third-party solutions. Furthermore, organizations are responsible for creating virtual machine images with the latest patches and security settings that adhere to corporate security policies, as recommended images available for download are intended for proof-of-concept use and are not suitable for production. Prior to deploying workloads to production clusters, organizations must also implement security and vulnerability scanning on all images within their private registry.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure multi-tenant owned or managed assets (physical and virtual) are designed and governed such that provider and customer (tenant) user access is appropriately segmented from other tenant users.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for multi-tenant isolation across compute, storage, networking, and management layers.

At the hypervisor level, vSphere segregates virtual machines with dedicated virtual CPU, memory, and storage resources, enforcing isolation through CPU scheduler controls, hyperthreading controls, CPU affinity settings, Transparent Page Sharing controls, and memory reservation policies.

Network segmentation is achieved via vSphere Distributed Switch capabilities like traffic filtering, VLAN tagging, and Private VLANs (PVLANs), which prevent lateral movement within the same VLAN. NSX extends this with Projects, providing isolated security and networking configurations per tenant, and NSX project users can configure objects under their own branch with tenant-level access control.

VCF Automation supports multi-tenancy through organizations, offering infrastructure isolation, resource quotas, and API token management per tenant. Workload domain isolation delivers complete infrastructure separation, complemented by fault domains and DRS VM-VM anti-affinity rules for physical isolation.

For containerized workloads, vSphere Namespaces provide resource-bounded environments with role-based access control, encryption, and network segmentation enforced through NSX integration and PVLANs.

Storage multitenancy is achieved through vSAN storage policies, datastore-level permissions, I/O limits, and cryptographic key managers for tenant separation.

VMware Live Recovery contributes to access segmentation in multi-tenant disaster recovery environments, particularly in shared recovery site configurations. It enables vCenter administrators to manage permissions by assigning separate virtual machine folders, dedicated resource pools, datastores, and networks to each user or organization, restricting permission propagation to prevent unintended access.

VMware vDefend further enhances multi-tenant segmentation through microsegmentation, tenant-aware IDS/IPS, and policy scoping capabilities. The Distributed Firewall enforces security policies at the virtual network interface card (vNIC) level, enabling granular east-west traffic isolation between tenants, with rules scoped using the "Applied To" field for targeted enforcement. vDefend IDS/IPS supports multi-tenancy by isolating security and networking configurations across tenants and logging tenant context for auditability.

Security Services Platform extends these capabilities with segmentation planning tools and VM tags that support tenant identification and policy enforcement.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for multi-tenant segmentation, the control's requirement for assets to be "designed and governed" necessitates active customer involvement.

Customers must define and implement appropriate policies, configure the provided tools (such as NSX Projects, vSphere Namespaces, vDefend firewall rules, and VLR permissions) to align with their specific multi-tenant architecture, and establish processes for ongoing management and auditing of tenant access.

It is important to note that in shared VMware Live Recovery site deployments, complete isolation between all users is not inherently guaranteed, and dedicated infrastructure is required if absolute separation is a strict requirement.

Description: Mechanisms exist to formally document a Customer Responsibility Matrix (CRM), delineating assigned responsibilities for controls between the Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and its customers.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure Multi-Tenant Service Providers (MTSP) facilitate security event logging capabilities for its customers that are consistent with applicable statutory, regulatory and/or contractual obligations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational logging infrastructure for multi-tenant service providers, capturing platform interactions such as authentication events, configuration changes, API calls, and storage or network modifications across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes log collection from various VCF components, including VCF Operations for Networks audit logs and Federation Service logs via VCF Identity Broker, and supports collection of third-party component logs.

NSX Projects and VCF Automation organizations establish tenant-level isolation for security, networking, and management objects, enabling scoped logging and per-tenant tracking of user activity and API token usage. VMware Live Recovery further contributes by generating tenant-attributable audit events for permission changes, recovery plan execution, and replication status, with RBAC ensuring per-user isolation and event attribution. VMware vDefend enhances this by providing network security event generation from Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and Distributed IDS/IPS, logging tenant context for configuration messages and attributing security events to individual tenants. Network Detection and Response (NDR) and Malware Prevention Service logs also capture tenant IDs, allowing for tenant-scoped threat detection and malware event reporting.

The platform supports forwarding these collected and isolated logs to multiple syslog destinations with TLS encryption or to SIEM systems like Splunk, providing the technical foundation for exporting tenant-level audit data.

While the platform delivers robust technical capabilities for multi-tenant security event logging, the responsibility for mapping these logged events to specific statutory, regulatory, or contractual obligations, defining and configuring per-tenant log retention policies, and establishing organizational processes for compliance reporting and log access controls rests with the service provider. These aspects require external systems and operational procedures beyond the native scope of VMware Cloud Foundation components.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure Multi-Tenant Service Providers (MTSP) facilitate prompt forensic investigations in the event of a suspected or confirmed security incident.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide robust technical mechanisms that enable Multi-Tenant Service Providers (MTSP) to facilitate prompt forensic investigations. VCF delivers comprehensive logging, audit, and evidence preservation capabilities essential for incident response.

VCF Operations audit events capture platform interactions, including authentication, configuration changes, and security-related activity, providing critical input for forensic timelines. Centralized log collection from vCenter, VCF Operations for Networks, and other VCF components through VCF Operations for Logs offers a single point for retrieving log-based evidence.

For evidence preservation, VM snapshots capture the memory, disk, and settings state of a virtual machine, with the quiesce option ensuring filesystem consistency. vSAN's Snapshot Service extends this with policy-based scheduling and retention, while vSAN immutable snapshots create tamper-resistant, read-only copies for forensic integrity.

Multi-tenancy is supported through NSX Projects and VCF Automation organizations, which isolate security, networking objects, and ownership tracking, allowing audit trails and log data to be scoped to specific tenant environments without exposing other tenants' data. VCF also supports forwarding tenant-scoped telemetry and audit data to external SIEM or forensic analysis platforms via syslog integration for deeper investigation.

VMware vDefend further enhances these capabilities by providing network-level threat detection, traffic analysis, and event correlation crucial for investigating security incidents within tenant environments. Security Intelligence analyzes network traffic flows, detecting suspicious patterns and classifying them using MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques. This allows investigators to review participating VMs, examine event sequences like command-and-control activity followed by data exfiltration, and trace lateral movement, establishing a network-layer forensic timeline.

The Security Services Platform monitors file inspection activity, including malicious file detections and trends, providing visibility into detected malware and its analysis. It also correlates exfiltration events with preceding infection-type events on the same workload, aiding in attack chain reconstruction.

The Distributed Firewall's audit logging records rule evaluations and connection attempts, offering evidence of network communications during an investigation. The vDefend Auditor role supports separation of duties, allowing forensic personnel to view security information and track activity without modifying configurations.

While VCF and vDefend provide the necessary technical tools and data, the Multi-Tenant Service Provider is responsible for establishing formal forensic investigation procedures, maintaining chain-of-custody documentation, and coordinating with law enforcement as part of their organizational processes.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure Multi-Tenant Service Providers (MTSP) facilitate prompt response to suspected or confirmed security incidents and vulnerabilities, including timely notification to affected customers.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust alerting, monitoring, and integration capabilities essential for multi-tenant service providers to detect and respond to security incidents. VCF Operations generates risk and critical alerts, offering actionable guidance with remediation steps or instructions for administrator notification. Platform operators can configure these alerts, perform detailed log analysis, troubleshoot issues, and run infrastructure diagnostics across the VCF environment.

For streamlined incident management, VCF Operations includes a Management Pack for ServiceNow, which automatically creates ServiceNow incidents from VCF alerts, associating them with configuration items. This integration supports alert propagation, incident state tracking, and update retrieval, effectively routing security incidents into existing ticketing and notification workflows. Furthermore, NSX Projects and VCF Automation organizations ensure tenant-level isolation, allowing incident investigation and response activities to be precisely scoped to the affected tenant without cross-tenant exposure. VCF Automation's ownership tracking links resources to specific organizations and owner identities, aiding in the identification of affected tenants. VCF also supports forwarding logs and alerts to external SIEM platforms via syslog, enabling service providers to integrate VCF telemetry into centralized incident response and orchestration systems.

VMware vDefend further enhances these capabilities by supporting multi-tenant IDS/IPS deployments and providing Intelligent Assist features. These features enable security teams to select and execute targeted or comprehensive remediation strategies in response to detected security incidents, including the ability to create emergency firewall policies for non-IDS/IPS events, thereby accelerating incident response within the vDefend platform across tenant environments.

While VCF and vDefend provide critical technical mechanisms for prompt incident response and identification of affected tenants, the organizational processes for customer notification, incident communication procedures, and vulnerability disclosure timelines fall outside the technical scope of the platforms. Service providers are responsible for defining and implementing these communication workflows to ensure timely notification to affected customers following a security incident.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure cloud providers use secure protocols for the import, export and management of data in cloud-based Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a secure foundation by utilizing TLS-encrypted channels for all management interfaces and API endpoints, including NSX Manager's configurable TLS 1.3 support with strong cipher suites and default deactivation of TLS 1.1. Data movement within the infrastructure is secured through encrypted vSphere vMotion, offering configurable encryption modes for live virtual machine migrations, and vSAN data-in-transit encryption using AES-256 for all inter-host traffic. vSAN File Service further supports NFS4 with Kerberos security options. VCF Operations ensures secure export and import of configuration data, such as outbound proxy settings and key provider data, through encryption requiring passphrases or API-provided passwords, and facilitates secure TLS certificate management.

VMware Live Recovery extends this security to disaster recovery operations, protecting all replication traffic between sites with mandatory TLS 1.2 encryption for newer versions of vSphere Replication, and offering optional activation for earlier versions. Mutual certificate-based authentication secures replication traffic endpoints, and all management communications between VMware Live Site Recovery and vCenter instances occur over TLS connections.

VMware vDefend contributes by securing its own component communications using certificates and robust TLS versions, and critically, by providing network-level enforcement capabilities. Its Security Intelligence identifies inherently insecure protocols like HTTP, Telnet, and FTP, while the vDefend Distributed Firewall can enforce policies to block or restrict their usage, thereby ensuring that only approved secure protocols are permitted for data movement between workloads within the environment.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for secure protocol usage, the customer retains responsibility for configuring and activating certain security features to fully ensure compliance across all Technology Assets, Applications, and Services (TAAS). This includes defining and enforcing specific policies within the vDefend Distributed Firewall to block insecure protocols for workload traffic, selecting appropriate encryption modes for vSphere vMotion, and activating network encryption for vSphere Replication in environments utilizing older versions. The customer must also manage and maintain certificate hygiene for external integrations and ensure consistent certificate management across backup and restored environments.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure interoperability by requiring cloud providers to use industry-recognized formats and provide documentation of custom changes for review.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation and VMware Live Recovery inherently ensure interoperability through their comprehensive adoption of industry-recognized formats and standards across virtualization, container orchestration, identity management, and API definitions. VCF utilizes Open Virtualization Format (OVF) as the standard distribution for virtual applications, supporting `.ovf` and `.ova` files in content libraries and enabling consistent workload deployment. For container orchestration, VMware Kubernetes Service leverages the standard Kubernetes API and supports standard Container Network Interface (CNI) plugins, integrating with NSX via Antrea CNI and NSX Container Plugin. Identity federation is secured through standard protocols such as SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) for integration with external identity providers. VMware Live Recovery and vSphere Replication publish their REST API definitions as OpenAPI specifications in YAML and JSON formats, facilitating consistent interaction with third-party tools. Storage integration relies on VMware APIs for Storage Awareness (VASA), a standard interface that enables VLR to operate across diverse vendor storage platforms.

VCF Operations provides robust mechanisms for documenting custom changes by tracking configuration drift across vCenter instances and vSphere clusters, comparing current settings against desired-state templates. These configuration drift templates can be integrated with source control repositories for versioning and audit, generating detailed drift reports that show historical changes by date, resource, and template. Administrators can trace specific configuration changes back to individual users and timestamps. However, the organizational process for reviewing and approving these documented changes remains outside the scope of the platform and is a customer responsibility.

Description: Mechanisms exist to control the location of cloud processing/storage based on business requirements that includes statutory, regulatory and contractual obligations.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to limit and manage the storage of sensitive/regulated data in public cloud providers.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize a Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB), or similar technology, to provide boundary protection and monitoring functions that both provide access to the cloud and protect the organization from misuse of cloud resources.
Response:
The Security Services Platform (SSP) within a VCF environment provides boundary protection functions. This includes a web proxy component, backed by Envoy as a reverse-proxy server, and certificate authority management for TLS inspection of web traffic. These capabilities enable web traffic visibility and policy enforcement at the network layer.

The platform does not function as a Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB) and does not broker access to external cloud services. It does not enforce shadow IT policies or provide cloud service usage controls.

The SSP capabilities do not replace the comprehensive SaaS visibility, Data Loss Prevention (DLP), and cloud governance functions that a dedicated CASB solution provides. Organizations must implement a dedicated CASB to fully address the requirements for brokering access to external cloud services, enforcing shadow IT policies, and providing comprehensive cloud service usage controls, SaaS visibility, DLP, and cloud governance.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prevent "side channel attacks" when using a Content Delivery Network (CDN) by restricting access to the origin server's IP address to the CDN and an authorized management network.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX-T, provides robust network virtualization and security capabilities essential for implementing granular access controls.

The Distributed Firewall (DFW) functionality allows for the creation of precise security policies that restrict inbound network traffic to virtual machines hosting origin servers. These policies can be configured to permit access exclusively from specified Content Delivery Network (CDN) IP ranges and designated authorized management networks, thereby isolating the origin server from unauthorized external access and mitigating side-channel attack vectors.

The customer is responsible for identifying and maintaining an accurate list of IP addresses or ranges for their chosen CDN and authorized management networks.

They must then define, implement, and continuously manage the corresponding DFW rules within NSX-T to enforce these access restrictions. This includes ensuring that policy updates are applied promptly as CDN configurations or management network details change.

Description: Mechanisms exist to specify applicable cybersecurity and data protection controls that must be implemented on external Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS), consistent with the contractual obligations established with the External Service Providers (ESP) owning, operating and/or maintaining external TAAS.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to authorize specified individuals to access External Service Providers (ESP) owned, operated and/or maintained external Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define formal processes to store, process and/or transmit sensitive/regulated data using External Service Providers (ESP) owned, operated and/or maintained external Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS), in accordance with all applicable statutory, regulatory and/or contractual obligations.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prohibit access to, or usage of, hosted Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) until applicable cybersecurity and data protection control implementation is verified.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust access control and policy frameworks that prohibit access to hosted assets until control implementation is verified. Granular permissions are enforced through role-based access control (RBAC) across all platform components, with VCF SSO providing centralized authentication and the CloudAdmin role model restricting platform-level operations. For Kubernetes workloads, vSphere Namespaces support permission roles, allowing access to be withheld until verification is complete. VCF Automation extends this with IaaS Resource Policies that can restrict virtual machine provisioning based on validation expressions, preventing resource creation when specific conditions are not met. Rights bundles can also be unpublished from organizations to restrict access to service instances and capabilities until readiness is confirmed.

VMware vDefend further contributes by enforcing a default-deny firewall posture and a structured segmentation planning workflow. The vDefend Distributed Firewall blocks traffic between workloads unless explicitly permitted, meaning newly deployed assets are prohibited from communicating until an administrator configures and verifies appropriate security policies. The Security Services Platform facilitates this verification through a structured segmentation planning workflow where application assets are classified, monitored for unprotected flows, and policies are refined. This allows administrators to verify security controls before transitioning from monitoring to enforcement. Security Intelligence supports this with flow visualization and segmentation monitoring, enabling lockdown rules to restrict access to assets not formally onboarded and verified, tracking their status throughout the process.

While these platforms provide the technical enforcement mechanisms, the organizational policy or process that defines what constitutes "verified" cybersecurity and data protection control implementation, and subsequently triggers access permission changes or network policy enforcement, remains a customer governance responsibility. The broader identity and access management decisions about who may access hosted services are also outside the direct scope of these technical platforms.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to utilize Software Defined Storage (SDS) to scale access management permissions to Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) leverages VMware vSAN as its Software Defined Storage (SDS solution, providing an object-based, enterprise-grade distributed storage system. This foundation enables policy-based storage management through Storage Policy Based Management (SPBM), where granular storage policies are assigned per virtual machine. These policies programmatically control critical access-related characteristics such as encryption, availability levels, redundancy (e.g., RAID type, failures to tolerate), performance characteristics (e.g., disk stripes, flash read cache, IOPS limits), and data reduction settings (compression, deduplication). SPBM offers a programmatic interface, allowing administrators to define multiple policies to meet diverse requirements and apply them at scale to virtual machines deployed on vSAN datastores, thereby scaling the application of storage-level access controls to Technology Assets, Applications, Services, and Data (TAASD).

Storage multitenancy is inherently supported through the application of vSAN storage policies per tenant, coupled with datastore permissions and I/O limits, ensuring robust storage isolation. VCF further enhances cryptographic isolation between tenants by supporting different cryptographic key managers for distinct folders, clusters, and workloads. Access management across the entire VCF platform is enforced through a comprehensive Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system. SDDC Manager implements granular permissions for managing datastores, storage resources, and associated infrastructure. VCF Automation extends these capabilities by providing storage profiles that expose storage capabilities for self-service provisioning, all while maintaining strict role-based controls over who can provision and consume these SDS resources.

Description: Mechanisms exist to facilitate the identification and implementation of relevant statutory, regulatory and contractual controls.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to document and review instances of non-compliance with statutory, regulatory and/or contractual obligations to develop appropriate risk mitigation actions.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to document and validate the scope of cybersecurity and data protection controls that are determined to meet statutory, regulatory and/or contractual compliance obligations.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure the organization is able to demonstrate conformity with applicable cybersecurity and data protection laws, regulations and/or contractual obligations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation includes a compliance management engine that continuously monitors infrastructure against security benchmarks, such as DISA STIG, calculating compliance scores, flagging violations, and providing remediation recommendations. It supports automated security posture assessment and remediation, generating both point-in-time and ongoing evidence for auditors.

VCF components generate detailed logs of security-relevant events, including authentication attempts, configuration changes, and administrative actions, which are forwarded to external SIEM systems for centralized retention and evidence preservation. Pre-built content packs offer dashboards and alerts for common compliance use cases like failed login tracking and policy violation detection.

VMware Live Recovery maintains recovery plan history reports, providing evidence of DR testing and execution, and supports event logging for protection group changes and operational activities, creating an audit trail of DR-related administrative actions. Its advanced features, such as the Latest instances option for ransomware recovery and sub-five-minute RPO settings, are specifically designed for regulated environments.

VMware vDefend contributes by providing auditable records of security policy configuration and enforcement, with its Rule Analysis feature verifying alignment between implemented rules and documented security requirements. Role-based access controls, including dedicated Auditor roles, facilitate segregation of duties and evidence collection during compliance assessments.

These integrated capabilities collectively produce the necessary artifacts and evidence for demonstrating adherence to technical cybersecurity and data protection controls.

However, the determination of applicable laws, regulations, and contractual obligations, the mapping of controls to specific requirements, and the overall management of the compliance program remain organizational responsibilities. The platform provides the technical mechanisms for evidence generation and posture monitoring, but the establishment of broader governance, documentation, and audit processes falls outside the product's scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to conduct assessments to demonstrate conformity with applicable cybersecurity and data protection laws, regulations and/or contractual obligations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides automated compliance scanning and remediation capabilities, enabling continuous evaluation of the infrastructure against defined security baselines such as DISA STIG. It calculates a compliance score for each object based on the ratio of compliant to total assessed symptoms, highlighting critical violations and offering remediation recommendations.

The platform's compliance check API supports both scheduled and on-demand conformity assessments, returning per-host status and notifications. VCF's security baseline serves as a reference configuration for evaluating both default and customized settings, allowing administrators to automate assessment and remediation workflows through product interfaces, command-line tools, and APIs, thereby reducing manual effort in preparing for and responding to compliance assessments. These capabilities facilitate repeatable, evidence-based conformity assessments of the VCF infrastructure.

VMware vDefend further contributes to conformity assessments through Security Services Platform's Security Segmentation scoring and assessment capabilities. This provides measurable, evidence-based evaluation of an organization's network microsegmentation posture by calculating a Security Segmentation score on a 100-point weighted scale across five categories. The scoring system supports both Relaxed and Strict modes, allowing organizations to tailor assessment rigor.

Environment Protection assessment evaluates workload protection by environment-specific segmentation policies, measuring the percentage of environment pairs operating in blocked mode and the percentage of all computes covered by at least one environment rule. The Inter-Environment Pair Protection Rule Action Summary report offers tabular analysis suitable for audit evidence. Additionally, Rule Analysis provides scheduled or on-demand analysis of the complete vDefend Distributed Firewall configuration, detecting contradictions and identifying shadowed rules to demonstrate policy consistency, which is a common audit requirement for network security controls.

While VMware Cloud Foundation and VMware vDefend provide essential technical mechanisms for conducting infrastructure and network security conformity assessments, the broader compliance assessment program remains an organizational responsibility. This includes determining which specific laws, regulations, or contractual obligations apply, selecting appropriate assessment frameworks, engaging external assessors, and evaluating controls beyond the technical scope of these platforms.
End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to ensure individuals performing audits and/or assessments have reasonable:
 (1) Professional qualifications to perform the audit and/or assessment; and
 (2) Subject matter expertise to perform review, interview and test activities for in-scope People, Processes, Technologies, Data and/or Facilities (PPTDF).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to provide a cybersecurity and data protection controls oversight function that reports to the organization's executive leadership.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement an internal audit function that is capable of providing senior organization management with insights into the appropriateness of the organization's technology and information governance processes.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to conduct periodic audits of cybersecurity and data protection controls to evaluate conformity with the organization's documented policies, standards and procedures.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to take corrective action to remediate instances of non-conformity with applicable statutory, regulatory, and/or contractual compliance obligations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust technical mechanisms for identifying and remediating non-conformities within the managed infrastructure. VCF Operations includes continuous compliance management, monitoring infrastructure against industry standards and custom policies. It detects violations, highlights risk areas, and offers actionable remediation recommendations, allowing administrators to take direct remedial action from the Security > Compliance page. Automated remediation actions can be configured to bring objects back into compliance without manual intervention for more severe non-compliance.

On the host layer, vSphere Configuration Profiles and vSphere Lifecycle Manager enforce desired-state configuration for ESX hosts, detecting and automatically remediating configuration drift to restore conformity with standards such as the DISA STIG. NSX also provides comprehensive compliance status reporting, detailing non-compliant configurations by description, standard violated, affected resource, and resource type.

While VCF addresses technical configuration conformity within its managed infrastructure, the broader process of managing statutory, regulatory, and contractual compliance obligations necessitates organizational governance and processes. Corrective actions for these wider obligations fall outside the direct scope of VCF's technical capabilities and require customer-defined policies and procedures.

Description: Mechanisms exist to regularly review processes and documented procedures to ensure conformity with the organization's cybersecurity and data protection policies, standards and other applicable requirements.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize independent assessors to evaluate cybersecurity and data protection controls at planned intervals or when the Technology Asset, Application and/or Service (TAAS) undergoes significant changes.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to regularly review Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) for adherence to the organization's cybersecurity and data protection policies and standards.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for the regular review of technology assets, applications, and services against defined cybersecurity and data protection policies. VCF Operations includes comprehensive compliance management capabilities that continuously monitor the infrastructure, assessing adherence to industry-standard security benchmarks and custom policies. It calculates compliance scores for managed objects, highlighting violations and enabling administrators to take remedial action directly from the Security > Compliance page.

Organizations can codify their specific cybersecurity and data protection policies into custom compliance benchmarks, ensuring consistent enforcement across multiple VCF deployments.

At the host layer, vSphere Configuration Profiles and vSphere Lifecycle Manager continuously assess ESX host configurations against approved baselines, detecting and optionally remediating drift. NSX further contributes with its own compliance status reporting, identifying non-compliant network configurations.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities by providing specific mechanisms for the functional review of network security controls.

The Security Services Platform offers a Security Segmentation score and report generation, enabling compliance officers to quantitatively assess and demonstrate adherence to network security best practices. Its metrics service collects key performance indicators and operational data from Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, Intrusion Detection and Prevention System, and TLS inspection, forming the basis for ongoing compliance reviews.

Security Intelligence provides policy recommendation analysis, comparing desired rules against current NSX inventory to identify and address gaps in network security policy. The Threat Event Monitoring dashboard offers a consolidated view of detected threats and policy violations, creating an auditable trail.

Furthermore, the Security Services Platform includes a dedicated Auditor role, facilitating secure access for compliance teams to perform reviews without requiring administrative privileges.

While the platform provides extensive technical mechanisms for continuous configuration-state review and specific functional review for network security, the organization remains responsible for defining its comprehensive cybersecurity and data protection policies and standards. The creation of custom compliance benchmarks within VCF Operations requires organizational input to codify these policies.

Additionally, the platform's configuration-state reviews do not encompass functional testing of all security controls across all technology assets. Organizations must supplement these capabilities with periodic functional validation, such as security assessments or controlled test scenarios, to verify that all security controls are actively operating as intended beyond their configured state.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to grant assessors minimum necessary access to conduct conformity assessments, including:
 (1) Logical access to design, development, production, inspection and testing artifacts; and 
 (2) Physical access to facilities.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide robust technical mechanisms to provision assessors with minimum necessary logical access for conformity assessments.

The platforms leverage comprehensive role-based access control (RBAC) frameworks, offering predefined roles such as Project Auditor, Viewer, and dedicated Auditor roles with read-only permissions across various operations. Administrators can define custom roles with granular permissions, scoping access to specific objects, operations, and data relevant to the assessment scope, such as read-only visibility into protection group configurations, recovery plan definitions, or security policies and flow data.

Identity federation support simplifies assessor account lifecycle management by integrating with existing identity providers, ensuring credentials are deprovisioned efficiently.

Furthermore, these solutions generate audit logs for user activity and permission changes, providing a verifiable record of assessor access provisioning and actions during an engagement.

While the platforms deliver sophisticated controls for logical access, the organization retains the responsibility for defining and enforcing the overarching access control policies and procedures. This includes establishing the criteria for minimum necessary access, managing the lifecycle of assessor accounts, and ensuring that physical access to facilities, which is outside the scope of these technical solutions, is appropriately controlled and monitored through separate organizational processes and physical security measures.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define acceptable methods to conduct a cybersecurity and/or data protection assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define the level of assessment rigor necessary to conduct a cybersecurity and/or data protection assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define an Evidence Request List (ERL) prior to the start of a cybersecurity and/or data protection assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define evidence sampling criteria for cybersecurity and/or data protection assessments.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to thoughtfully plan audits by including input from operational risk and compliance partners to minimize the impact of audit-related activities on business operations.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to determine whether a government agency has an applicable and valid legal basis to request data from the organization and what further steps need to be taken, if necessary.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to notify customers about investigation request notifications, unless the applicable legal basis for a government agency's action prohibits notification (e.g., potential criminal prosecution).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to support official investigations by provisioning government investigators with "least privileges" and "least functionality" to ensure that government investigators only have access to the Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD) needed to perform the investigation.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust role-based access control framework that enables the creation of custom roles with granularly selected permissions, ensuring investigators are provisioned with least privilege and least functionality. This allows restricting access to only the specific systems, data, and operations required for an investigation, exemplified by VCF Automation's Project Auditor role providing read-only access. Identity federation centralizes investigator account lifecycle management, supporting time-bounded access policies through the organization's identity provider. VCF Operations further enhances this by allowing per-permission role construction and enforcing privilege-based data access controls, while user activity auditing records investigator actions, providing an immutable evidence trail exportable to external syslog.

Similarly, VMware Live Recovery's Live Site Recovery and vSphere Replication offer RBAC frameworks with predefined and customizable roles, allowing precise scoping of access to disaster recovery operations. Permission checks are enforced on individual operations, and audit logs record permission creation and modification events. VMware vDefend also contributes with its RBAC framework, enabling least-privilege access to network security data and policy artifacts, with roles like the Auditor providing read-only visibility.

While the platform provides the essential technical mechanisms for granular access control and auditing, the organization remains responsible for defining the specific scope of investigator access, establishing the procedures for provisioning and deprovisioning accounts, and managing the overall investigation process in coordination with legal or regulatory authorities. Tailoring least-functionality access for external government investigators requires organizational processes and identity management controls beyond the native built-in roles.

Description: Mechanisms exist to constrain the host government from having unrestricted and non-monitored access to the organization's Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD) that could potentially violate other applicable statutory, regulatory and/or contractual obligations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical mechanisms to constrain and monitor access to infrastructure resources.

Granular role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced across vCenter, SDDC Manager, and NSX, enabling the provisioning of external users with highly restricted custom roles based on least-privilege principles. VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) further ensures policy hierarchy enforcement, preventing project-level administrators from overriding organization-level security policies.

All platform interactions are subject to comprehensive audit logging through VCF Operations, capturing user logins, configuration modifications, and administrative actions across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources. ESX hosts generate independent audit records for authentication attempts and configuration changes, with native syslog forwarding supporting integration with external SIEM systems for centralized log collection, correlation, and long-term retention. Login banners can be configured to provide legal and procedural notice regarding system access conditions and monitoring.

VMware vDefend further contributes by providing technical controls that limit and monitor network access within the environment. Its Security Intelligence capabilities detect and report suspicious traffic events, categorizing them by detector technique and tactic, and tracking detected versus prevented events to assess control effectiveness.

While these technical controls provide essential access restriction, monitoring, and audit trail capabilities, the organization retains responsibility for defining what constitutes "government surveillance," establishing legal review processes, and enforcing specific contractual obligations. Addressing sovereign government access obligations or jurisdictional legal frameworks remains a matter of organizational policy, legal counsel, and contractual agreements.

Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to contract localized representation to perform specified functions in regard to representing statutory and/or regulatory compliance matters.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define instances of control reciprocity within assessment boundaries.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define instances of control inheritance within assessment boundaries.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to govern technologies and/or data that have potential:
 (1) "Dual-use” capabilities for civil and military;
 (2) Use by terrorists; and/or 
 (3) Weapons of Mass Destruction (WMD) applications.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to identify if the organization handles United States Munitions List (USML) or Commerce Control List (CCL):
 (1) Items;
 (2) Technical data; and/or
 (3) Provides defense services.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to restrict logical and physical access to United States (US) export-controlled data to US: 
 (1) Citizens; and/or
 (2) Green Card holders.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to generate detailed logs of export-controlled data including:
 (1) Logical and physical access; and
 (2) Export activities.
Response:
VMware vDefend's Security Intelligence and Security Services Platform offers capabilities for exporting operational data, including CSV and JSON formats for flow data, security policy rules, groups, services, and service entries. These functions are designed to support security analysis and provide operational insights within the environment.

However, vDefend does not generate logs specifically for export-controlled data in a regulatory context, such as ITAR or EAR export activities. It also does not track logical or physical access to controlled technical data, nor does it provide audit logs for export-controlled information transactions.

Organizations subject to export control regulations are therefore responsible for implementing dedicated data classification, access logging, and export tracking controls outside of vDefend to meet the specific requirements of this control.

Description: Mechanisms exist to produce a Statement of Applicability (SOA), or similar document, for compliance-related scoping activities.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to facilitate the implementation of configuration management controls.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for configuration management, including Configuration Templates to define and enforce desired states, integrating with source control for versioning, auditing, and change control, and supporting automated testing and validation. Configuration Drifts Templates actively track deviations from the defined desired state, enabling timely review and remediation. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) ensures consistency for ESX hosts through image-based lifecycle management and Configuration Profiles. VCF Automation extends these capabilities by integrating with external tools like Ansible and Puppet for automated workload configuration enforcement.

VMware Live Recovery offers specific mechanisms for disaster recovery configurations, such as the Configuration Import/Export Tool for versioning and backup of recovery plans and inventory, and the DR IP Customizer for structured network configuration management during recovery. VMware vDefend facilitates configuration management for network security policies through draft configuration workflows, allowing for review and validation before publication, and a federation architecture that centralizes global policy management while permitting local customization. Security Services Platform further enhances this with deployment validation and configuration synchronization metrics, while Security Intelligence maintains application context in a CMDB for segmentation planning.

While the platform provides extensive technical mechanisms to facilitate configuration management, the organization remains responsible for defining and implementing the overarching configuration management controls. This includes establishing formal policies, procedures, and roles for configuration definition, change control, drift remediation, and auditing. The customer must also integrate these platform capabilities into their broader operational workflows, including defining approval processes for configuration changes, managing source control repositories, and ensuring that personnel are adequately trained to utilize these mechanisms effectively.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement a segregation of duties for configuration management that prevents developers from performing production configuration management duties.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements segregation of duties for configuration management through robust role-based access control (RBAC) across all platform components, including vCenter, VCF Automation, and VCF Operations. Roles, as fundamental units of access control, associate names with specific rights and are assigned to users and groups, enabling precise control over actions and objects. This role model inherently separates development and production responsibilities, allowing administrators to restrict modifications to production configurations. VCF SSO centralizes authentication and authorization, ensuring all configuration changes adhere to this role-based access model and preventing direct host access.

VMware Live Recovery further supports this by implementing RBAC for disaster recovery configuration management. VMware Live Site Recovery defines roles with collections of privileges, including predefined roles like SrmAdministrator and SrmProtectionGroupsAdministrator, which separate administrative access levels. vSphere Replication offers customizable roles, allowing administrators to clone and modify roles to enforce least-privilege access for replication infrastructure management.

VMware vDefend contributes to segregation of duties for firewall and security policy configuration. Its Distributed Firewall supports draft configuration management, separating the proposal of changes from their approval and publication. In federated environments, the Global Manager enforces global configurations while delegating site-specific adjustments to Local Managers, reinforcing role separation. Integration with external LDAP identity providers aligns vDefend administrative access with existing directory-based role structures, preventing developer accounts from gaining production firewall administration privileges. Additionally, vDefend can enforce network-level separation between development and production environments through block policies and Security Intelligence monitoring.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms, customers are responsible for defining and implementing specific organizational policies, establishing appropriate role assignments, and configuring the RBAC models to align with their unique segregation of duties requirements. This includes creating custom roles, assigning users and groups to these roles, and establishing the workflows for draft configuration review and approval processes to ensure that developers are explicitly prevented from performing production configuration management duties.

Description: Mechanisms exist to develop, document and maintain secure baseline configurations for Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) that are consistent with industry-accepted system hardening standards.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms to develop, document, and maintain secure baseline configurations for Technology Assets, Applications, and Services, aligning with industry-accepted system hardening standards. The VCF Security Baseline is a consolidated, validated document that builds on VCF security fundamentals, addressing frequently requested compliance standards and frameworks. It is designed to tailor security configurations without impacting VCF's design objectives and supports evaluating both default and non-default configurations. Broadcom further provides DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) and STIG Readiness Guides for VCF components, alongside detailed hardening guidance for major components such as vCenter and NSX, covering installation, access, authentication, roles, permissions, auditing, and logging.

VCF Operations includes compliance scanning capabilities that evaluate infrastructure against these baseline configurations, ensuring consistency across SDDC components, with VCF 9 introducing a new consolidated out-of-the-box benchmark. VMware Live Recovery contributes by offering configurable security baseline settings for its replication infrastructure, including TLS configuration parameters and documented network encryption requirements for replication traffic. VMware vDefend further enhances this by providing structured configuration management capabilities within its network security domain, including draft configuration management with locking and comment requirements, predefined TLS Decryption Action Profiles (e.g., Balanced, High Security), and pre-deployment validation through a deployment wizard to prevent configuration drift. The Security Services Platform also supports backup and restore of configuration data and offers a Security Assessment capability to evaluate workload security posture against configurable baseline requirements, recording issues with CVSS scores and CVE identifiers.

While VMware Cloud Foundation delivers these robust, integrated mechanisms for developing, documenting, and maintaining secure baselines, customers are responsible for operationalizing these capabilities, defining specific organizational policies that leverage the provided baselines and hardening guidance, and ensuring their consistent implementation and adherence across their deployed infrastructure.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to review and update baseline configurations:
 (1) At least annually;
 (2) When required due to so; or
 (3) As part of system component installations and upgrades.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive mechanisms to review and update baseline configurations across its integrated platform. VCF's lifecycle management orchestrates updates for all platform components, ensuring configurations are reviewed and updated as part of system component installations and upgrades. This includes automatic post-upgrade reconciliation of configuration drift fixes and administrator review of changes in upgraded alert definitions, symptom definitions, recommendations, and views.

VCF Operations further offers compliance scanning capabilities, enabling ongoing evaluation of infrastructure against baseline configurations, such as the VCF 9 Security Baseline. These tools facilitate regular reviews and identification of configuration drift between scheduled update cycles, supporting periodic and event-driven assessments.

VMware vDefend enhances these capabilities by providing Security Intelligence Policy Recommendations, which analyze observed traffic to generate and refresh firewall rule recommendations. These recommendations can be reviewed, modified, and published, with the ability to be rerun to detect changes, thereby supporting periodic and change-triggered review cycles for segmentation policy baselines.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms and tools for configuration review and update, the customer is responsible for defining the specific policies, processes, and human accountability required to fully implement and operationalize these capabilities. This includes establishing the frequency of reviews (e.g., annually), defining the triggers for "when required," and ensuring administrators actively review and approve changes identified by the platform's tools. The customer must also define their specific baseline configurations and hardening standards beyond the provided benchmarks, and integrate the platform's outputs into their overall configuration governance program.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to govern and report on baseline configurations of Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) through Continuous Diagnostics and Mitigation (CDM), or similar technologies.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust automated mechanisms for governing and reporting on baseline configurations of technology assets. VCF Operations' Configuration Drifts feature defines desired-state configuration templates, continuously monitors vCenter instances and vSphere clusters for deviations, and generates notifications for unauthorized or unintended changes. The Configuration Drift dashboard offers a consolidated view, with downloadable reports for evidence collection. Configuration templates integrate with source control repositories, enabling versioned, auditable management of configuration baselines and supporting change control workflows.

VCF also includes built-in compliance benchmarks based on the VCF Security Baseline, Security Configuration Guide, and DISA STIGs, continuously monitoring infrastructure objects and providing actionable remediation recommendations. At the host level, vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) defines desired software state images for ESX clusters and performs compliance checks, while SDDC Manager supports numerous simultaneous cluster compliance checks. VCF Operations further enhances this with alert definitions based on operational metrics, performance, and configuration properties, complete with associated remediation guidance.

VMware vDefend contributes to automated baseline configuration management through Security Intelligence's automated infrastructure classification, which infers workload classifications for review. The Security Services Platform validates system-generated groups, auto-tagging of workloads, and Distributed Firewall rules, offering an infrastructure monitoring page for auto-detected services. In federated environments, a centralized Global Manager streamlines IDS/IPS configurations and security policies across multiple data centers, providing automated discovery and policy baseline generation.

While these platforms deliver comprehensive automated capabilities for infrastructure and network security baseline governance and reporting, organizations remain responsible for defining the specific desired-state configuration templates, compliance benchmarks, and security policies that align with their unique operational and security requirements. This includes establishing processes for reviewing and accepting inferred classifications, acting upon drift notifications, and implementing remediation recommendations to maintain a secure and compliant posture across all Technology Assets, Applications, and Services.

Description: Mechanisms exist to retain previous versions of baseline configuration to support roll back.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for retaining previous versions of baseline configurations across its components, enabling robust rollback capabilities. VCF Operations maintains detailed version history for orchestrator objects, including workflows and configuration templates, allowing administrators to track changes, audit modifications, and restore objects to any prior state. Configuration Drifts templates integrate with source control repositories, providing full version control and change review workflows for configuration baselines. VCF Automation blueprints also support version history tracking, facilitating review and reversion to earlier infrastructure definitions. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages cluster images through base images, firmware, and vendor add-ons, with fixed patch baselines preserving specific known-good configurations for rollback. Furthermore, file-based backup and restore capabilities are available for SDDC Manager, VMware vCenter, and NSX, capturing the configuration state for restoration. VM-level snapshots offer an additional layer of point-in-time state capture for rollback.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities by providing a Configuration Import/Export Tool that captures the full disaster recovery configuration into XML files, serving as point-in-time baselines. These exports can be performed via UI, script, or REST API, and can be embedded as a recovery plan step for automated capture. VLR supports scheduled configuration exports with configurable retention policies, providing a rolling history of configuration baselines. Administrators can utilize the import capability to restore from any previously exported configuration file.

VMware vDefend also retains previous versions of its security policy configurations. The Distributed Firewall supports a draft-and-publish workflow, preserving prior published configurations for rollback. IDS/IPS signature management includes rollback capabilities for signature bundles, allowing reversion to existing sets. The Security Services Platform supports backup and restore of its configuration data, ensuring that configuration changes made after a backup are overwritten during restoration, effectively rolling back to the backed-up state. These integrated features ensure that previous configuration states are consistently available for recovery and audit across the platform.

Description: Mechanisms exist to manage baseline configurations for development and test environments separately from operational baseline configurations to minimize the risk of unintentional changes.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for managing separate baseline configurations across development, test, and production environments. VCF workload domains offer infrastructure-level separation, enabling distinct domains with independent configurations, access policies, and lifecycle operations. NSX network segments and security zones further support network segregation between these environments. Host profiles in vCenter allow administrators to define and consistently apply standard configurations across hosts within each environment, facilitating the maintenance of distinct baselines.

VCF Operations integrates with source control repositories for configuration drift templates, supporting change control with reviews and automated testing and validation of configuration updates before they reach production. Automation capabilities, including constraint tags and cloud zone capability tags, ensure deployment templates target the correct environment, complemented by a Test Configuration capability that runs hypothetical deployments against project resources.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) contributes to managing separate baseline configurations for test and production environments through its disaster recovery testing framework, which is built around explicit test and production isolation. VLR provides dedicated test network configurations that are isolated from production networks on the recovery site during recovery plan tests, preventing interference with operational environments. Recovery plan testing makes no changes to the production environment, allowing organizations to validate DR configurations and test failover procedures without risk of unintentional changes to operational baselines.

VMware vDefend contributes by enabling environment isolation through its Distributed Firewall, segmenting development, staging, and production environments into distinct security zones. Firewall rules can be scoped to specific environments, reducing cross-environment contamination and preserving operational integrity, with each environment maintaining its own set of security policies. Security Intelligence Segmentation Planning supports defining an Environments hierarchy, and Environment Security scoring evaluates inter-environment policy coverage, highlighting areas lacking proper isolation. The Security Services Platform deployment wizard validates configurations before implementation, reducing the risk of misconfiguration errors during environment setup.

Organizations remain responsible for defining the specific environment-specific baselines themselves and for establishing and enforcing the change management processes that govern how configurations, including infrastructure, DR, and security policies, are promoted or moved between development, test, and production environments. While vDefend enforces network security separation, it does not manage non-network configuration baselines such as host profiles, application settings, or deployment templates.

Description: Mechanisms exist to configure Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) utilized in high-risk areas with more restrictive baseline configurations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for applying more restrictive baseline configurations to technology assets in high-risk areas. This includes the VCF Security Baseline, validated reference architectures, and detailed DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) for securing operating systems, applications, and network devices.

Individual component Security Configuration Guides offer hardening recommendations for vSphere, NSX, and vSAN.

VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure against these baselines and custom policies, identifying non-compliant objects and providing actionable remediation, including automated actions.

Critical Production policy types and Conservative risk level settings are available for production and mission-critical workloads, allowing stricter operational thresholds. Workload domains further enable infrastructure-level isolation for high-risk workloads, each with independent configuration baselines and access policies.

VMware vDefend enhances these capabilities, particularly at the network security layer.

The Security Services Platform facilitates the classification of workloads into security zones such as Production, DMZ, and PCI, enabling the application of differentiated security policies.

The Distributed Firewall enforces security policy at the vNIC level, supporting group-based and tag-based policy application, allowing for more restrictive allow-lists and microsegmentation for high-risk workloads.

Security Intelligence provides traffic flow analysis and visualization, identifying at-risk workloads for targeted restrictive firewall configurations.

IDS/IPS profiles can be applied with maximum sensitivity to high-risk zones, and Gateway Firewall rules can be differentiated to apply stricter north-south traffic controls to segments hosting sensitive workloads.

The platform provides all necessary technical mechanisms to define, apply, monitor, and enforce more restrictive baseline configurations for assets identified as high-risk.

The customer is responsible for identifying and classifying their specific high-risk areas and then utilizing these inherent platform capabilities to implement and maintain the appropriate restrictive configurations and policies.

Description: Mechanisms exist to configure network devices to synchronize startup and running configuration files.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for synchronizing startup and running configurations within its virtual networking domain.

NSX maintains a centralized configuration state for virtual switches, segments, and transport nodes. Transport Node Profiles ensure consistent application of desired network configurations across hosts, including automatic reapplication after reboots. For stateless hosts deployed via vSphere Auto Deploy, the network state is rebuilt from stored configuration profiles (image, host, NSX) upon power-on. Stateful hosts benefit from versioned configurations managed by NSX and vSphere Distributed Switches, which are reapplied when hosts rejoin the management plane. The NSX API includes a ConfigurationState object, allowing administrators to verify the full realization of configuration changes across the infrastructure. Furthermore, vSphere Lifecycle Manager and Configuration Profiles actively detect and remediate any configuration drift from the defined baseline.

VCF's scope does not extend to managing configuration synchronization for physical network devices, such as top-of-rack switches or routers. Customers are responsible for implementing and managing external network management tools to ensure startup and running configuration synchronization for their physical network infrastructure.

Description: Mechanisms exist to document, assess risk and approve or deny deviations to standardized configurations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for detecting and documenting deviations from standardized configurations. VCF Operations monitors infrastructure objects against compliance benchmarks and configuration baselines, generating configuration drift notifications that detail specific changes, their timing, and the current state versus desired values. Compliance scoring prioritizes critical violated standards. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) complements this by generating specific events for replication configuration changes, such as VmReplicationConfigurationChangedEvent, and offers reports and diagnostics to capture the current state for review. VMware vDefend further enhances these capabilities with pre-implementation validation through the Security Services Platform (SSP) deployment wizard, identifying potential deviations before they take effect.
