VCF Automation maintains its own audit log, accessible via the Infrastructure tab, capturing create, delete, and update operations, blueprint deployments, and Day 2 actions, with capabilities for filtering, searching, and CSV export.

Similarly, VCF Operations for Networks provides exportable audit log data. These component-level audit capabilities are designed to feed into the broader VCF Operations monitoring platform, offering a consolidated view across infrastructure, automation, and network layers.

For external integration, VCF supports syslog forwarding to up to three targets with TLS encryption.

VMware vDefend significantly contributes to the audit record corpus, generating and managing multiple categories of audit-relevant records that feed into centralized collection and analysis platforms, while also providing its own domain-specific centralized analysis capabilities for network security events.

The vDefend Distributed Firewall supports configurable rule logging, forwarding detailed connection logs (including rule ID, source/destination, ports, protocol, and custom tags) via syslog to centralized log management platforms like VCF Operations for Logs.

Security Intelligence offers centralized analysis of network traffic flows, identifying unprotected flows, generating policy recommendations, and producing downloadable reports, providing a consolidated view of network security posture across multiple segments.

The Security Services Platform Rule Analysis feature generates downloadable reports on firewall rule anomalies, and Security Segmentation scoring and reporting capabilities aid in demonstrating compliance.

Furthermore, vDefend IDS/IPS event correlation produces structured event records with signature metadata, and Malware Prevention analysis reports contribute to the auditable data, with Security Intelligence tracking affected workloads and maintaining full event history for comprehensive historical analysis.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to integrate the analysis of audit records with analysis of vulnerability scanners, network performance, system monitoring and other sources to further enhance the ability to identify inappropriate or unusual activity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides automated mechanisms to integrate audit record analysis with scanning, performance monitoring, and other data sources across the infrastructure stack. VCF Operations serves as the central integration point, collecting and correlating data from multiple sources including vCenter events, ESX host logs, vSAN performance metrics, NSX network configuration changes, and Kubernetes API activity. VCF Operations audit events capture platform interactions such as user logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications, with detailed views that facilitate the analysis of suspicious user actions or system issues.

VCF Operations can run metric correlation across all metrics for a selected object to identify metrics with similar or opposite behavioral change over the same time period, enabling robust cross-source analysis. VCF Operations for Logs collects and aggregates log data from vCenter and ESX hosts, providing dashboards and analytics for centralized log analysis. VCF Operations for Networks provides its own audit log, which integrates with syslog, allowing network monitoring data to be collected alongside infrastructure logs.

VCF supports syslog forwarding to up to three external targets using TLS encryption, enabling audit records from across the VCF stack to be sent to a SIEM or log aggregation platform for comprehensive integration with vulnerability scanner output and other external security tools. The alarm infrastructure in vCenter integrates with other server components such as events and performance counters, supporting metric-based alarm expressions that trigger alerts when performance metrics indicate unusual activity. VCF Operations self-monitoring metrics track internal health indicators, and sensor metrics such as temperature and fan health state are collected for host systems, broadening the scope of monitored data available for correlation.

VMware vDefend Security Intelligence collects and visualizes asset collections, VMs, workloads, and traffic flows through the Security Explorer, integrating with Security Explorer on the Monitor & Plan page to visualize recommendation inputs and recommended groups. Security Intelligence also allows filtering of affected workloads and provides access to full event history for signature-based detections.

However, vDefend does not perform vulnerability scanning or ingest vulnerability scanner output, network performance metrics, or external system monitoring data directly; integration of those additional sources requires external tooling and a SIEM or analytics platform.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to correlate information from audit records with information obtained from monitoring physical access to further enhance the ability to identify suspicious, inappropriate, unusual or malevolent activity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust audit logging and forwarding capabilities essential for correlating logical audit records with physical access monitoring. VCF Operations captures detailed audit events for platform interactions, including user logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications, offering a granular view for analyzing suspicious user actions or system issues. These audit reports provide traceability of objects and users across the environment, and user activity logs can be forwarded to an external syslog server. Concurrently, VCF Automation maintains audit logs for actions such as deployments, configuration changes, and resource modifications, complete with timestamps, user names, and trace IDs, which are exportable for integration with external analysis tools. VCF Operations also collects hardware sensor metrics, such as temperature and fan health state for host systems, which can serve as supplementary indicators when correlating logical events with physical conditions within the data center.

While VCF delivers the comprehensive logical audit data and the necessary syslog forwarding capabilities, the automated correlation of these VCF audit records with physical access control data, such as badge readers, door sensors, and surveillance systems, necessitates an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) or security analytics platform. This external platform must be configured to ingest both VCF log feeds and outputs from physical security systems to perform the required correlation and identify suspicious, inappropriate, unusual, or malevolent activity.

Description: Mechanisms exist to specify the permitted actions for both users and Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) associated with the review, analysis and reporting of audit information.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide robust, granular role-based access control (RBAC) mechanisms to specify permitted actions for both users and technology assets involved in the review, analysis, and reporting of audit information. Across VCF components, including VCF Operations, VCF Automation, vCenter, ESX, and NSX, dedicated audit roles are implemented with restricted, read-only access to audit data, preventing modification or deletion of audit trails. For instance, VCF Operations audit users have roles that limit them to read-only review, and NSX Manager prevents changes to audit user role assignments. VCF Automation provides distinct Organization Auditor roles with view-only rights to audit logs and related resources.

VMware Live Recovery further enhances this by offering a comprehensive permission model that allows administrators to define custom roles with specific privileges for interacting with audit-relevant DR objects, such as event logs and recovery plan history, ensuring audit review without operational access. Permissions are enforced at operation time, and changes to permissions are meticulously tracked through audit events. Similarly, VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform includes a predefined Auditor role, granting read-only access to critical operational areas and full access for export functions, enabling audit personnel to extract data without the ability to modify security policies or configurations. The platform ensures that access to analytical outputs and the ability to deploy policy recommendations are also gated by RBAC, ensuring that only authorized roles can perform these actions.

While the platform provides the comprehensive technical mechanisms for specifying permitted actions, the customer is responsible for defining their specific organizational audit policies, establishing appropriate roles and responsibilities, and configuring these RBAC mechanisms to align with their internal governance requirements for audit information access, review, and reporting. This includes assigning users to the appropriate roles and regularly reviewing access privileges.

Description: Mechanisms exist to adjust the level of audit review, analysis and reporting based on evolving threat information from law enforcement, industry associations or other credible sources of threat intelligence.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides configurable monitoring, alerting, and log management capabilities that administrators can tune in response to changing conditions. VCF Operations generates comprehensive audit events across categories such as Access, Access_control, Account_management, Configuration, Data_access, Network, Permissions, Session, System, Policy, and Firewall. Administrators can adjust the audit events time range and apply filters to focus review on specific activity types, users, or time periods. Risk alerts in VCF Operations indicate problems that require near-term investigation, and alert definitions can be configured to trigger at different thresholds, allowing for tightened monitoring when threat conditions evolve. VCF Operations can forward audit logs and events to an external syslog server, enabling integration with a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) or security analytics platform.

The organizational decision to modify audit review thresholds, reporting cadence, or analysis depth in response to specific threat intelligence requires administrative action and potentially integration with external threat intelligence platforms. Organizations must establish processes to correlate VCF audit data with external threat indicators and adjust their review and analysis posture accordingly. VMware vDefend does not provide mechanisms to adjust audit review and reporting levels based on external threat intelligence; such adjustments are organizational governance activities outside the product's scope.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to compile audit records into an organization-wide audit trail that is time-correlated.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides automated mechanisms through VCF Operations for Logs, which serves as a centralized log aggregation platform. This platform collects audit records from vCenter, ESX hosts, NSX, SDDC Manager, and other VCF components, compiling them into a unified, time-correlated audit trail.

VCF Operations captures platform interactions, including user logins, capability checks, and configuration modifications, with each audit event containing detailed timestamps and contextual metadata. Time-range filtering is supported, enabling analysts to correlate events across components within specific windows.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) and VMware vDefend components generate timestamped audit records for DR operations, security events, and internal service activities, which are forwarded via syslog to VCF Operations for Logs or external SIEM platforms. This ensures their inclusion in the centralized audit trail.

Time correlation across all VCF components, including VLR and vDefend, relies on robust NTP synchronization, with VCF tracking NTP events and drift corrections to maintain forensic accuracy.

VCF Operations for Logs facilitates the compilation of these diverse audit records into a cohesive, time-correlated organization-wide audit trail.

While VCF provides the automated mechanisms for compilation and time correlation, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing specific audit record retention policies within VCF Operations for Logs and any integrated external SIEM platforms to meet organizational compliance requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to provide privileged users or roles the capability to change the auditing to be performed on specified system components, based on specific event criteria within specified time thresholds.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides privileged administrators with the capability to adjust audit event categories, configure syslog forwarding, and manage log retention and rotation policies for various components, including ESX hosts and vCenter. Administrators can also adjust the time range for audit event display and analysis. VMware Live Recovery enables privileged administrators to modify logging levels for individual components, such as the recovery module and persistence layer, and edit alarm definitions to specify actions based on event triggers. VMware vDefend allows administrators to enable or disable logging on individual Distributed Firewall rules and sections, thereby controlling the granularity of network security audit data. Furthermore, vDefend IDS/IPS offers configurable action thresholds and rate filters, enabling privileged users to set trigger conditions based on rule match counts within specified time periods, directly influencing event generation based on specific criteria and time. Across all components, robust role-based access control mechanisms ensure that only appropriately authorized privileged users can access and modify these audit configurations.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for privileged users to modify auditing behavior on specified system components based on event criteria and time thresholds, the overarching organizational policies that dictate when and why audit configurations should be altered, including the definition of specific event criteria thresholds and the establishment of approval workflows for audit modifications, remain the responsibility of the customer. These processes are external to the platform's inherent capabilities.

Description: Mechanisms exist to maintain a current and accurate inventory of technology-related Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) being logged.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides centralized logging and monitoring capabilities that contribute to maintaining an inventory of technology assets being logged. VCF Operations for Logs collects data from VCF inventory objects such as VMware vCenter instances and VMware ESX hosts, with log collection configurations explicitly showing which infrastructure assets are actively sending logs. Log information is available across the vSphere, vSAN, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation management packs, providing administrators with visibility into the scope of logging across the platform. VCF Operations offers inventory dashboards for compute, network, and storage that can be cross-referenced with logging configurations to identify potential gaps. The Service Discovery feature identifies services running on monitored hosts and collects metrics, aiding in the identification of assets that should be forwarding logs. Furthermore, Log Assist can generate on-demand log bundles for any selected inventory object, supporting ad hoc verification of expected log data production.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform maintains an inventory of technology assets, including infrastructure imported via CSV, and associates these assets with logged security events such as Security Intelligence traffic flows and alert notifications. This enables visibility into which assets are generating logged activity within the platform's scope. The platform supports filtering of alerts by asset identifiers, facilitating the tracking of monitored and logged inventory assets.

While VCF and vDefend provide significant technical mechanisms for tracking logged assets within their respective domains, maintaining a formal, current, and accurate inventory of *all* technology assets being logged across the entire organization requires additional customer responsibilities. This includes establishing organizational processes to track which assets are configured for log forwarding, verifying comprehensive coverage across guest workloads and applications, and reconciling the logging inventory against a broader, authoritative IT asset management solution.

VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) components, including VMware Live Recovery (VLR) and VMware vDefend, provide robust mechanisms to configure technology assets, applications, and services to produce comprehensive event logs. These logs consistently capture the type of event, with VCF Operations categorizing events into types such as Access, Configuration, and System, while VLR identifies events by named types like SPDsUnprotEvent and PermissionsAddedEvent. vDefend further categorizes events by security function, including Distributed Firewall rule hits, IDS/IPS detections, and Malware Prevention verdicts. All event logs across these platforms include precise timestamps, such as ISO 8601 format in NSX Manager audit logs and "Created On" fields in VCF Operations, enabling accurate time-based correlation. The source and location of events are clearly identified; VCF Operations logs include source fields like ESX host or syslog forwarder, VLR events are associated with specific objects and identifiable instances, and vDefend logs pinpoint sources such as vNICs, virtual machines, network segments, and client VM IDs.

The platforms also ensure that event logs contain sufficient information regarding the outcome and user identity. VCF Operations audit events capture explicit success and failure states for operations, and logging levels can be filtered to discern outcomes. VLR recovery plan execution events include execution state and result status, with error-level events indicating failures, while vDefend logs record outcomes such as allowed/dropped/rejected traffic, alert/block actions, and malware analysis verdicts. User identity is consistently captured; VCF Operations audit logs can be filtered by User ID and User Name, VCF Automation records the event owner, and NSX Manager audit logs capture _create_user and _last_modified_user for configuration changes. vDefend's Identity Firewall correlates network traffic with Active Directory users, providing user-attributed security event logging.

The VCF Operations for Logs Analyze page provides a centralized interface for searching and correlating these detailed log events across all sources.

Description: Mechanisms exist to protect sensitive/regulated data contained in log files.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation protects sensitive and regulated data within log files through a multi-faceted approach. The platform's design philosophy prioritizes avoiding the writing of sensitive information, such as passwords or encryption keys, to logs. Consequently, log files are not encrypted at rest, as they are not intended to contain such data.

Access to log data is rigorously controlled via role-based access control (RBAC) within VCF Operations for Logs, restricting viewing and searching capabilities to authorized SSO users and groups. Auditing features track user access to audit data and management interfaces, providing accountability with detailed logs filtered by user ID, name, authentication source, session, client IP, category, and message. For data in transit, VCF supports TLS-encrypted forwarding of logs to external syslog targets. VCF Operations for Logs can also be deployed in FIPS Compliance Mode to meet stringent cryptographic requirements. Log retention and lifecycle management tools, such as the `log.keepOld` parameter and options to limit log file size and edit log folders, further reduce the exposure window for any inadvertently logged sensitive data.

VMware vDefend complements these capabilities by enforcing role-based access to its log data through distinct Auditor and Admin roles on the Security Services Platform. vDefend also supports TLS-encrypted forwarding of log messages, including file analysis lifecycle events and threat verdicts, to remote logging servers using mutual TLS configurations. To minimize sensitive data exposure, vDefend recommends selective logging for Distributed Firewall rules, enabling it only when necessary for monitoring or troubleshooting and disabling it once the required data is collected.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms for log protection, the customer retains critical responsibilities. Administrators must exercise diligence when collecting support bundles or core files, as these diagnostic artifacts may contain sensitive information. The definition and implementation of specific log retention policies, logging levels, and log file size limits, though facilitated by VCF tools, ultimately fall to the customer. Furthermore, the decision to deploy VCF Operations for Logs in FIPS Compliance Mode and the adherence to selective logging recommendations for vDefend are customer-driven actions. The broader enterprise-wide log access governance and the processes for handling sensitive data within diagnostic outputs remain operational responsibilities of the customer.

Description: Mechanisms exist to link system access to individual users or service accounts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes robust mechanisms to link system access to individual users and service accounts through comprehensive identity-aware audit logging across its integrated components. VCF Operations captures detailed audit events for interactions such as logins, logouts, configuration modifications, and searches, attributing each action to a specific User ID, User Name, Auth Source, and Client IP, enabling straightforward traceability. VCF Automation generates audit logs for reportable actions like blueprint deployments and Day 2 operations, distinguishing between user and service accounts, with service accounts specifically designed for API-based access and secured via Request Service Account Authorization. At the vSphere layer, the AuthorizationManager API manages permissions and roles, ensuring authenticated users' actions are tied to their accounts. NSX audit logging records user identities for network configuration changes, and VCF Operations for Networks supports exporting audit log data for external analysis.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) extends this capability to disaster recovery operations, recording which user performed specific actions like removing datastores or consistency groups from protection groups, and tracking permission changes. VLR manages per-user permissions in shared recovery site configurations, ensuring individual accountability and visibility while using service accounts for secure cross-site authentication.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform maintains immutable audit logs for all access events, providing a clear record linking system access to the performing accounts. Its dedicated Auditor role facilitates the review of tracked activity, and Malware Prevention records detailed file inspection events for correlation and traceability.

While the platform provides extensive technical mechanisms for linking system access to identities, customers are responsible for defining and implementing policies and processes to fully operationalize these capabilities. This includes establishing guidelines for the creation and management of service accounts, particularly for external integrations such as creating separate user accounts on storage arrays for VLR servers. Customers must also define procedures for the regular review and analysis of audit logs, leveraging the provided export capabilities and dedicated auditor roles to ensure ongoing accountability and to correlate events for suspicious activity or system issues.

Description: Mechanisms exist to log and review the actions of users and/or services with elevated privileges.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms to log and facilitate the review of actions performed by users and services with elevated privileges across all platform components.

vCenter Server includes a privilege checks recorder that meticulously monitors and records privileges utilized during operations, offering granular filtering and retrieval options by managed object ID, request operation ID, principal, privilege checked, and session identifier. This data is programmatically accessible via the PrivilegeChecks service interface.

The VCF Operations Audit Events framework captures critical platform interactions, including user logins, logouts, privilege checks, and configuration modifications across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources, attributing each action to a specific user.

VCF Operations for Logs centralizes log collection from vCenter Server and ESX hosts, providing advanced dashboards and analytics for efficient review and correlation.

VCF Automation maintains detailed audit logs for all reportable action types, including timestamps, user names, project names, and trace IDs.

VCF Operations for Networks captures administrative actions, including CRUD operations and login/logout alerts, in its audit logs.

VCF Operations supports forwarding user activity logs to external syslog servers with TLS encryption, enabling centralized collection and review in external SIEM or log management platforms.

ESX hosts maintain local logging with configurable retention, and vCenter Server offers database retention policies.

API audit logging captures REST API calls, SDK usage, and PowerCLI commands with user attribution.

VCF Operations provides configurable logging levels and component-specific logs via the log-trace CLI command, allowing administrators to increase verbosity for investigating privileged operations.

VMware Live Recovery logs actions performed by privileged users and services through audit events, authorization logging, and verbose operational logging across its components.

Permission change events, such as PermissionsAddedEvent, PermissionsUpdatedEvent, and PermissionsDeletedEvent, provide a direct audit trail of sensitive administrative operations.

VLR offers authorization logging between the VLR Server and vCenter, tracking privilege checks and authorization decisions.

The external API module logs API access at a verbose level by default, capturing programmatic interactions.

All VLR components log at a verbose level by default, providing detailed operational records of privileged actions like protection group creation and recovery plan execution.

VCF Operations orchestrator workflows for replication management, which require administrator privileges, are also logged upon execution.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform logs all access events by default, with audit logging being non-disableable, ensuring all privileged management actions are captured.

The platform includes an Auditor role, providing a read-only function specifically for reviewing tracked activity, thereby enabling periodic review of actions taken by users with elevated privileges.

Logging verbosity for vDefend components, such as the Guest Introspection Host Agent (MUX), can be configured to capture additional detail about service-level operations when necessary for investigation.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms for logging and facilitating the review of privileged actions, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing the organizational policies and operational procedures for regular review, analysis, and response to these logs. This includes establishing retention policies, defining review frequencies, assigning accountability for review, and integrating log data into broader security monitoring and incident response workflows.

Description: Mechanisms exist to verbosely log all traffic (both allowed and blocked) arriving at network boundary devices, including firewalls, Intrusion Detection / Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS) and inbound and outbound proxies.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, in conjunction with VMware vDefend, provides comprehensive mechanisms for verbose logging of all traffic at network boundary devices. NSX networking within VCF supports per-rule logging on its firewalls, and VCF Operations for Networks offers VPC Flow Logs that capture IP traffic, filterable by disposition (Accept, Reject, All). Logging can also be enabled on Compute Gateway Firewall rules. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes log collection and analysis from VCF components, including NSX, and can integrate with third-party devices, providing a consolidated platform for traffic log review.

VMware vDefend significantly enhances these capabilities by providing detailed traffic logging across its Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and IDS/IPS components. The vDefend Distributed Firewall allows per-rule logging for both allowed and blocked traffic, with customizable log tags for simplified parsing, extending this granularity to PRE_FILTER rules. The vDefend Gateway Firewall similarly supports logging of matched rules, directly addressing north-south boundary traffic. vDefend's IDS/IPS generates comprehensive security event logs, including metadata about matched signatures and actions taken, which can be forwarded to SIEM endpoints for centralized analysis.

While the platform provides the necessary technical mechanisms, the actual implementation of verbose logging is an administrative configuration choice. Logging must be explicitly enabled on individual firewall rules within both NSX and vDefend, and organizations are responsible for balancing logging verbosity against storage and performance requirements. The aggregation and long-term retention of these logs require either VCF Operations for Logs or a third-party SIEM solution, which the customer must configure and manage.

Description: Mechanisms exist to limit Personal Data (PD) contained in audit records to the elements identified in the Data Privacy Risk Assessment (DPRA).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to centrally manage and configure the content required to be captured in audit records generated by organization-defined system components.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for the centralized collection and management of audit records. VCF Operations for Logs serves as the primary centralized log collection platform, aggregating structured audit data from vCenter, ESX hosts, NSX Manager, and other VCF components. Administrators can configure log sources and establish retention policies based on log type, source, and compliance requirements. Individual VCF components generate audit records with well-defined content fields; for instance, NSX Manager logs include detailed metadata such as component name, entity ID, and username, while VCF Operations captures events across categories like access, configuration, and network actions. VCF Automation further contributes by creating audit logs for reportable actions, including blueprint deployments and Day 2 operations. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) enhances this by allowing administrators to configure the content detail captured for its components through granular logging levels (e.g., panic, error, info) for various modules, and these structured audit events are forwarded via syslog to centralized platforms like VCF Operations for Logs or external SIEMs. VMware vDefend supports centralized management of IDS/IPS signatures from a Global Manager, ensuring consistent audit-relevant detection content across distributed components, and its Security Services Platform provides compliance reporting reflecting these centrally managed security configurations.

While VCF components provide rich, structured audit data and offer mechanisms to configure their specific logging levels and content, the centralized configuration of the *specific content fields or verbosity* captured at each source component across *all* organization-defined system components typically requires component-by-component adjustments or relies on external log management and SIEM platforms for a truly unified policy. The platform facilitates the centralized management of collected audit records and offers component-level content configuration, but a single, overarching mechanism to dictate granular content capture across the entire ecosystem is not natively provided.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure databases produce audit records that contain sufficient information to monitor database activities.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation's internal infrastructure databases, including the embedded PostgreSQL instances utilized by vCenter and SDDC Manager, inherently produce audit records. These records encompass transaction and database logs that meticulously track changes, including all SQL transactions, which are critical for data integrity verification and disaster recovery.

At the platform level, VCF Operations provides comprehensive auditing capabilities, generating audit events across categories such as data access, configuration changes, and account management. These events capture essential details including the specific action performed, the user identity responsible, and precise timestamps, enabling detailed monitoring of database-level activity. Traceability reports further enhance visibility by tracking collected data and the associated users.

User activity audit information is readily available for download in PDF or XLS formats, and VCF Operations can forward these activity logs to an external syslog server for centralized retention and analysis. The vSphere Web Services API offers programmatic access to audit data through user log events, which mark actions and status associated with managed objects. Furthermore, the DiagnosticManager within vCenter provides operations for browsing diagnostic logs, generating log bundles, and querying log descriptions, ensuring administrators have deep visibility into the internal state and activities of VCF's database-backed services.

Description: Mechanisms exist to allocate and proactively manage sufficient event log storage capacity to reduce the likelihood of such capacity being exceeded.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for allocating and proactively managing event log storage capacity. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes log aggregation, enabling administrators to configure retention policies by log type and source, control log file sizes, and manage the number of retained log files. ESX hosts maintain local logging with configurable retention and rotation, while vCenter implements database retention policies with automated cleanup based on age or size. VCF Operations proactively monitors storage capacity, triggering built-in alerts for high disk usage and publishing Capacity Analytics metrics that project utilization up to three days into the future, allowing for anticipatory action. Syslog forwarding to external targets provides an independent, secondary copy of log data, mitigating local storage exhaustion risks. VMware Live Recovery components offer configurable log storage management through file size and count limits, log rotation, and adjustable logging levels, with VLR Server compressing old log files and vSphere Replication implementing its own rotation policies. VMware vDefend's Distributed Firewall enforces bounded rotation for packet logs, and its Security Services Platform proactively manages storage for flow data and security events by automatically pausing ingestion when capacity is low, resuming when sufficient space is available, and dynamically reducing data retention duration if disk capacity is reached. Platform Host Capacity dashboards provide visibility into resource utilization and data collection rates, supported by documented sizing guidance.

While the platform delivers robust technical capabilities for log storage management, customers are responsible for defining and implementing specific log retention policies, configuring appropriate log file sizes, rotation parameters, and logging levels across their environment. Proactive capacity planning, including allocating sufficient underlying storage resources and responding to capacity alerts and projections from VCF Operations and vDefend's dashboards, remains a customer operational responsibility to ensure log data integrity and availability.

Description: Mechanisms exist to alert appropriate personnel in the event of a log processing failure and take actions to remedy the disruption.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) incorporates comprehensive alerting and remediation mechanisms designed to address log processing disruptions across its integrated monitoring stack. VCF Operations facilitates the creation of alert definitions based on log events, enabling administrators to define symptoms that specifically detect log processing failures. These symptoms are then aggregated into alert definitions configured to notify relevant personnel. The alert framework supports various event types indicative of log processing issues, including System Degradation, Data Availability, Collector Down, and Object Error. When an alert is triggered, administrators can embed specific text instructions within the alert, providing clear, actionable remediation guidance.

VCF Operations for Logs further enhances this by offering log-based alerting through configurable symptom definitions within the Infrastructure Operations module, with the Log File plug-in supporting notification rules to ensure alerts reach the correct teams. Log Assist provides self-healing capabilities for transient log forwarding disruptions, automatically addressing intermittent network or infrastructure issues. Furthermore, VCF components generate fault events when connectivity to configured syslog targets is interrupted, ensuring that disruptions in log forwarding to external SIEM systems are detected and reported.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) complements these capabilities by providing event logging with configurable alarms for operational failures across the disaster recovery environment. These alarms can be configured to send email notifications, SNMP traps, or execute scripts, offering multiple channels to reach personnel. The VCF Operations Management Pack for vSphere Replication integrates replication error states into the centralized VCF Operations platform, allowing for consolidated monitoring of potential log-related disruptions originating from replication activities.

While the platform provides the necessary technical mechanisms for detection, alerting, and remediation guidance, the customer is responsible for defining specific symptom and alert thresholds, configuring notification rules to identify and reach appropriate personnel, and establishing the organizational policies and procedures for responding to and remediating identified log processing failures. The customer must also define the specific actions to be taken in response to various alert types, leveraging the platform's capabilities for guided remediation.

Description: Mechanisms exist to provide 24x7x365 near real-time alerting capability when an event log processing failure occurs.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive, near real-time alerting capabilities for event log processing failures.

VCF Operations includes robust mechanisms for defining alerts based on log events, allowing administrators to create symptom definitions that detect log processing failures and configure alert definitions to notify appropriate personnel. The alert framework supports multiple notification channels, including SNMP traps, log file entries, and outbound notification rules, and can provide clear remediation guidance.

VCF Operations for Logs offers configurable log-based alerting to monitor for specific conditions, while intelligent alerting capabilities help filter noise.

At the infrastructure level, Log Assist automatically handles transient log forwarding disruptions and surfaces issues it cannot resolve. vCenter Server's events and alarms framework further enables detection of logging service health changes with automated responses.

For external SIEM integration, VCF components generate fault events when syslog connectivity is interrupted, ensuring log delivery failures are detected. These mechanisms operate continuously, 24x7x365, with notifications delivered in near real-time as alarms fire immediately upon event detection.

VMware Live Recovery contributes to the overall monitoring posture through its own event-driven alarm framework, which operates continuously and triggers near real-time notifications for monitored events via email, SNMP traps, or script execution.

The VCF Operations Management Pack for vSphere Replication also provides always-on monitoring of replication error states, surfacing alerts to the centralized VCF Operations platform.

While VMware Live Recovery provides a robust general alerting framework, the specific detection of log processing failures is primarily handled by the underlying VCF platform's monitoring stack. The platform fully provides the necessary technical mechanisms to meet the control's requirement for 24x7x365 near real-time alerting for event log processing failures.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to alert appropriate personnel when the allocated volume reaches an organization-defined percentage of maximum event log storage capacity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive automated mechanisms to alert personnel when event log storage capacity reaches an organization-defined percentage. VCF Operations includes a Capacity Remaining policy element within the Policy Workspace, allowing administrators to configure precise percentage thresholds for capacity remaining alerts. These configurable thresholds are applied across all monitored storage resources, including datastores specifically used for log retention. Administrators can customize these settings per policy, overriding default thresholds for distinct environments and configuring different capacity calculations for production versus testing environments using custom groups.

VCF Operations for Logs further enhances this capability with pre-built content pack alerts, such as the SysDiskUsage alert, which triggers when disk usage for partitions exceeds 89% for more than 10 minutes, providing out-of-the-box detection of log storage exhaustion. Additionally, VCF Operations publishes Capacity Analytics metrics, offering forward-looking analysis of disk space to provide advance warning before storage is fully exhausted.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) monitors resource consumption on its server host, raising alarms when resource thresholds are reached, covering disk space where log files are stored. VLR also supports creating alarms for insufficient storage capacity conditions at the target site and manages log storage through configurable file size and count limits.

VMware vDefend generates automated alert notifications for general storage exhaustion and includes automated responses like reducing data retention or suspending data ingestion when disk capacity is exceeded.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific percentage thresholds for log storage capacity and configuring these within the VCF Policy Workspace. Furthermore, the organization must identify and designate the appropriate personnel to receive these automated alerts. While vDefend provides some storage exhaustion alerts and mitigations, the explicit configurable threshold-based alerting to designated personnel at an organization-defined percentage of log storage capacity is primarily a function of the underlying VCF monitoring stack.

Description: Mechanisms exist to provide an event log report generation capability to aid in detecting and assessing anomalous activities.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive event log report generation capabilities across its entire component stack, directly supporting the detection and assessment of anomalous activities.

VCF Operations for Logs collects and aggregates logs from VMware vCenter and VMware ESX hosts, offering advanced dashboards and analytics for event analysis. The Event Trends tab enables operators to construct and execute queries using search filters to analyze log events for trends and anomalies. VCF Operations extends this with scheduled report snapshots of views and dashboards, utilizing predefined report templates or custom configurations to track resource status and identify potential risks. Anomalies in metrics are highlighted during root-cause investigations, and audit reports provide traceability of objects and users.

The VCF Operations Audit Events framework captures interactions such as user logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources, registering each action as an event to detect suspicious access patterns and policy violations while providing user accountability.

ESX hosts maintain local logging with configurable retention, and vCenter provides database retention policies with automated cleanup. The vSphere Web Services API supports custom event structures (EventEx) with sophisticated metadata and event filtering, allowing collection criteria based on user name, entity type, time, and state. EventHistoryCollector objects can be created with filters to collect events matching specific criteria.

VCF supports syslog forwarding to up to three targets using various protocols, with TLS encryption available for confidentiality in transit, enabling integration with external log management and SIEM platforms for consolidated report generation and correlation.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) provides event logging and report generation capabilities specifically focused on disaster recovery operations.

vSphere Replication includes built-in reports displaying historic replication data over configurable time periods, allowing administrators to review replication trends and identify anomalous patterns in DR activity. The replication monitoring interface offers consolidated visibility into outgoing and incoming replications, local and remote site status, and replication issues, surfacing conditions that may indicate anomalous behavior such as unexpected replication failures, RPO violations, or configuration changes.

Both VLR and vSphere Replication generate structured audit events for significant operations, such as host configuration changes for replication, which can be used for automated monitoring.

The vSphere Replication REST API provides programmatic access to current replication issues, enabling integration with external reporting and monitoring tools, and the VCF Operations Management Pack for vSphere Replication extends reporting by surfacing replication metrics and alerts into the centralized VCF Operations reporting framework.

VMware vDefend provides extensive event log generation and reporting capabilities across its security components, directly supporting anomaly detection and assessment within the network security domain.

vDefend Distributed IDS/IPS generates detailed security event logs viewable for event analysis, with a Timeline view displaying events over selected time spans based on severity ratings to identify patterns and anomalous spikes in intrusion-related activity. Custom signatures generate advanced events with detailed metadata, supporting structured investigation workflows.

The Malware Prevention Service generates structured event logs with timestamps, analysis status, and event metadata, enabling analysts to identify anomalous file activity patterns.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) collects key abnormal activity and malicious events, correlating detection events into campaigns that provide a view of threats by identifying different stages of an attack. NDR can also send event logs to SIEM endpoints, supporting Splunk or custom endpoints.

Security Intelligence provides a Suspicious Traffic Events dashboard for monitoring and analysis, and the Security Services Platform Monitor and Plan Overview dashboard displays security posture summaries, flow trends, suspicious traffic event summaries, and Network Traffic Anomaly (NTA) detection events.

These dashboards and event views provide the reporting surface for security analysts to detect and assess anomalous network activity across the vDefend-protected environment, complementing the broader VCF monitoring stack by providing network-security-specific event data and dashboards that feed into those reporting workflows.

Description: Mechanisms exist to provide and implement the capability for auditing the parameters of user query events for data sets containing Personal Data (PD).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for auditing user query parameters at the infrastructure level through its integrated logging and audit frameworks. VMware vCenter API audit logging captures REST API calls, SDK usage, and PowerCLI commands, attributing them to specific users and recording the parameters of queries made against the infrastructure. The vSphere Web Services API supports EventFilterSpec objects for collecting events based on user name, entity type, time, and state, while EventHistoryCollector objects can filter events to match specific criteria, such as operations performed by a particular user within a given time range. Event data objects are populated at runtime with values associated with the source of an event, including compute resource, datacenter, datastore, host, network, and VM properties. The VCF Operations Audit Events framework captures platform interactions, including searches, configuration modifications, logins, logouts, and capability checks, generating detailed audit records for each action. Audit data can be filtered by user ID, user name, authentication source, session, client IP, category, and message, with user activity audit information available for download as PDF or XLS reports. NSX Manager audit logs track creation user, creation time, last modified user, last modified time, and revision history for security policy objects. VCF Automation maintains audit logs that record timestamps, messages, user names, project names, and trace IDs for reportable actions, and VCF Operations for Networks supports exporting audit log data as CSV files for external analysis. These capabilities deliver a detailed audit trail of who accessed or modified infrastructure resources and with what parameters.

While VCF provides comprehensive auditing for infrastructure-level queries and operations, it does not have visibility into application-layer queries against datasets containing personal data residing within guest workloads. Auditing query parameters within guest applications or databases that contain personal data requires the implementation of application-level logging tools and processes running inside the customer's workloads.

Description: Mechanisms exist to employ trend analyses to determine if security control implementations, the frequency of continuous monitoring activities, and/or the types of activities used in the continuous monitoring process need to be modified based on empirical data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for employing trend analyses through VCF Operations. This includes built-in trend analysis and forecasting capabilities that leverage historic data to generate trends for resource use, availability, and event patterns across the monitored environment. VCF Operations Trend Views allow administrators to visualize workload metrics over extended periods, identifying demand patterns. Event Trend Analysis compares query results to prior time periods, and dynamic thresholds in alert definitions adapt to baseline behavior changes using VCF Operations analytics. Pre-built dashboards, such as the Security Operations dashboard, offer comprehensive visualization of monitoring data.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) contributes by providing historical data and reporting for disaster recovery monitoring activities, including replication traffic and recovery plan outcomes. The VCF Operations Management Pack for vSphere Replication integrates VLR health and status metrics into VCF Operations, allowing this data to participate in the platform's broader trend views, dynamic threshold calculations, and capacity forecasting.

VMware vDefend further enhances these capabilities with network-specific trend analysis through Security Intelligence and the Security Services Platform. This includes Flow Trends for network communication patterns, continuous monitoring of segmentation drift, IDS/IPS security dashboards for intrusion severity and distribution trends, and Threat Monitoring for file inspection statistics. These components collectively provide the empirical data and analytical tools necessary to evaluate whether security control implementations, monitoring frequencies, or monitoring activities require modification.

While the platform delivers comprehensive technical mechanisms for data collection, trend visualization, and analytical insights across infrastructure, disaster recovery, and network security, the organization retains the responsibility for performing the overarching trend analysis across all continuous monitoring domains, interpreting the findings, making informed decisions, and actively implementing modifications to security controls and monitoring processes based on the empirical data. The platform provides the intelligence, but the strategic action and policy adjustments are customer-driven.

Description: Mechanisms exist to configure Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) to use an authoritative time source to generate time stamps for event logs.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for time synchronization across all platform components. ESX hosts natively support both Network Time Protocol (NTP) and Precision Time Protocol (PTP) through the HostDateTimeSystem, requiring configuration with authoritative NTP servers during deployment.

VCF management components can be configured to use these ESX NTP servers, establishing a consistent time hierarchy. SDDC Manager offers centralized capabilities to update NTP server configurations for VCF core components, and VCF Operations actively monitors NTP health, collecting offset and reach metrics.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) components generate timestamped event logs and audit records, inheriting their time source from the underlying VCF platform's NTP or PTP configuration. VLR further enhances this by actively monitoring time synchronization between its server components, enforcing a configurable clock skew threshold and raising alerts for time drift.

VMware vDefend and the Security Services Platform mandate accurate NTP configuration as a prerequisite for critical functions such as certificate validation, log correlation, and secure communications. Specific components like the Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and NDR Sensor rely directly on synchronized time for accurate event log processing, identity-based policy workflows, and time-based policy enforcement.

The Security Services Platform generates extensive timestamped log and metric data, all dependent on consistent time synchronization for meaningful correlation, trend analysis, and forensic review. The platform provides comprehensive mechanisms to configure all Technology Assets, Applications, and Services to use an authoritative time source for event log timestamps.

Description: Mechanisms exist to synchronize internal system clocks with an authoritative time source.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for synchronizing internal system clocks with an authoritative time source across all its components. SDDC Manager configures Network Time Protocol (NTP) settings for ESX hosts and management appliances during deployment, allowing administrators to specify external NTP servers. Individual VCF components, including vCenter, NSX Manager, and VCF Operations appliances, support independent NTP configuration. NSX transport nodes utilize the underlying operating system's time services for synchronization. For workloads requiring higher precision, VCF also supports Precision Time Protocol (PTP) at the hypervisor level, which can achieve sub-microsecond accuracy and be passed through to guest virtual machines. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) synchronizes its internal system clocks with the authoritative time source configured on the underlying VCF platform and enforces clock synchronization consistency across its server components through a configurable `maxClockSkew` parameter, raising events for detected time drift. VMware vDefend both depends on and enforces time synchronization, with components like Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, Security Services Platform, and NDR Sensors requiring accurate NTP synchronization for time-based rules, certificate validation, log correlation, and secure communications, validating time synchronization as a prerequisite during installation and operation.

The customer is responsible for establishing and maintaining an authoritative time source, such as external NTP servers, and configuring all VCF, VLR, and vDefend components to consistently synchronize with this source. This includes specifying the NTP server details, verifying the operational status of time synchronization on all transport nodes and appliances, and ensuring that clock skew remains within acceptable thresholds across the entire environment to support accurate log correlation, Kerberos authentication, certificate validation, and security policy enforcement.

Description: Mechanisms exist to protect event logs and audit tools from unauthorized access, modification and deletion.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms to protect event logs from unauthorized access. VCF components generate comprehensive audit logs that record security-relevant events, which are then forwarded to centralized collection points such as VCF Operations for Logs. Access to VCF Operations for Logs is strictly governed by role-based access controls, limiting who can view and manage log data. Log forwarding from VCF components to external syslog servers utilizes TLS encryption, ensuring log data integrity and confidentiality in transit. VCF Operations can be configured to send its user activity log to an external syslog server, and audit logs can be filtered by various criteria including user ID, authentication source, and client IP. NSX audit logs employ a structured syslog format with an audit flag to distinguish security-relevant events.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further protects event logs through its role-based access controls, with the vSphere Replication VRM diagnostics role specifically restricting log bundle generation, retrieval, and deletion to authorized personnel. VLR's broader privilege model governs administrative access across all disaster recovery functions, and it generates audit log entries for permission changes. VLR, as a Linux-based appliance, supports syslog forwarding to centralized log management platforms for offsite safekeeping.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform ensures audit logs are enabled by default and explicitly prevents their disabling, guaranteeing that access event logs cannot be suppressed or deleted through configuration. The Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, IDS/IPS, and Malware Prevention components within vDefend each generate structured security event logs, with Malware Prevention producing timestamped event records that support integrity verification.

While these platforms provide extensive capabilities for log generation, secure forwarding, and access control, VCF, VLR, and vDefend do not inherently offer immutable or tamper-evident log storage. Protection against unauthorized log modification or deletion at the storage layer requires integration with an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system, a write-once-read-many (WORM) log repository, or equivalent tamper-resistant storage. Organizations must implement such external systems and forward logs to them to fully satisfy the control's intent regarding the integrity and non-repudiation of archived log data.

Description: Mechanisms exist to back up event logs onto a physically different system or system component than the Security Incident Event Manager (SIEM) or similar automated tool.
Response:
The VMware Cloud Foundation platform provides comprehensive mechanisms to back up event logs onto physically different systems or system components. VCF components write application and audit logs to their local file systems and are capable of forwarding these logs to remote destinations for backup and long-term retention, supporting syslog forwarding to up to three remote targets with TLS encryption. VCF Operations for Logs serves as a centralized log aggregation platform, collecting logs from vCenter, ESX hosts, NSX, and other VCF management components. Operating on its own dedicated cluster of nodes, separate from the log-generating components, VCF Operations for Logs inherently provides a physically distinct system for log backup. Furthermore, VCF Operations can be configured to send its user activity log to an external syslog server for additional separation, and vCenter backup operations can selectively preserve event log data as part of its backup jobs.

VMware Live Recovery stores application and audit logs on its appliance filesystem and offers multiple mechanisms for backup to physically separate systems, including SCP, WinSCP, and a log collector tool. VLR events integrate with vCenter's event system, which supports syslog forwarding, and as a Linux-based appliance, VLR also supports native syslog forwarding to centralized log management platforms.

VMware vDefend generates extensive security event logs across its components and supports forwarding these logs to physically separate systems for backup and long-term retention. vDefend Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and IDS/IPS components support logging of security events and can export these events directly to syslog.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) can send event logs to SIEM endpoints, including Splunk or custom endpoints, ensuring security event data can be forwarded to physically separate SIEM or log archive infrastructure.

While the platform provides the comprehensive technical capabilities for log forwarding and separate storage, the customer is responsible for configuring the specific syslog forwarding targets, establishing and managing any external SIEM or log archive infrastructure, and defining the associated log retention policies and operational procedures for these backups.

Description: Mechanisms exist to restrict access to the management of event logs to privileged users with a specific business need.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust role-based access controls across its management components, including VCF Operations for Logs, to restrict who can view, search, and manage event logs. Users are assigned to groups with granular object-level access through role assignments, ensuring that only authorized personnel can access or modify log data and configurations. VCF Operations for Networks backup configurations allow administrators to control the inclusion or exclusion of audit logs, further managing log data accessibility. The VCF Operations orchestrator enforces role-based access control, restricting users to specific permissions based on assigned roles and group membership.

VMware Live Recovery similarly restricts access to event log management through role-based access controls, with the vSphere Replication VRM diagnostics role specifically governing the generation, retrieval, and deletion of log bundles. The VRM Administrator role can be customized to create tailored permissions, and logging configuration through the Log Manager is restricted to administrative users.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform restricts access to audit and event log functions via role-based access control, allowing for the assignment of roles to local or directory-managed users and groups to scope log access to personnel with a specific business need. All platforms audit permission changes, providing visibility into who modifies log access controls.

While the technical mechanisms for restricting and auditing log access are comprehensively provided, the organizational determination of which users have a specific business need for log access, the definition of least-privilege policies, and the ongoing management of these access policies require administrative process and governance outside the direct scope of the platform. Broader RBAC enforcement for log management functions outside the vDefend Security Services Platform, such as in external SIEM or log aggregation tools, also remains an organizational responsibility.

Description: Cryptographic mechanisms exist to protect the integrity of event logs and audit tools.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) components implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect the integrity of event logs during transmission. NSX Manager supports remote logging over TLS and LI-TLS protocols, encrypting log data in transit using certificates with SHA256WITHRSA signatures and 4096-bit RSA keys. vSphere Supervisor Platform syslog forwarding utilizes TLS encryption with certificate verification, and vCenter provides both API and UI methods for configuring TLS-encrypted syslog forwarding. VCF Operations and VCF Operations HCX similarly send user activity logs to external syslog servers via TLS. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) requires SSL/TLS certificates for all TLS connections to the VLR server, with a configurable minimum TLS version, and vSphere Replication uses TLSv1.2 for network encryption of replication traffic. VLR events leverage vCenter's TLS-encrypted syslog forwarding. VMware vDefend components, including the Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, IDS/IPS, and Malware Prevention Service, generate security event logs. The Security Services Platform syslog server and the Malware Prevention Service support TLS encryption for the transmission of these log messages to remote destinations, protecting event data against interception and tampering while in transit.

While these capabilities ensure the cryptographic integrity of log data during transmission to a centralized logging platform, the platform does not provide cryptographic integrity verification, such as digital signing or hashing, for stored log files themselves. VCF log files on disk are not encrypted. Organizations requiring tamper detection and cryptographic integrity protection of stored logs must implement these controls at the log management platform or SIEM that receives the forwarded events. The PostgreSQL database used by the Security Services Platform provides relational data integrity, but this is a database consistency mechanism and not a cryptographic integrity control.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to enforce dual authorization for the movement or deletion of event logs.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust role-based access controls (RBAC) across its components, including VCF Operations for Logs and NSX. These controls restrict administrative functions and permissions, allowing organizations to define granular access for managing log configurations and operations. VCF Operations for Logs provides access control management for SSO users and groups, governing who can view, search, and manage collected log data. Similarly, NSX employs RBAC to separate administrative duties across roles such as Network Admin and Security Admin, each with specific permissions. These capabilities enable administrators to limit log management privileges to a select group of authorized personnel.

Furthermore, VCF Operations for Logs and NSX support forwarding event logs to external syslog servers or SIEM platforms. This mechanism creates a separation between log generation and storage, ensuring that once logs are forwarded, the VCF administrator alone cannot delete or alter those external copies, thereby providing an indirect form of separation of duties for archived logs.

While VCF provides the foundational RBAC and log forwarding capabilities, it does not natively enforce a dual authorization mechanism requiring two distinct approvals for the movement or deletion of event logs. The platform does not include a built-in two-person approval workflow for these specific log operations. Organizations must implement the explicit dual authorization requirement through their own defined operational processes or leverage the capabilities of an external log management platform that receives the forwarded events.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize a non-repudiation capability to protect against an individual falsely denying having performed a particular action.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for non-repudiation by generating comprehensive audit logs that attribute every administrative action to an authenticated identity. VCF Operations records platform interactions, including logins, configuration modifications, and user activity, with detailed reports linking actions to specific users. NSX Manager automatically generates immutable audit logs for all API calls and CLI commands, embedding metadata that definitively ties network management actions to the performing user. VCF Automation further extends this capability by creating audit logs for reportable actions such as deploying cloud templates or performing Day 2 operations, each entry including the user name and timestamp. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) enforces authentication through VCF SSO for all operations and generates user-attributed audit events for disaster recovery actions, including protection group modifications and replication configurations. VMware vDefend contributes to this capability by producing detailed audit trails for network security operations, such as firewall rule matches, and by enforcing role-based access controls that attribute security policy enforcement and incident response decisions to authenticated identities. Across all these components, the platform provides the capability to forward these audit events to external syslog or Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems.

While the platform provides the essential technical mechanisms for generating, attributing, and forwarding audit data, the customer is responsible for the full utilization of this non-repudiation capability. This includes configuring and managing the external log management platforms, establishing appropriate long-term retention policies, and ensuring the tamper-evident storage and integrity of the collected audit logs to definitively protect against an individual falsely denying having performed a particular action.

Description: Mechanisms exist to bind the identity of the information producer to the information generated.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes robust mechanisms to bind the identity of information producers to the information generated across its components. VCF Operations records audit events for all platform interactions, attributing each event to the authenticated user who performed it, capturing details such as User ID, User Name, Auth Source, and Session. NSX employs a principal identity mechanism that binds object ownership to the creating identity, ensuring only the creator can modify or delete objects, and generates comprehensive audit logs for all API calls, including username and entity ID. VCF Automation creates audit logs for reportable actions like deploying cloud templates and Day 2 operations, linking each entry to the user name and project. VCF Operations further tracks report ownership, recording the user who generates a report or creates its schedule. All authentication events across VCF components, including SSO and identity provider integrations, are logged with user attribution, ensuring traceability of actions to authenticated identities.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) extends this by requiring authentication through VCF SSO for all operations, enforcing session binding for REST API requests, and recording user-attributed audit events for protection group operations, permission changes, and replication configurations. At the infrastructure level, vSphere Replication uses mutual certificate-based authentication to bind machine identity to replication data. VMware vDefend binds user identity, such as Active Directory group membership, to firewall rule enforcement, enabling security policies to reference specific users or groups for network traffic attribution, with identity derived from authenticated LDAP/LDAPS synchronization.

While the platform provides extensive mechanisms for binding identity to operational logs, configuration objects, automation records, reports, and network traffic, it does not provide broader information-producer identity binding outside of these contexts. Mechanisms for data labeling, document signing, or application-level identity binding for customer-specific data remain outside the platform's direct scope. Customers are responsible for implementing additional controls and processes to bind identity to information generated by their applications or user-created content not directly managed by the VCF platform components.

Description: Mechanisms exist to retain event logs for a time period consistent with records retention requirements to provide support for after-the-fact investigations of security incidents and to meet statutory, regulatory and contractual retention requirements.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for retaining event logs across its components, enabling organizations to align retention periods with their specific records retention requirements. VCF Operations for Logs serves as a centralized log management platform, retaining log data based on configurable retention periods, with audit events viewable across selectable time ranges. VCF Automation offers its own activity log history retention setting, allowing administrators to specify the number of days to keep logs or disable automatic deletion for indefinite preservation. At the hypervisor layer, ESX hosts maintain a rolling retention model, requiring administrators to size log file counts and rotation intervals to meet minimum retention needs. All VCF components support syslog forwarding to external log management systems and SIEM platforms, which is the standard approach for organizations with long-term retention requirements beyond VCF's built-in storage, facilitating centralized retention policy enforcement. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) also provides configurable log retention mechanisms for disaster recovery operations, preserving recovery plan history and allowing administrators to configure the number of history reports retained for export and offline archival. VLR employs log rotation, compression of old log files, and configurable per-component logging levels and rate limit log purge intervals. Event logs from VLR can be forwarded through vCenter's syslog to external log management platforms and SIEM systems for long-term retention and centralized policy enforcement. VMware vDefend retains security event data generated by its features for defined periods, such as Network Detection and Response retaining event and campaign data for 12 months, with other metrics having configurable and viewable retention periods.

While VCF, VLR, and vDefend provide the technical capabilities and configurable mechanisms for log retention, the organization is responsible for defining its specific statutory, regulatory, and contractual records retention requirements. This includes configuring the retention periods within the platforms, sizing log storage, and implementing and managing external log management or SIEM solutions to which logs are forwarded for comprehensive, long-term archival and centralized policy enforcement, especially since vDefend does not serve as an organization-wide log retention solution. The customer must ensure that the configured retention periods and external archival strategies meet their defined compliance obligations.

Description: Mechanisms exist to monitor for evidence of unauthorized exfiltration or disclosure of non-public information.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive monitoring capabilities designed to surface evidence of unauthorized data access or disclosure through audit logging, behavioral analytics, and real-time alerting.

VCF Operations delivers event auditing that provides visibility into platform interaction changes across the VCF infrastructure, identifying suspicious access events and policy violations. VCF Operations Analytics configures thresholds and generates alerts based on aberrations in entity behavior, flagging unusual patterns such as unexpected access to sensitive resources, anomalous data movement, or activity from accounts outside normal usage patterns. The user activity audit in VCF Operations tracks logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications, with audit trails forwardable to external syslog servers for correlation with other security tools.

VCF Operations for Networks captures administrative actions and login/logout alerts, providing network-level visibility into administrative behavior. For deeper network-level exfiltration detection, VCF integrates with VMware vDefend, which provides traffic analysis, deep packet inspection, and network detection and response capabilities. vDefend Security Intelligence monitors network traffic to identify anomalous patterns and suspicious activity indicative of unauthorized exfiltration or disclosure of non-public information, correlating detection events to identify threat campaign stages at the network level.

While VCF and vDefend offer robust mechanisms for monitoring and detecting evidence of unauthorized exfiltration or disclosure based on access patterns, behavioral anomalies, and network traffic, they do not provide full data loss prevention (DLP) or content inspection for data-at-rest or application-layer data classification. Coverage is limited to network-observable indicators and platform-level access events.

Customers are responsible for defining what constitutes non-public information, establishing policies for its handling, and implementing complementary DLP or Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB) controls to fully address content-aware data exfiltration and disclosure requirements.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to analyze network traffic to detect covert data exfiltration.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides foundational network traffic visibility and flow analysis capabilities essential for identifying anomalous transfer patterns.
