VCF Automation also offers a Limited Service Accounts View right to restrict visibility, and system administrators can establish a controlled provisioning chain for other system administrator and user accounts within the provider organization.

While VCF delivers the technical tools to enumerate, manage, and audit user and service accounts, the establishment and ongoing execution of an organizational process for maintaining a current, authoritative inventory of all authorized accounts remains the responsibility of the customer's identity governance program.

Description: Mechanisms exist to uniquely identify and centrally Authenticate, Authorize and Audit (AAA) organizational users and processes acting on behalf of organizational users.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for uniquely identifying and centrally authenticating, authorizing, and auditing (AAA) organizational users and processes acting on their behalf. VCF SSO serves as the central identity broker, managing all user and service authentication flows across vCenter, ESX, NSX, SDDC Manager, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation. This includes issuing SAML tokens, supporting various authentication contexts like PasswordProtectedTransport and holder-of-key tokens, and enabling identity federation with enterprise identity providers via SAML or OIDC. This federation extends existing AAA infrastructure to VCF, facilitating multifactor authentication and automated user lifecycle management.

Authorization is enforced through comprehensive role-based access control (RBAC) across all VCF components, with specific roles defined for vCenter (e.g., CloudAdmin), NSX (e.g., Enterprise Admin), VCF Automation (e.g., Organization Administrator), and VMware Kubernetes Service (e.g., Owner).

Service-to-service authentication utilizes certificate-based Principal Identity users, with each VCF component registering as a solution user with VCF SSO. The centralization provided by VCF SSO eliminates the need for direct authentication to individual ESX hosts and consolidates audit logging.

VMware vDefend Security Services Platform further supports authentication for its own management plane using local or directory-managed accounts, providing distinct admin and audit roles for access separation.

While the platform delivers the technical capabilities for a centralized AAA model, organizations retain the responsibility for defining identity governance policies, managing the underlying identity provider infrastructure, and establishing specific role assignment procedures to align with their operational and security requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to require individuals to be authenticated with an individual authenticator when a group authenticator is utilized.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to employ replay-resistant authentication.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust, automated mechanisms to ensure replay-resistant authentication across its components. VCF SSO utilizes SAML token-based authentication, where tokens are time-limited, bound to the authenticated session, and incorporate holder-of-key security artifacts that prevent replay by unauthorized clients. Credentials transmitted via the PasswordProtectedTransport authentication context method are secured over HTTPS, providing transport-layer replay protection through TLS. NSX Manager authentication mechanisms are specifically engineered to resist replay attacks, supporting session-based authentication with cookies to maintain authenticated state securely. For API and webhook security, VCF employs hash-based message authentication codes (HMAC) to authenticate webhook behavior requests, preventing tampering and replay of API calls. The VCF Automation API uses `csp-auth-token` for API requests, and provider management accounts authenticate using API access tokens that rotate after each use, limiting the window for token replay. Identity federation with external identity providers through SAML or OIDC inherits replay-resistant mechanisms from the enterprise identity provider, including nonce-based challenges and time-bound assertions. VCF Operations supports single sign-on authentication, allowing secure token reuse within a session while relying on these underlying replay-resistant token exchange protocols.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides these foundational technical capabilities, the organization is responsible for the proper configuration of identity federation, the consistent enforcement of TLS across the entire environment, and the selection of external identity providers that implement and maintain appropriate replay protection mechanisms.

Description: Mechanisms exist to accept and electronically verify organizational Personal Identity Verification (PIV) credentials.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) supports identity federation with enterprise identity providers through VCF Single Sign-On (SSO), which accepts industry-standard SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) authentication protocols. This capability allows VCF to integrate with external identity providers such as Okta, Ping Identity, and Microsoft Entra ID, which can be configured to accept and electronically verify Personal Identity Verification (PIV) credentials, including certificate-based authentication flows. VCF SSO acts as the centralized authentication service for all VCF components, delegating credential verification to the federated enterprise identity provider. This federation model also facilitates multifactor authentication (MFA) and automatic user synchronization. VCF Automation similarly supports OIDC, SAML, and AD/LDAP identity sources for single sign-on, while VCF Operations supports principal identity authentication through client certificates.

While VCF provides the necessary standards-based identity federation framework, it does not natively verify PIV credentials itself. Organizations are responsible for configuring their chosen enterprise identity provider to accept and electronically verify PIV credentials, including smart cards or derived credentials, and subsequently federating that provider with VCF SSO.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement Out-of-Band Authentication (OOBA) under specific conditions.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) supports identity federation with enterprise identity providers, including Okta, Ping Identity, and Microsoft Entra ID, through VCF Single Sign-On (SSO). This federation leverages SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) authentication protocols, enabling VCF to delegate authentication to the configured enterprise identity provider. These external identity providers can be configured to enforce out-of-band authentication (OOBA) methods such as push notifications, SMS verification, or hardware token challenges. VCF's federation model provides the necessary integration point for OOBA enforcement, with the identity provider handling the OOBA channel and VCF accepting the resulting SAML assertion or OIDC token. This capability extends across all VCF components, including vCenter, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation, ensuring that OOBA policies configured at the identity provider are inherited by all federated services.

VCF does not natively perform out-of-band authentication. Organizations must configure their enterprise identity provider to require OOBA for the specific conditions outlined in their security policy, then federate that provider with VCF SSO to extend these requirements to VCF administrative access.

Description: Mechanisms exist to uniquely identify and centrally Authenticate, Authorize and Audit (AAA) third-party users and processes that provide services to the organization.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes centralized, standards-based mechanisms for the unique identification, authentication, authorization, and auditing (AAA) of third-party users and processes. VCF SSO functions as the platform's central authentication service, through which all user authentication flows, issuing tokens that establish identity and trust with API endpoints. VCF SSO supports federation with enterprise identity providers via OIDC and SAML, enabling organizations to integrate their existing directory services and enforce their own authentication policies, including multifactor authentication. This federation model ensures third-party users authenticate against the organization's identity provider, with VCF components accepting the issued tokens for a single point of identity verification.

Programmatic and service-to-service access is secured through certificate-based authentication, providing unique identification and auditability for automated processes. Authorization is enforced via role-based access controls, where permissions linked to each authenticated account govern object visibility and operational capabilities. Comprehensive audit logging captures all authentication and authorization events across VCF components, maintaining a detailed record of third-party user activity.

The organization is responsible for configuring the integration with its enterprise identity provider, defining specific roles and permissions for third-party users and processes, and establishing processes for the regular review of audit logs to ensure adherence to organizational security policies and compliance requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to accept and electronically verify Personal Identity Verification (PIV) credentials from third-parties.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for integrating with enterprise identity infrastructures that support Personal Identity Verification (PIV) credentials. VCF supports federated authentication through industry-standard protocols such as SAML and OIDC, allowing it to integrate with enterprise identity providers configured to accept PIV-based authentication. When an organization's identity provider, such as Active Directory Federation Services, is configured for smart card login, VCF seamlessly inherits this authentication flow. Users authenticate to the federated identity provider using their PIV credential, and VCF accepts the resulting SAML assertion or OIDC token, thereby participating in PIV-based authentication chains without directly handling the PIV credential. Furthermore, VCF components leverage certificate-based authentication for secure service-to-service communication, and all identity sources configured within VCF SSO require proper certificate validation.

While VCF provides the essential federation and certificate-based authentication endpoints, it does not natively implement PIV middleware, credential readers, or direct Federal PKI trust chain validation. These critical functions are the responsibility of the enterprise identity provider and the client-side smart card infrastructure, which must be established and managed by the customer. The customer is therefore responsible for configuring their identity provider to accept and verify PIV credentials and ensuring the necessary client-side components are in place to facilitate PIV authentication.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to accept Federal Identity, Credential and Access Management (FICAM)-approved third-party credentials.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the foundational, standards-based identity federation mechanisms required to accept FICAM-approved third-party credentials. vCenter Server supports federation with external identity providers, establishing a trust relationship where the external provider performs user authentication. This federation leverages industry-standard protocols such as SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC), both of which are commonly employed by FICAM-approved credential providers.

Integrating with these external identity providers enables advanced authentication features, including multifactor authentication (MFA) and automatic account synchronization. The Client Credentials grant type further facilitates automated service-to-service authentication workflows. VCF Automation extends these capabilities by allowing organizations to configure identity providers supporting OIDC, SAML, and AD/LDAP protocols for single sign-on, and can act as an identity provider proxy. For Kubernetes workloads, vSphere Namespaces natively support external OIDC providers alongside VCF SSO. VCF's contribution is providing the necessary federation endpoints and token-based authentication mechanisms that ensure interoperability with such providers.

To fully realize the intent of this control, the customer is responsible for selecting and configuring an external identity provider that is officially FICAM-approved. The actual FICAM approval process and the issuance of credentials occur entirely outside the VCF platform.

Description: Mechanisms exist to conform systems to Federal Identity, Credential and Access Management (FICAM)-issued profiles.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms to integrate with Federal Identity, Credential and Access Management (FICAM)-issued profiles. VCF supports industry-standard identity protocols such as SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC), which form the foundation for FICAM profiles.

vCenter Server facilitates federation with external identity providers, trusting these providers to perform authentication via token-based mechanisms. This standards-based approach enables VCF to align with the authentication and federation requirements specified in FICAM-issued profiles. Identity provider federation within vCenter delivers multifactor authentication support, automatic account synchronization, and automated user registration and termination across services, directly supporting the account management and authentication assurance levels defined in FICAM profiles.

VCF Automation further enhances this by integrating with OIDC, SAML, and AD/LDAP, and can defer user permissions to the identity provider through the "Defer to Identity Provider" role, allowing profile-based role and attribute assertions to flow into VCF Automation's access control decisions. VCF Automation can also function as an identity provider proxy for relying parties.

VCF SSO supports the import of users and groups from configured identity sources, maintaining enterprise directory group memberships and role assignments, which is critical for the identity lifecycle management aspects of FICAM profiles.

Achieving full conformance to specific FICAM-issued profiles necessitates the appropriate configuration of the external identity provider and enterprise directory to meet those precise profile requirements. VCF provides the essential standards-based federation and access control mechanisms that seamlessly integrate with FICAM-conformant infrastructure, but the ultimate responsibility for configuring the external identity ecosystem to meet specific FICAM profile mandates rests with the customer.

Description: Mechanisms exist to disassociate user attributes or credential assertion relationships among individuals, credential service providers and relying parties.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation integrates with external identity providers using standards-based federation protocols such as SAML 2.0 and OIDC, operating as a relying party that consumes credential assertions. This architecture ensures that when an external provider revokes a user's credentials or modifies their attributes, VCF SSO validates these assertions at each authentication event and honors the disassociation on the subsequent authentication attempt. The Identity Broker within VCF allows administrators to review, correct, modify, or remove attribute mappings between its directory and Active Directory attributes, providing direct control over synchronized user information. Furthermore, system administrators can directly change provider account attributes, revoke previously granted access, and delete provider accounts within VCF. The "Defer to Identity Provider" role ensures that access rights are determined by the user's external identity provider, preventing local duplication of credential assertion relationships.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the technical mechanisms to disassociate user attributes and credential assertion relationships, the primary responsibility for initiating and managing these disassociations, such as removing users or revoking tokens, resides within the customer's external identity provider. Customers are responsible for establishing and enforcing policies and procedures for identity lifecycle management within their chosen identity providers to ensure timely and complete disassociation.

Description: Mechanisms exist to restrict the use of external authenticators to those that are National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)-compliant and maintain a list of accepted external authenticators.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to uniquely identify and centrally Authenticate, Authorize and Audit (AAA) devices before establishing a connection using bidirectional authentication that is cryptographically- based and replay resistant.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation uniquely identifies and authenticates devices through a suite of cryptographically-based, bidirectional, and replay-resistant mechanisms before establishing connections. ESX hosts authenticate to vCenter using unique machine SSL certificates and mutual TLS, ensuring both the host and vCenter verify each other's identity. NSX Manager authentication mechanisms are inherently resistant to replay attacks, and VCF's token-based authentication, including holder-of-key tokens, provides replay resistance for both user and programmatic device access. VCF Operations authenticates discovered network devices using credentials appropriate for SSH, SNMPv3 (which includes cryptographic verification), and API-based access, and supports mutual CHAP authentication for storage devices, providing bidirectional cryptographic verification. VCF Automation leverages the OAuth device authorization flow for authenticating devices with limited input capabilities.

VMware Live Site Recovery provides device-level identification and authentication through certificate-based site pairing, verifying remote site identities via certificate validation and securing all communications with TLS and certificate-based authentication.

VMware vDefend contributes to device authentication through its NDR Sensor onboarding process, where each sensor utilizes a unique registration token for authentication and registration. While vDefend's scope is specific to its sensors and does not extend to broad device authentication for general network infrastructure, the comprehensive capabilities across VCF, VLR, and vDefend ensure that robust, cryptographically-based, bidirectional, and replay-resistant authentication mechanisms are in place for the platform's managed devices and components.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure device identification and authentication is accurate by centrally-managing the joining of systems to the domain as part of the initial asset configuration management process.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) employs vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM), Configuration Profiles, and Host Profiles to centrally manage ESX host configurations, ensuring consistency across infrastructure components through a desired state configuration approach. When ESX hosts are commissioned into a VCF workload domain, SDDC Manager orchestrates the initial setup, which includes authentication configuration, certificate provisioning, and domain membership. This process establishes the VCF equivalent of centrally managing the joining of systems to its management domain. VCF also supports Trusted Platform Module (TPM) on ESX hosts, enabling the retrieval of TPM event logs for attestation services to validate boot-time measurements, thereby providing a hardware-rooted device attestation mechanism for the hypervisor layer. Furthermore, VCF Single Sign-On and the VCF Identity Broker provide centralized identity management across VCF instances, allowing consistent authentication configuration to be applied and managed from a single point.

These capabilities are specifically focused on ESX hosts and VCF infrastructure components. The customer remains responsible for implementing similar centralized management and authentication mechanisms for other systems within the broader environment, including guest operating systems and applications running on the VCF platform, to achieve comprehensive coverage for all assets.

Description: Mechanisms exist to enforce cryptographic communications keys to prevent one key from being used to access multiple devices.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) employs robust mechanisms to ensure cryptographic communication keys are unique and not reused across multiple devices or sessions. Internal VCF communications leverage Transport Layer Security (TLS) 1.3, which establishes unique session keys through a secure handshake process. Each connection independently negotiates its own key material, ensuring that a key from one session cannot be used to decrypt traffic from another session or access a different device. For Encrypted vSphere vMotion, the platform generates one-time keys (nonces) for each migration session, specifically designed to prevent key reuse across migration operations. Similarly, vSAN data-in-transit encryption dynamically generates a distinct encryption key every time two hosts establish a connection, and this key is exclusively used for traffic between those specific hosts during that particular session. These integrated capabilities collectively enforce the principle that each cryptographic communication session utilizes its own unique key material, thereby preventing a single key from being reused across devices or sessions.

VMware vDefend's Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation policies at the workload level, ensuring that network access between devices adheres to explicitly defined, policy-controlled paths. This limits lateral movement between devices in the environment. The Identity Firewall further extends these controls by enabling identity-based firewall rules tied to Active Directory groups and physical device event logs, supporting more granular authorization beyond simple network location. While vDefend does not manage cryptographic keys or device credentials directly, its enforcement of network-layer boundaries significantly limits the potential scope of any compromise, complementing the key management practices by restricting unauthorized communication pathways.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and authenticate third-party Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for identifying and authenticating third-party technology assets, applications, and services. VCF SSO offers centralized, token-based authentication and supports identity federation via SAML and OpenID Connect, enabling integration with enterprise identity providers for centralized user lifecycle management and multifactor authentication. For programmatic interactions, NSX utilizes principal identities for third-party applications, and solution users across VCF authenticate using certificates for secure service-to-service communication. VMware Kubernetes Service supports both VCF SSO and external OIDC providers for workload authentication. Additionally, Trusted Platform Module (TPM) management in vCenter provides hardware-based identification and integrity verification for ESX hosts.

VMware vDefend further contributes to these capabilities through cryptographic authentication mechanisms within its distributed security architecture. The Security Services Platform (SSP) manages the authentication lifecycle for NDR Sensors, which connect using Mutual TLS (mTLS) for bidirectional cryptographic verification. Administrators control sensor authorization through registration tokens. SSP employs public key authentication for ongoing connections and manages certificates, including CA bundles and imported certificates, to establish trust with external services and integrated components, thereby authenticating third-party network services interacting with its inspection functions.

While these platforms deliver robust technical tools for identification and authentication, a comprehensive third-party Technology Assets, Applications, and Services (TAAS) governance program necessitates organizational processes. This includes defining policies for asset inventory, risk classification, and the full lifecycle management of all third-party technology, which extends beyond the technical capabilities provided by the infrastructure and network security platforms.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure external service providers provide current and accurate information for any third-party user with access to the organization's data or assets.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prohibit privileged access by non-organizational users.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive, built-in mechanisms to prohibit privileged access by non-organizational users. VCF SSO acts as the centralized authentication boundary for the entire platform, requiring all administrative access to vCenter, NSX, ESX, and SDDC Manager to flow through it. By federating VCF SSO with an organization's enterprise identity provider, access is inherently restricted to users who exist within the organization's directory, preventing non-organizational users from authenticating.

Beyond initial authentication, VCF implements a robust role-based access control (RBAC) model across all its components. Administrators assign global and organization-specific roles, ensuring that even authenticated organizational users receive only the privileges explicitly granted to them. Organization-specific roles are confined to users within their respective organizations, and these roles contain only a subset of published rights, preventing privilege creep.

VCF Automation extends this with organization-level user management, allowing administrators to disable users and groups to immediately revoke access, while the Organization Owner role manages users without inherent service access, enforcing separation of duties. Custom roles with limited privilege sets can be created, and the accessControls API offers programmatic control over access control lists for fine-grained permission management. VMware vDefend further contributes by enforcing RBAC within its own management plane via the Security Services Platform, where role assignments control access to privileged security operations, and its directory-managed account integration ties role assignments to organizational identity.

The effective implementation of these mechanisms requires the customer to properly configure VCF SSO with their enterprise identity provider, accurately define organizational users within their directory services, and diligently assign roles and privileges according to their established security policies and least privilege principles.

Description:
Automated mechanisms exist to enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for:
 (1) Remote network access; 
 (2) Third-party Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS); and/ or
 (3) Non-console access to critical TAAS that store, transmit and/or process sensitive/regulated data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides automated mechanisms to integrate with external identity providers for Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) enforcement. VCF Single Sign-On (SSO) can federate to an enterprise identity provider using industry-standard protocols such as SAML 2.0 or OpenID Connect (OIDC). This integration enables administrators to require MFA for all interactive access to VCF management interfaces, including vCenter, SDDC Manager, and other core components. The VCF Identity Broker facilitates this federation, supporting documented configuration paths for various providers. For API access involving human users, VCF supports the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type, which is compatible with MFA-enabled authentication flows.

VCF does not include a native, built-in MFA mechanism; its role is to integrate with external MFA services. Therefore, organizations must separately deploy and configure an identity provider that supports MFA policies. Subsequent configuration of VCF SSO to federate with this external provider is required. It is imperative that the identity provider federation is configured to utilize MFA-compatible authentication flows, such as the Authorization Code grant type, as the Password grant type is a single-factor method and incompatible with MFA requirements. Non-interactive access by VCF solution user accounts relies on certificate-based authentication, which operates on a distinct trust model separate from MFA.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) to authenticate network access for privileged accounts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the essential mechanisms to integrate with external Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) solutions for privileged account access to its management interfaces. All administrative access to VCF components, including vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, and VCF Operations, is routed through VCF SSO. By federating VCF SSO with an enterprise identity provider using SAML 2.0 or OIDC, organizations can enforce MFA policies for privileged accounts. The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type is supported for this purpose, ensuring compatibility with MFA enforcement by the federated identity provider. VCF's role-based access control model ensures that users assigned privileged roles, such as the Administrator system role in vCenter, CloudAdmin, Assembler Administrator, and Cloud Administrator for VCF Operations for Networks, authenticate through the federated identity provider where MFA can be required.

VCF does not include a built-in MFA mechanism; therefore, the enforcement of MFA is dependent on an external identity provider. The organization is responsible for deploying and configuring an identity provider with appropriate MFA policies and subsequently federating VCF SSO to it. Service-to-service authentication between VCF components utilizes certificate-based solution user accounts, which operate outside the scope of user-based MFA flows.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) to authenticate network access for non-privileged accounts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational infrastructure to enable multi-factor authentication (MFA) for network access by non-privileged accounts. It supports identity provider federation, allowing vCenter to integrate with enterprise identity providers to enforce MFA for all user accounts. The platform leverages the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type, which is essential for MFA-compatible authentication flows, and integrates with standards-based identity providers such as AD FS. VMware Identity Broker also supports this critical grant type.

Role-based access control is consistently applied across all VCF components, including vCenter, NSX, SDDC Manager, VCF Operations, and VCF Operations for Networks, enabling the precise scoping of non-privileged accounts. VCF Operations further supports the import of user accounts from external authorization sources and their assignment to specific roles through its Access Control interface, while VCF Operations for Networks facilitates streamlined access via SSO configuration.

While VCF provides the necessary federation infrastructure and compatible authentication protocols, the actual multi-factor authentication capability, such as token generation, push notifications, or biometric verification, is provided by the external identity provider. Organizations are responsible for configuring and integrating an external identity provider that supports MFA and for ensuring the use of the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type, as the Password OAuth 2.0 grant type is a single-factor method incompatible with MFA requirements. The customer must also define and manage the specific policies and configurations within their chosen identity provider to enforce MFA for all relevant network access by non-privileged accounts.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) to authenticate local access for privileged accounts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) supports multifactor authentication (MFA) for privileged account access through integration with external identity providers. All administrative access to VCF management interfaces, including vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, and VCF Operations, is routed through VCF SSO. By federating VCF SSO to an enterprise identity provider using SAML 2.0 or OIDC, organizations can enforce MFA policies on privileged accounts before they authenticate to VCF components. The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type is the compatible authentication flow for MFA, supported by both vCenter and VMware Identity Broker. When federation is configured with this flow, privileged users authenticating to VCF through supported providers are subject to the identity provider's MFA policies. VCF's role-based access control model defines several privileged roles across its components.

VCF does not include a built-in MFA mechanism; its enforcement is entirely dependent on an external identity provider. The organization must deploy and configure an identity provider with MFA policies and then federate VCF SSO to it. Critically, for VCF local accounts that authenticate directly without federation, MFA is not available. While password complexity, lockout policies, and session timeouts serve as compensating controls for these direct local authentications, they do not provide multifactor authentication. Service-to-service authentication between VCF components utilizes certificate-based solution user accounts, which operate outside the MFA flow.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for access to privileged and non-privileged accounts such that one of the factors is independently provided by a device separate from the system being accessed.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms to support multi-factor authentication (MFA) for both privileged and non-privileged accounts through its identity provider federation capabilities. VCF's authentication architecture is designed to delegate authentication to external identity providers (IdPs) using industry-standard protocols such as SAML and OIDC. This delegation enables the IdP to handle the MFA challenge, including the delivery of out-of-band factors from a device separate from the system being accessed, such as a mobile phone, hardware token, or authenticator application. The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type is the recommended authentication flow for MFA compatibility, ensuring that client applications, vCenter, VCF Operations interfaces, and PowerCLI can leverage these out-of-band authentication methods. Identity federation is consistently available across all VCF management interfaces, allowing administrators to enforce strong MFA policies.

While VCF provides the essential architectural framework for MFA, the customer is responsible for configuring and integrating an external identity provider that supports and enforces the organization's specific MFA policies, including the selection of out-of-band factor types and their delivery mechanisms. The customer must also ensure that all authentication flows within their environment utilize MFA-compatible grant types, such as the Authorization Code grant type, and avoid the use of the Password grant type, which is incompatible with MFA. The ultimate choice, configuration, and management of the external IdP and its MFA capabilities, along with the associated organizational policies, reside outside of VCF's direct implementation.

Description: Mechanisms exist to enable alternative Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) tokens when the primary MFA solution is not able to be used.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) supports multi-factor authentication through federation with external identity providers (IdPs) using SAML and OIDC protocols. VCF's authentication architecture does not impose restrictions on the MFA methods an IdP can present, allowing organizations to configure primary and fallback MFA options, such as backup codes, secondary authenticator apps, or recovery tokens, as supported by their chosen IdP. The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type is compatible with MFA-enabled authentication flows, providing the necessary flexibility for integrating diverse identity infrastructures.

While VCF provides the necessary integration points, it does not natively manage MFA token provisioning, enrollment, or failover. The availability and configuration of alternative MFA tokens are entirely determined by the external identity provider's setup. For local VCF accounts that authenticate directly through VCF SSO without federation, MFA is not supported, rendering alternative MFA tokens inapplicable. Therefore, the responsibility for implementing and managing alternative authentication methods when the primary MFA solution is unavailable rests with the external identity provider and the customer's configuration of that IdP.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize a formal user registration and de-registration process that governs the assignment of access rights.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides centralized identity management capabilities through its Single Sign-On (SSO) service, which acts as the core authentication and identity provider. User and group provisioning is supported via integration with enterprise directories such as Active Directory or LDAP, as well as Just-In-Time (JIT) provisioning from federated identity providers, dynamically creating user accounts upon first login. VCF Operations facilitates the technical aspect of user registration by allowing the import of user accounts from authorization sources and the assignment of role-based access through its Access Control interface. User groups can be manually created or imported from SSO or LDAP databases. VCF Automation further enables organization administrators to manage user rights, create local roles, and clone rights bundles for custom access configurations.

De-provisioning is handled through the integrated identity provider; when accounts are removed or disabled in the federated identity provider or directory service, access to VCF is automatically revoked. VCF Automation also offers explicit user account deactivation via its Access Control interface, disabling access while retaining the user account for potential future reactivation.

Role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced across all VCF components, including vCenter, SDDC Manager, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation, ensuring that the assignment of specific access rights is governed independently of account provisioning.

While VCF delivers the technical enforcement layer for user provisioning, de-provisioning, and access control, it does not inherently provide a standalone registration or approval workflow. The establishment of a formal user registration and de-registration process, including associated approval workflows and periodic entitlement reviews, remains an organizational responsibility that falls outside the scope of VCF's technical capabilities.

Description: Mechanisms exist to revoke user access rights following changes in personnel roles and duties, if no longer necessary or permitted.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust role-based access control (RBAC) across its entire stack, including vCenter, VCF Automation, and VCF Operations, offering comprehensive mechanisms to adjust or revoke user access rights when personnel roles and duties change. Access is intrinsically linked to roles assigned to users and groups, allowing administrators to modify these assignments to reflect current responsibilities.

In vCenter, predefined roles consist of specific privilege sets; modifications to these roles by System Administrators are immediately applied to all users assigned that role, ensuring prompt access adjustments. VCF Automation and VCF Operations similarly enable organization administrators to edit group role assignments, deactivate user accounts to temporarily disable access, or permanently revoke access and delete provider accounts.

For granular control, VCF supports entity-level access with View, Edit, and Full Control permissions, which can be assigned to specific user roles and published to organizations as needed. Furthermore, when VCF is federated with an enterprise identity provider or directory service, changes to group memberships within the directory are automatically reflected in VCF access, effectively revoking access when a user is removed from a mapped AD or LDAP group.

VMware vDefend also contributes by providing its own RBAC within its management plane, allowing administrators to update or remove role assignments for Security Services Platform and Security Intelligence users through the SSP user management interface, ensuring access aligns with current job functions.

While VMware Cloud Foundation and vDefend provide the essential technical mechanisms for adjusting and revoking access, the establishment and execution of the organizational processes to identify when personnel role changes occur and to initiate the appropriate access modifications remain the customer's responsibility. This includes defining policies and procedures for access reviews and revocation workflows.

Description: Mechanisms exist to revoke user access rights in a timely manner, upon termination of employment or contract.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for the timely revocation of user access rights. VCF integrates seamlessly with external identity providers such as Active Directory, LDAP, SAML, and OIDC. When a user's status changes within the organization's identity provider—specifically, when they are disabled or removed—their ability to authenticate to VCF components is immediately impacted. Role-based access control (RBAC) within VCF is often tied to federated group memberships, ensuring that changes to group affiliations in the identity provider are reflected across VCF environments without requiring individual component updates.

For accounts managed directly within VCF components, administrators have dedicated interfaces within VCF Automation to deactivate or delete user accounts and revoke individual access tokens. Disabling a user in VCF Automation effectively removes their active access.

Furthermore, system administrators can revoke previously granted access and delete provider accounts through VCF management interfaces. Access tokens issued by VCF have a default lifetime of one hour, with refresh tokens lasting 90 days, but these can be revoked proactively.

VCF Automation also enforces deployment lease policies, which automatically shut down and destroy deployments after their expiration, serving as an additional safeguard to limit resource access for users who may no longer be active.

While VCF provides the essential technical capabilities and tools for access revocation, the customer is responsible for establishing and enforcing the operational processes and policies that ensure these mechanisms are triggered in a timely manner upon termination of employment or contract. This includes promptly updating user statuses in the integrated identity providers and utilizing VCF's native administrative tools as part of a comprehensive offboarding procedure.

Description: Mechanisms exist to enforce Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) for Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD) to restrict access to individuals assigned specific roles with legitimate business needs.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation enforces role-based access control (RBAC) across all its components, restricting access to technology assets, applications, services, and data based on assigned roles. vCenter provides fine-grained authorization through a permissions model that associates roles with users or groups on objects in the vCenter inventory hierarchy, supporting predefined roles like operator, administrator, and super administrator, as well as custom roles with immediate privilege updates. NSX implements RBAC across multiple operational domains, allowing administrators to create custom roles and assign predefined RBAC roles for service-to-service authentication. SDDC Manager utilizes a role-based access control system with granular permissions, including a comprehensive System Administrator role. VCF Operations supports role-based access through service role assignment and defines its own roles within vCenter. VCF Automation governs access through predefined roles at the project level, with editable role assignments for groups. For Kubernetes workloads, vSphere Namespaces support Owner, Can Edit, and Can View permission roles, which map to Kubernetes ClusterRoles.

VMware Live Recovery enforces role-based access control through three purpose-built roles registered in vCenter: Administrator, Protection Groups Administrator, and VRM Administrator. These roles are collections of privileges for specific DR tasks, and the VRM Administrator role can be cloned and modified to create custom roles. The platform supports combining roles for users belonging to multiple groups and allows for complex permission hierarchies with the Propagate to Children option. For cross-site operations, VMware Live Recovery performs dual-site permission checks, requiring appropriate permissions on both the protected and recovery sites.

VMware vDefend enforces role-based access control (RBAC) across its components to restrict access to security policy management, firewall rule administration, threat analysis, and other features based on assigned user roles. The vDefend Distributed Firewall implements RBAC to control who can create or modify firewall rules, thereby maintaining the integrity of network security policies. The Security Services Platform implements RBAC with five defined roles (Enterprise Admin, Auditor, Security Engineer, Security Operator, Support Bundle Collector), supporting assignments to individual remote users or groups and allowing multiple roles per user or group. Security Intelligence utilizes NSX built-in roles, providing granular separation of duties across security analytics functions without supporting custom roles. vDefend's RBAC controls complement the broader VCF RBAC model by ensuring that infrastructure administration privileges do not automatically grant network security policy modification capabilities, enforcing a critical separation of duties.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms to enforce RBAC, the customer is responsible for defining specific roles, establishing the legitimate business needs that justify access, and assigning individuals or groups to those roles in alignment with organizational security policies and operational requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to govern naming standards for usernames and Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure proper user identification management for non-consumer users and administrators.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes centralized user identity management for platform administrators and non-consumer users across all integrated components. VCF Single Sign-On (SSO) functions as the primary identity management hub, facilitating secure federation with enterprise identity providers through industry-standard protocols such as OIDC, SAML, and AD/LDAP. The Identity Broker appliance is responsible for managing the precise mapping between external identity providers and internal directory services, ensuring consistent user identity across all systems by utilizing unique identifiers.

Each major VCF component integrates robust user lifecycle management capabilities. NSX Manager supports comprehensive local user management, including creation, deletion, activation, deactivation, password reset, username changes, role assignment, and configurable password expiration through the Enterprise Admin role. VCF Automation provides capabilities for user account creation, deactivation, deletion, and API-based remapping of users between identity providers. VCF Operations supports importing users from SSO and LDAP sources, enabling granular role-based access control assignment.

System Administrators possess the authority to create additional administrator and user accounts within the provider organization. Furthermore, federating vCenter to an enterprise identity provider enables automatic registration and termination of users across services, significantly reducing the identity management burden. VCF supports dynamic group assignment based on SAML and OIDC token assertions, ensuring users receive appropriate role assignments at login based on their group membership in the external identity provider. Principal Identity management within NSX provides dedicated service account identities specifically for third-party integrations and automation platforms.

While VCF provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for user identification management, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing organizational policies and processes that govern user lifecycle, role assignments, and access reviews. This includes establishing the criteria for "proper" user identification management, configuring the integration with external identity providers, and ensuring adherence to internal security and compliance requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify contractors and other third-party users through unique username characteristics.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation integrates with enterprise identity providers through VCF SSO, serving as the centralized authentication point for the platform. Organizations connect their identity sources, such as Active Directory or LDAP directories, and can specify a Base User DN to control which user accounts are synced.

Naming conventions and username characteristics established in the external directory, including prefixes or organizational unit placement that distinguish contractors and third-party users from employees, are faithfully carried through into VCF. Once authenticated, each user's identity is consistently represented across VCF components, with usernames appearing in audit logs, access control lists, and user information views. VCF Automation makes basic user information visible, including username, full name, description, and role, which allows administrators to identify user types at a glance. For deployments using federated identity providers via SAML or OIDC, VCF inspects claims from the upstream identity provider's token to obtain and preserve the organization's established username scheme.

While VCF consumes and displays these unique username characteristics, the actual creation and enforcement of naming conventions that distinguish contractors and third-party users must be defined and managed within the external identity provider.

Description: Mechanisms exist to dynamically manage usernames and system identifiers.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation dynamically consumes and manages user identities through its integration with external identity sources such as Active Directory, LDAP, or federated identity providers via SAML and OIDC. This ensures that changes to user accounts in the upstream directory, including creation, disablement, or deletion, are reflected within VCF without requiring manual intervention.

VCF Automation supports the import of users from these identity providers, representing them by their name identifier. For system identifiers, VCF manages various non-human accounts dynamically. ESX provides the HostLocalAccountManager interface for programmatic creation and management of local user accounts on ESX hosts. NSX Manager supports renaming user accounts through its CLI, and VCF Operations manages its own service accounts and integrations. When Active Directory integration is configured, the Common Name setting maps to userPrincipalName by default, maintaining alignment of system-level identity references with directory changes.

While VCF dynamically consumes identity changes and manages its own system identifiers, the primary lifecycle management of user accounts, encompassing provisioning, suspension, and deprovisioning, is performed within the external identity management system configured by the customer.

Description: Mechanisms exist to coordinate username identifiers with external organizations for cross-organization management of identifiers.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive mechanisms for coordinating username identifiers with external organizations, enabling robust cross-organization management of identities. VCF Automation supports integration with multiple external identity providers (IdPs) through industry-standard protocols including SAML, OIDC, and VCF SSO. This allows organization administrators to import users and groups, utilizing the name identifier format to ensure consistent coordination of usernames across organizational boundaries. Additionally, VCF incorporates support for the System for Cross-domain Identity Management (SCIM) standard, which automates the secure exchange of user identity information between disparate identity domains. The platform's organizational structure facilitates the secure grouping and management of resources, users, and services, allowing for the sharing of defined entities across different organizations.

While VCF delivers these foundational capabilities, the customer is responsible for configuring the appropriate identity provider types within their organizations. This includes managing the remapping of users when integrating with new external identity sources or transitioning between existing ones, ensuring continuous coordination of username identifiers.

Description: Mechanisms exist to uniquely manage privileged accounts to identify the account as a privileged user or service.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements robust mechanisms to uniquely manage privileged accounts and clearly distinguish them from standard user accounts across all platform components. Each user account within VCF Operations is assigned a unique identity and associated with specific roles that dictate permissible activities and object access. The description field on user accounts serves as a clear mechanism to identify account types and summarize access privileges, effectively marking accounts as privileged.

VCF explicitly differentiates between human and non-human privileged identities. Service accounts in VCF Automation are designated exclusively for API-based access, separate from interactive user accounts. Provider management accounts, created by system administrators with specific privileges, can be registered programmatically via the REST API. NSX utilizes principal identities for local authentication and predefined RBAC roles, providing a distinct identity type for service-to-service communication. Solution users further distinguish themselves by authenticating with certificates rather than passwords.

VCF SSO centralizes authentication, mapping roles to users imported from SAML identity providers based on attribute mappings. The CloudAdmin role model establishes a clear separation between platform-level privileged operations and standard administrative functions. Administrators gain visibility into privileged account activity through the ability to filter and retrieve privilege checks by managed object IDs, principals, privileges checked, and session identifiers.

Within its own management plane, vDefend Security Services Platform (SSP) maintains distinct local privileged accounts, including an admin user with the Enterprise Admin role and an audit user with the Auditor role, each possessing unique identities and defined privilege levels. SSP also supports directory-managed accounts via LDAP/Active Directory, requiring userPrincipalName configuration for remote users and groups to ensure unique identification when assigning privileged roles. Security Intelligence further enforces that users must hold required privileges to administer compute entity classifications.

The platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for unique management and identification of privileged accounts.

Description: Mechanisms exist to generate pairwise pseudonymous identifiers with no identifying information about a data subject to discourage activity tracking and profiling of the data subject.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to:
 (1) Securely manage authenticators for users and devices; and
 (2) Ensure the strength of authentication is appropriate to the classification of the data being accessed.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust, built-in capabilities for managing authenticators and ensuring authentication strength across its components. VCF SSO centralizes authentication, issuing token-based credentials and supporting federation with enterprise identity providers. This enables multifactor authentication (MFA) via the Authorization Code grant type, automatic user provisioning/deprovisioning, and token-based authentication, significantly reducing credential exposure risk. For local accounts, VCF components enforce stringent password complexity rules, such as a minimum of 12-15 characters with mixed case, numeric, and special character requirements, enforced through mechanisms like Linux PAM modules. Password expiration policies are configurable, with typical defaults around 90 days, and account lockout policies are implemented after repeated failed login attempts. Service-to-service authentication leverages certificate-based credentials and API access tokens that rotate after each use, with service accounts having restricted access. VCF further provides comprehensive certificate lifecycle management through SDDC Manager, integrating with external Certificate Authorities and supporting non-disruptive certificate rotation.

While VCF delivers the technical controls for authenticator strength, complexity, expiration, and rotation within its platform, the broader organizational policies governing authenticator lifecycle management and the determination of appropriate authentication strength for specific data classifications extend beyond VCF's scope. Customers are responsible for defining and implementing enterprise-wide policies, processes, and governance structures that dictate authenticator management across all systems and ensure authentication strength aligns with their data classification schema.

Description: Mechanisms exist to enforce complexity, length and lifespan considerations to ensure strong criteria for password-based authentication.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements robust mechanisms to enforce password complexity, length, and lifespan across all platform components. NSX Manager and NSX Edge leverage Linux PAM modules to enforce complexity, including minimums for length (e.g., 12 characters), character types (uppercase, lowercase, numeric, special), and unique characters, with additional restrictions against dictionary words, palindromes, and monotonic sequences on NSX Edge. VCF Operations enforces a minimum password length of 15 characters with similar complexity requirements. Password expiration is enforced, defaulting to 90 days for NSX components and 180 days for SSP local accounts, with configurable lifespans up to 9,999 days. SDDC Manager provides centralized password lifecycle management, enabling on-demand or scheduled password rotation for all VCF components, generating randomized passwords that adhere to component-specific policies. Account lockout mechanisms are also enforced after repeated failed login attempts across various components.

VMware vDefend further contributes to this posture by enforcing strong password policies across its management interfaces. The Security Services Platform (SSP), as the centralized management console for vDefend, mandates a minimum password length of 12 characters, a maximum of 128 characters, and requires at least one uppercase, one lowercase, one numeric, and one special character for local user accounts. Passwords for these accounts are valid for 180 days, with notifications provided prior to expiration. NSX Manager and NSX Edge, integral to vDefend's distributed and gateway firewall services, enforce similar complexity, length, and lifespan requirements, including retry limits and configurable expiration periods. SSP also supports integration with external directory services via LDAPS, allowing organizations to extend their enterprise-wide password policies to vDefend administrative access through federated authentication.

The platform provides comprehensive technical capabilities to enforce strong password criteria. Customers are responsible for configuring these robust mechanisms to align with their specific organizational password policies and operational requirements.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to validate certificates by constructing and verifying a certification path to an accepted trust anchor including checking certificate status information for PKI-based authentication.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust automated mechanisms for validating certificates by constructing and verifying certification paths to accepted trust anchors during TLS communication and PKI-based authentication. VCF components, including SSO and solution user accounts, validate security artifacts and certificate chains against trusted certificate authorities for service-to-service communication. The platform offers comprehensive certificate trust management, with VCF Automation retrieving intermediate and leaf certificates to complete incomplete chains and allowing administrators to make selective trust decisions.

VCF Operations for Networks supports both automatic and manual acceptance of certificate changes, while the vSphere Automation API exposes interfaces for programmatic management of TrustedRootChains, VMCARoot, and TLS certificates. The integrated VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA) issues certificates to platform components and can operate as a subordinate CA to an enterprise PKI, ensuring all issued certificates chain back to the enterprise root. Standard certificate validation can be activated to verify certificates during connection testing, checking the certificate chain against configured trust anchors.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) leverages PKI-based authentication for site pairing and appliance management, verifying Platform Services Controller certificates through the VCF certificate infrastructure. Site pairing relies on certificate exchange and verification, with options to inspect certificates before acceptance. vSphere Replication automatically installs encryption agents on ESX hosts using certificate-based authentication, and all VLR-vCenter communications utilize TLS with certificate validation for mutual authentication.

VMware vDefend further enhances this capability within its Security Services Platform (SSP), employing PKI-based certificate validation for internal component authentication and secure communications with external identity providers. The SSP maintains its own certificate hierarchy, including a root CA and intermediate CAs, which are automatically created during node onboarding to establish verifiable certification paths from leaf certificates to a trusted root. The platform actively monitors certificate status, identifying expired, expiring, or valid certificates and generating alarms as needed. Administrators can manage certificates by viewing details, replacing them, generating Certificate Signing Requests, and importing certificates from their chosen certificate authority.

The NDR Sensor specifically uses PKIX certificate path validation for automated authentication to the SSP during registration and ongoing communication. For external integrations, the SSP validates LDAP certificates for secure LDAPS authentication and supports importing web proxy or ingress certificates signed by external CAs.

While VCF, VLR, and vDefend provide extensive automated certificate path validation and trust store management, full PKI lifecycle management, including comprehensive certificate revocation checking (CRL/OCSP) against external certificate authorities, depends on the enterprise PKI infrastructure that VCF integrates with. Customers are responsible for configuring and maintaining their enterprise PKI to provide complete revocation status information and for ensuring proper integration with VCF for external certificate authority services.

Description: Mechanisms exist to conduct in-person or trusted third-party identify verification before user accounts for third-parties are created.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) integrates with enterprise identity providers through VCF SSO and VCF Identity Broker, supporting federation via SAML and OpenID Connect (OIDC). When federated with an external identity provider, all user authentication, including for third-party accounts, is handled by that provider. This architecture ensures that identity verification processes, including any requirements for in-person or trusted third-party verification, are enforced by the organization's identity provider before a user can authenticate to VCF. VCF Identity Broker further supports automated user and group provisioning from the identity provider using SCIM 2.0, ensuring that account creation and deprovisioning adhere to the lifecycle managed by the enterprise identity system. VCF does not maintain its own user accounts for federated users; it relies on the identity provider's assertions and inherits the identity verification decisions made upstream.

While VCF provides the necessary integration points and defers authentication and account lifecycle management to enterprise identity providers, the actual implementation and enforcement of in-person or trusted third-party identity verification mechanisms remain an organizational procedure. Customers are responsible for selecting, configuring, and maintaining an identity provider that incorporates these specific verification processes before provisioning third-party user accounts.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to determine if password authenticators are sufficiently strong enough to satisfy organization-defined password length and complexity requirements.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements automated mechanisms to enforce organization-defined password length and complexity requirements across its management components. VCF Operations enforces a configurable password strength policy, allowing administrators to define minimum character length, complexity criteria, and password change policies through Global Settings.

NSX Manager and NSX Edge nodes leverage the Linux PAM module to enforce password complexity, with default settings requiring a minimum of 12 characters, including mixed character types, and configurable length parameters via the NSX API. VMware vCenter and ESX environments enforce their own password complexity requirements through VCF SSO authentication policy settings.

The VCF deployment process further ensures compliance by auto-generating passwords for new appliances that meet configured complexity rules. These integrated, automated enforcement mechanisms operate at the point of password creation and change, rejecting any password that does not satisfy the established length and complexity rules.

VMware vDefend extends this capability through its Security Services Platform (SSP), which enforces automated password strength requirements for its local administrative users. SSP mandates specific strength criteria for passwords, including a minimum of 12 characters, a maximum of 128 characters, and the inclusion of uppercase, lowercase, numeric, and special characters.

Token passwords within SSP adhere to a similar complexity model. The SSP also incorporates password lifecycle controls, such as configurable validity periods and expiration notifications, ensuring that new or reset passwords consistently meet the defined strength criteria. These automated enforcement mechanisms collectively provide a robust, platform-wide capability to determine and enforce organization-defined password strength, length, and complexity requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to protect authenticators commensurate with the sensitivity of the information to which use of the authenticator permits access.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust mechanisms to protect authenticators across their lifecycle, ensuring security at rest, in transit, and during use.

Passwords stored on VCF components are secured through one-way hashing with strong algorithms like SHA512 and salting, or encrypted using random keys.

Service-to-service authentication within the platform leverages X.509 certificates, eliminating the need to transmit password-based credentials, while VCF SSO issues time-limited bearer and holder-of-key tokens for secure, short-duration access.

Credential stores, such as those used by the vSphere Web Services SDK, employ filesystem-level permissions and encryption via javax.crypto libraries, and Java keystores are password-protected.

All management communication channels are protected with TLS encryption, safeguarding authenticators during transmission, and VCF supports automatic renewal of TLS certificates to maintain credential currency.

Furthermore, VCF minimizes the storage of user credentials by integrating with external identity providers via SAML and OIDC through VCF SSO and the VCF Identity Broker.

Backup files that may contain credential material are encrypted with a user-specified passphrase, requiring secure storage by the administrator.

VMware vDefend contributes to authenticator protection by securing its own management interface through local account credentials or directory-managed authentication via LDAP, with the Security Services Platform (SSP) enforcing authentication, authorization, and access control.

Its Security Intelligence capability actively detects Credential Access attacks within the monitored environment, providing critical visibility into threats targeting authenticators.

While VCF provides the foundational technical mechanisms for protecting authenticators, customers are responsible for securely configuring these capabilities, such as establishing strong passphrases for backup encryption and ensuring the secure integration and configuration of external identity providers.

The broader enforcement of organizational password storage standards or token lifecycle management across systems outside of VCF's direct management plane remains a customer responsibility.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure that unencrypted, static authenticators are not embedded in applications, scripts or stored on function keys.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation employs robust authentication mechanisms to prevent the embedding of unencrypted, static authenticators. Service-to-service communications leverage certificate-based authentication, while VCF Single Sign-On provides centralized token-based authentication, eliminating the need for individual components to store static credentials. The platform's API SDKs and automation tools support SAML token authentication and session-based authentication, which are the recommended methods for production environments over basic username/password authentication. PowerCLI further enhances security by enforcing PKCE on authorization clients, mitigating credential exposure during authorization code flows. While basic authentication is available for specific interactions like content library publishing, it can be configured to utilize these more secure alternative methods.

VMware Cloud Foundation does not perform scans of customer-developed applications, scripts, or virtual machine workloads for embedded credentials. Organizations are therefore responsible for establishing and enforcing practices within their own code and automation to ensure that unencrypted, static authenticators are not present.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to ensure organization-defined token quality requirements are satisfied for hardware token-based authentication.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure default authenticators are changed as part of account creation or system installation.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation ensures that default authenticators are never utilized. During initial deployment, the VCF Installer Deployment Wizard mandates the specification or auto-generation of unique passwords for every component, including VCF Operations, SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX, and ESX hosts. The deployment process will not proceed unless these unique password configurations are established, thereby preventing any component from operating with shared or factory-default credentials.

Following deployment, SDDC Manager provides centralized password management capabilities, allowing administrators to rotate passwords on demand or schedule automatic rotations. These rotations generate randomized passwords that adhere to the component's defined password policy and automatically re-establish necessary component integrations. The SDDC Manager API further supports scheduling custom rotation intervals for automated workflows. Provider management accounts leverage API access tokens that rotate after each use, preventing token reuse. VCF Operations also offers its own credential rotation function for system-managed credentials, requiring administrator action for individual endpoints.

Separately, the VMware vDefend Security Services Platform Installer requires that default passwords for its admin and audit user accounts be set during deployment according to defined password rules, ensuring default authenticators are replaced at installation time for vDefend's local management plane.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement security safeguards to manage the risk of compromise due to individuals having accounts on multiple Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms to manage the risk associated with individuals having accounts on multiple technology assets, applications, and services. VCF SSO serves as a centralized identity management system, federating identity across core components such as VMware vCenter, NSX Manager, SDDC Manager, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation. This design significantly reduces credential sprawl by offering a single authentication point, thereby mitigating the risk of compromise stemming from the management of numerous independent accounts. Furthermore, VCF SSO supports identity federation with enterprise directory services, enabling organizations to map existing corporate identities into the VCF environment. Users authenticate once against a trusted identity source and receive appropriate access to VCF components based on their roles, eliminating the need for separate local accounts on each system.

For service-to-service communication, VCF supports service accounts with configurable privilege levels, allowing administrators to tailor roles based on security requirements. VCF Operations credential management tracks these credentials across endpoints, ensuring centralized visibility into service account usage. Where local accounts are necessary, such as default accounts on NSX appliances or ESX hosts, VCF ensures they have distinct credentials and defined roles. Administrators can manage local ESX accounts via the HostLocalAccountManager interface, and VCF Operations continuously monitors user security aspects to detect risks and verify appropriate access levels across the environment.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to prohibit the use of cached authenticators after organization-defined time period.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend implement robust automated mechanisms to prohibit the use of cached authenticators after an organization-defined time period. VCF enforces expiration of cached authenticators through token-based authentication with configurable timeouts across all components. VCF SSO issues time-limited tokens, with default expiration periods ranging from 3600 seconds for OAuth access tokens in VCF Operations and Automation, to 90 days for VCF Automation API tokens. vCenter SSO tokens include a `LifetimeType.expires` property for absolute validity windows, and holder-of-key tokens respect these boundaries. For automation workflows, Automation Orchestrator access tokens expire in 3600 seconds, and provider management accounts utilize API access tokens that rotate after each use, preventing reuse even within their validity window.

Similarly, VMware vDefend enforces expiration of cached authenticators via token-based authentication on the Security Services Platform (SSP) management plane. SSP issues time-limited tokens for all management API access, with a default lifetime of 30 minutes and a configurable range up to 1440 minutes. Upon token expiration, the session is invalidated, requiring re-authentication. SSP subsystems, such as Security Intelligence, enforce their own token expiration policies, with threat metadata feed access tokens expiring after 3600 seconds and sensor onboarding registration tokens valid for only 30 minutes. SSP tracks token status (Available, Expired, Fully Used) to prevent reuse and allows administrators to delete expired tokens. Both platforms also provide manual mechanisms for administrators to invalidate tokens before their scheduled expiration.

While the platforms provide comprehensive automated mechanisms and configurable parameters for authenticator expiration, the organization is responsible for defining the specific time periods that align with their security policies and risk posture. This includes configuring the default token lifetimes provided by VCF and vDefend to meet organizational requirements. Additionally, maintaining system time synchronization across the environment is essential for the correct functioning of Kerberos authentication and accurate token expiration.

Description: Mechanisms exist to protect and store passwords via a password manager tool.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust, built-in password management capabilities specifically for its infrastructure service accounts across the VCF fleet. Through the Security > Password Management interface, administrators can centrally view, remediate, and manage service account passwords for critical components such as ESX hosts, VMware vCenter, and NSX. All passwords stored within VCF components are encrypted, utilizing strong mechanisms like one-way hashing algorithms with salts for NSX, and random keys in the local file system for other credentials, ensuring their protection.
