VMware vDefend complements this by requiring existing Admin privileges to manage user accounts and assign roles within the Security Services Platform, thereby gating role assignment behind an already privileged account. It supports integration with enterprise identity directories for user account management, allowing organizations to leverage existing identity governance tooling.

While VCF and vDefend provide the technical controls for scoping, restricting, and auditing privileged access, the establishment, enforcement, and recording of the management approval workflow for granting these privileges remain an organizational responsibility. Neither platform natively enforces or records the management approval process itself; this must be governed by customer-defined policies and external identity governance tooling.

Description: Mechanisms exist to audit the execution of privileged functions.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for auditing privileged function execution across its management components. vCenter Server includes a privilege checks recorder that monitors and records which privileges are checked during operations. This recorder allows administrators to query recordings by managed object ID, request operation ID, principal, privilege name, and session identifier through the PrivilegeChecks API, providing granular detail on invoked privileged operations, the invoking principal, and affected objects.

VCF Operations further enhances auditing with User Permissions Audit reports, detailing user permissions, access groups, and privileges, available for download. Its System Audit capability tracks changes to objects, metrics, applications, and custom groups. VCF Operations also generates structured audit events across categories such as Access, Access_control, Account_management, Configuration, Data_access, Permissions, Session, and System, creating a detailed record of privileged activity.

VMware Live Recovery generates audit events for privileged operations within the disaster recovery environment. Permission-related events are recorded during the configuration of virtual machine protection, creation or modification of protection groups, and execution of recovery plans. These events capture user identity, the action performed, and affected objects.

VLR's event system specifically records operations requiring elevated privileges, including recovery plan execution, reprotect operations, and administrative configuration changes. Events are posted to the vCenter event log on both the protected and recovery sites, establishing a dual-site audit trail for cross-site privileged operations.

VMware vDefend provides robust, built-in mechanisms for auditing privileged functions within its security platform components. The Security Services Platform (SSP) logs all access events in audit logs that are enabled by default and cannot be disabled, ensuring a mandatory and tamper-resistant record of privileged operations.

SSP includes predefined local user accounts: an admin account with the enterprise_admin role for full management access, and a dedicated audit account with the auditor role, providing full access to APIs and services specifically for system auditing. This separation allows audit personnel to review privileged activity without requiring administrative privileges.

SSP implements role-based access control with an Auditor role specifically designed to view information and track activity without the ability to make changes. For SSP operations, the Auditor role has Read access to Deploy and Manage, Upgrade, Authentication and Authorization, Server Configuration, and Backup and Restore functions. For Security Intelligence operations, the Auditor role has Read permissions for most operations and Full Access for export-related functions, enabling auditors to extract audit data for external analysis without modifying platform configuration. These capabilities provide built-in, always-on audit logging and a dedicated auditor role within the vDefend security platform.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions to include disabling, circumventing or altering implemented security safeguards / countermeasures.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements robust privilege separation across all management components through a comprehensive role-based access control (RBAC) model. This ensures users can only perform actions explicitly permitted by their assigned roles, with privileges granted exclusively to authenticated users or groups. vCenter's authorization model provides fine-grained control, validating caller privileges against target objects for every API call and UI action. Administrators can define custom roles with the principle of least privilege, restricting specific operations such as cryptographic functions or ESX Agent Manager actions to only those explicitly authorized. For Kubernetes workloads, vSphere Namespaces map to Kubernetes ClusterRoles, limiting namespace-level operations to authorized users, with authentication managed by VCF SSO or external OpenID Connect providers. The platform also provides tools, such as the privilege checks recorder, to assist in generating roles with the minimum required privileges for specific workflows.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) extends this enforcement to disaster recovery operations, prohibiting non-privileged users from executing privileged DR functions through its own role-based permission model. Specific administrative roles are mandated for critical actions like creating or modifying protection groups, executing recovery plans, or performing reprotect operations. Cross-site operations further enhance security by requiring dual authorization, ensuring appropriate privileges on both protected and recovery site vCenter instances.

VMware vDefend enforces privilege separation across its management interfaces, utilizing RBAC with distinct privilege levels (e.g., Enterprise Administrator, Security Engineer, Security Operator) to gate access to security services. Operations such as managing Malware Prevention Service or running Rule Analysis are restricted based on these roles, preventing unauthorized alteration of security configurations or disabling of protective controls. The vDefend Distributed Firewall includes policy locking mechanisms, adding an extra layer of protection to prevent inadvertent modification of critical security policies, even by authorized users. Furthermore, vDefend's Distributed Firewall and Gateway Firewall provide infrastructure-level controls that prevent workloads and users from circumventing network-level security safeguards by enforcing segmentation and access policies at the vNIC level, independent of the workload's own privilege model.

The platform provides the comprehensive technical mechanisms to prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions, including those that could disable, circumvent, or alter security safeguards. Customers are responsible for defining and assigning roles based on the principle of least privilege, configuring specific security policies, and establishing governance to ensure these powerful capabilities are utilized effectively within their environment.

Description: Mechanisms exist to authorize remote access to perform privileged commands on critical Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) or where sensitive/regulated data is stored, transmitted and/or processed only for compelling operational needs.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust, granular privilege-based access controls that restrict who can perform privileged commands remotely across the entire SDDC. VCF SSO centrally authenticates all administrative access, enforcing role-based access control (RBAC) across vCenter, NSX, VCF Operations, and other core components. Privilege requirements are consistently applied to system objects regardless of the access method, whether through the vSphere Client, CLI, or SDK. Administrators can define custom roles by cloning built-in roles and adjusting individual privileges, enabling precise control over specific operations such as cryptographic tasks, cluster configuration, or fabric host management. VCF Operations further enforces privilege-based access control, preventing unauthorized users from viewing collected data, and provides visibility into privilege exercise through filterable checks by managed object, principal, session, and operation ID. VCF supports integration with external identity providers through identity federation, enabling multi-factor authentication for administrative sessions, thereby strengthening the authorization process for remote access. VMware vDefend complements these capabilities by enabling network-level policies that restrict which source addresses or network segments are permitted to reach management interfaces used for privileged command execution, effectively limiting remote administrative access to authorized network paths. The Security Services Platform CLI and UI require explicit role-based privileges, such as Admin privileges for Bare Metal Server onboarding and inventory verification, ensuring that even when network access is permitted, only authorized roles can execute privileged operations.

While VCF and vDefend provide the essential technical mechanisms for controlling and restricting remote privileged access, the definition of what constitutes a "compelling operational need" and the overarching organizational policy for authorizing remote access based on such needs remain the responsibility of the customer. Organizations must establish and enforce these policies, ensuring that Bare Metal server onboarding, which requires sudo privileges and SSH access, is restricted to approved personnel and justified operational needs via complementary access management controls outside of the platform's direct enforcement.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to prevent applications from executing at higher privilege levels than the user's privileges.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) incorporates automated privilege checks that inherently prevent applications and users from executing operations beyond their assigned permissions. Privileges are system-defined and immutable, ensuring client applications cannot alter or escalate them. Every API call, CLI command, and UI action undergoes a rigorous privilege check against the caller's permissions on the target object prior to execution, irrespective of the access method. This comprehensive authorization model mandates that all SDK-based applications and automated scripts operate under roles containing only the minimum necessary privileges for their specific functions.

The platform further enhances this through features like ESX Agent Manager, which provides an additional layer of privilege gating for agent virtual machines, requiring specific privileges like EAM.Modify to prevent unauthorized disruption. VCF Operations similarly enforces privilege-based access control, blocking users without appropriate permissions from viewing data outside their scope. While administrators are empowered to create custom roles and leverage privilege check recordings to generate workflow-specific roles, and explicit guidance advises against granting administrator privileges to automated scripts, these actions serve to configure and optimize the inherent, automated privilege enforcement mechanisms provided by VCF.

Description: Mechanisms exist to enforce a limit for consecutive invalid login attempts by a user during an organization-defined time period and automatically locks the account when the maximum number of unsuccessful attempts is exceeded.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms to enforce account lockout policies across its management components.

VCF Operations automatically locks accounts after a configurable number of failed login attempts, with a default threshold of seven attempts. For VCF Operations for Networks, the system locks accounts after five consecutive failed attempts for 15 minutes, with subsequent failures extending the lockout duration by an additional 15 minutes, up to a maximum of one day. The super administrator account lockout policy is activated by default and cannot be deactivated.

VCF Automation supports account lockout after a configurable number of invalid login attempts when enabled through its password policy system settings. NSX Manager further supports configurable lockout periods and maximum authentication failure thresholds, accessible via CLI commands, with default lockout and reset periods set to 900 seconds.

While the platform offers extensive configurability for these lockout settings, administrators are responsible for defining the specific maximum number of unsuccessful attempts and the corresponding lockout durations to align with their organization's security policies. Additionally, administrators must ensure that account lockout is enabled within VCF Automation's password policy system settings and manage the manual unlocking of accounts as per operational procedures.

Description: Mechanisms exist to limit the number of concurrent sessions for each system account.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides mechanisms to limit concurrent sessions across its management components, exhibiting varying granularity. VCF Operations allows administrators to configure the number of concurrent UI login sessions permitted per user, with these settings taking effect for subsequent logins. VCF Operations for Networks enforces a maximum of 100 unique active sessions. NSX Manager controls API session concurrency with a per-client limit of 40 concurrent connections and a global limit of 199, where the per-client limit is adjustable. vSphere offers a configurable maximum session count through the `config.vmacore.soap.maxSessionCount` property, which can be set via `vpx.conf` or the vSphere Client.

While VCF Operations directly addresses per-user session limits for its UI, the per-client and global limits in NSX and vSphere provide session bounding at the connection level rather than strictly per user account. Organizations requiring strict per-user session limits consistently across all VCF components must supplement these built-in controls with session policies enforced at the identity provider level.

Description: Mechanisms exist to initiate a session lock after an organization-defined time period of inactivity, or upon receiving a request from a user and retain the session lock until the user reestablishes access using established identification and authentication methods.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust session management capabilities across its management interfaces, including configurable user session timeouts for VCF Operations for Networks and NSX Manager. These mechanisms initiate automatic termination of idle sessions after an organization-defined period, requiring users to re-authenticate to establish a new session. For API-based access, the vSphere Web Services API employs SessionManager to control session lifetime and visibility, utilizing session IDs as temporary authentication tokens. VCF PowerCLI sessions also support configurable web operation timeout durations at the session scope.

However, VCF does not implement a session lock-and-resume pattern; instead, it terminates idle sessions, necessitating a full re-authentication process rather than unlocking a preserved session state. To fully meet the intent of initiating and retaining a session lock, organizations must implement and enforce workstation-level screen lock policies in conjunction with VCF's session timeout settings.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement pattern-hiding displays to conceal information previously visible on the display during the session lock.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to log out users, both locally on the network and for remote sessions, at the end of the session or after an organization-defined period of inactivity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust automated mechanisms for user session termination across its management interfaces, addressing both local and remote sessions.

vCenter Server, NSX Manager, VCF Automation's Provider Management Portal, and VCF Operations for Networks all incorporate configurable inactivity timeouts. These timeouts can be adjusted to meet specific organizational policies, with vCenter defaulting to 30 minutes and VCF Operations for Networks offering a range from 15 minutes to 24 hours. The platform's SessionManager tracks session lifetimes, enforcing timeouts for both interactive UI sessions and API connections, and invalidates authentication tokens upon session termination. Additionally, explicit logout methods are available at the API layer through the vSphere Web Services API and the VCF Operations PowerShell plug-in, allowing for programmatic session termination.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the comprehensive technical capabilities for automated session termination, the organization is responsible for defining the specific period of inactivity that aligns with its security policy and for configuring these platform mechanisms accordingly.

Description: Mechanisms exist to provide a logout capability and display an explicit logout message to users indicating the reliable termination of the session.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for session termination across its management interfaces and APIs.

Users can initiate logout through web interfaces for vCenter, NSX Manager, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation.

Programmatically, the vSphere Web Services API offers a Logout method via the SessionManager to close authenticated connections, and the VCF Operations CLI includes a logout command for interactive sessions. The govmomi Go SDK also exposes login and logout abstractions through its SessionManager.

NSX Manager further enhances session management by generating audit log messages for both login and logout operations, capturing essential details such as username, source IP, operation type, and status, thereby confirming that session termination events are tracked for organizational monitoring.

While VCF's logout functions reliably terminate sessions and invalidate tokens, the consistent display of an explicit "session terminated" confirmation message to the user across all management interfaces is not uniformly documented. Although VCF supports login banners and user agreement messages, these are focused on pre-authentication notifications and do not address the requirement for a post-logout confirmation. Organizations must verify the specific logout message behavior in each management interface during deployment validation to ensure compliance with the explicit notification requirement.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify and document the supporting rationale for specific user actions that can be performed on a system without identification or authentication.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement a reference monitor that is tamperproof, always-invoked, small enough to be subject to analysis / testing and the completeness of which can be assured.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements a robust reference monitor architecture through the ESX hypervisor, which mediates all access requests between virtual machines and underlying hardware resources. This hypervisor is always invoked, ensuring no workload can bypass it to directly access hardware. Its tamperproof nature is established through Secure Boot, UEFI firmware validation, and hardware Trusted Platform Modules (TPM) that provide boot-time measurements and support attestation workflows. The ESX hypervisor is a purpose-built, thin software layer, designed to be small enough for thorough analysis and testing, with its completeness assured through prescriptive hardening guidance found in the vSphere Security Configuration Guide and DISA STIG.

VMware vDefend extends this reference monitor concept to the network security domain. The Distributed Firewall operates within the hypervisor kernel at the virtual network interface (vNIC) level, acting as an inline, always-invoked component that cannot be bypassed by guest workloads. It inherits the tamperproof properties of the ESX hypervisor, preventing guest modification or circumvention, with policy changes requiring authenticated administrative access through the NSX Manager. Similarly, the vDefend Gateway Firewall provides an analogous reference monitor function at the network perimeter, mediating all north-south traffic. Together, these components provide always-invoked, tamperproof access mediation for both east-west and north-south traffic flows, ensuring comprehensive network security enforcement.

Description: Mechanisms exist to verify the identity of a user before issuing authenticators or modifying access permissions.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements comprehensive mechanisms to verify user identity before issuing authenticators or modifying access permissions. Centralized authentication via VCF SSO validates user identity against configured identity sources prior to issuing session tokens or granting access. This process includes proper certificate validation for all identity sources, ensuring identity verification occurs through trusted channels before authentication tokens or permissions are provisioned. VCF extends this capability through federation with external identity providers using SAML via the VCF Identity Broker, enabling organizations to integrate with shared identity providers that can enforce multifactor authentication (MFA) and provide automatic account synchronization, thereby strengthening identity verification before any credentials are issued. Each user account is uniquely identified, and valid session identifiers are required to establish trust with API endpoints.

Access permissions within VCF are managed through a robust role-based authorization model. The AuthorizationManager associates privileges with principals through their assigned roles, and permissions on managed objects can only be set or updated via the AuthorizationManager API after the calling user has been successfully authenticated and their identity verified. This design ensures that even if an identity provider associates a user with a system-level role, the user has no inherent rights until appropriate permissions are explicitly granted, preventing default access escalation. All authentication events and privilege checks are meticulously captured in VCF Operations audit reports, providing comprehensive traceability of identity validation actions and access grants, filterable by object IDs, principals, privileges, and session identifiers.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure management approvals are required for new accounts or changes in permissions to existing accounts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust technical capabilities that support management approval workflows for account creation and permission changes. VCF Automation includes built-in approval policy capabilities designed to enforce management review before changes take effect. These policies can be configured with multiple approvers, requiring all designated approvers to approve an action before its execution.

Role-based access control (RBAC) further restricts account creation and permission assignment to authorized administrators, ensuring that only privileged users can initiate such changes. VCF Automation also integrates with external ITSM platforms, such as ServiceNow, through a plug-in that facilitates routing account and permission change requests through an organization's standard change management process.

Additionally, VCF Operations offers an access request approval workflow for the Automation Portal, where OAuth token access requests undergo review and approval. All user account changes, including modifications to local user accounts, are meticulously logged by NSX Manager, providing a comprehensive audit trail.

While VCF delivers the technical enforcement layer and approval workflow tooling, it does not include a dedicated user access review or identity governance console. Organizations are responsible for configuring the specific approval policies, defining the management approvers, and integrating VCF's identity infrastructure with external Identity and Access Management (IAM) or ITSM platforms to implement the full management approval lifecycle for account and permission requests.

Description: Mechanisms exist to require evidence of individual identification to be presented to the registration authority.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) contributes to the enforcement of identity evidence requirements by securely integrating with external identity providers (IdPs) using industry-standard protocols such as SAML and OIDC. VCF delegates user authentication to these external IdPs, which are responsible for the initial identity proofing, credential issuance, and the verification of evidence of individual identity before accounts are provisioned or access is granted.

VCF establishes and maintains trust with these external IdPs through rigorous certificate validation, including SHA256 fingerprint verification during OIDC registration with NSX Manager, and requires metadata for non-publicly accessible providers, ensuring that only identity assertions from verified and trusted sources are accepted. The vSphere Supervisor platform, utilizing Pinniped, inspects upstream ID token claims to validate user identity, thereby extending the chain of trust from the IdP's verification process through to the VCF session.

Account creation within VCF is restricted to System Administrators, and role mappings, such as "Defer to Identity Provider," necessitate an exact, case-sensitive match between roles defined by the organization and those supplied by the identity provider, preventing unauthorized assignments.

While VCF provides the essential technical integration points and trust validation mechanisms, the formal process of an individual presenting evidence of their identity to a registration authority for initial proofing remains an organizational responsibility. This critical identity verification step, including the definition of policies and procedures for identity proofing and credential issuance, must be implemented and managed by the customer's HR and identity management teams within their chosen external identity provider.

Description: Mechanisms exist to require that the presented identity evidence be validated and verified through organizational-defined methods of validation and verification.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for identity evidence validation and verification by integrating with and delegating authentication to external identity providers. VCF supports federation using SAML and OIDC, ensuring that user authentication, identity proofing, credential issuance, and evidence-of-identity requirements are handled by the organization's trusted identity management system before access to VCF is granted. Trust between VCF and these external providers is established through rigorous certificate validation, requiring administrators to verify certificate thumbprints or provide XML metadata for non-public CAs, thereby ensuring VCF only accepts assertions from verified sources. The vSphere Supervisor platform further enhances this by utilizing Pinniped to inspect upstream OIDC ID token claims, validating username claims and establishing a chain of trust from the identity provider's verification process through to the VCF session. Within VCF, account creation is strictly limited to System Administrators, and the "Defer to Identity Provider" role mandates an exact, case-sensitive match between roles or groups supplied by the identity provider and those defined within the organization, preventing unauthorized role mappings.

While VCF provides the technical integration and enforcement points, the formal identity proofing process, where an individual presents evidence of their identity to a registration authority, remains an organizational responsibility. This process is typically managed by HR and identity management teams utilizing the external identity provider. Organizations are responsible for defining their specific methods of identity validation and verification and for enforcing their own identity verification policies, including multifactor authentication and credential validation, as prerequisites for VCF access.

Description: Mechanisms exist to require that the validation and verification of identity evidence be conducted in person before a designated registration authority.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to require that a notice of proofing be delivered through an out-of-band channel to verify the user's address (physical or digital).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to enforce Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) for policy-driven, dynamic authorizations that supports the secure sharing of information.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides mechanisms that contribute to attribute-based access control through its robust tagging and policy infrastructure. While the platform's primary authorization model is role-based access control (RBAC), vCenter's authorization system enables granular control by filtering privilege checks based on objects, operation IDs, principals, privileges, and sessions. Access control lists (ACLs) further define user access to specific entities. Tags and categories serve as attribute-like mechanisms, allowing administrators to create tag-based policies for security, compliance, and operational automation. This enables tag-based delegation, assigning management responsibilities based on object attributes rather than static role membership alone, thereby offering a functional approximation of attribute-based policy enforcement within the platform.

Similarly, VMware vDefend contributes to attribute-based access control concepts, particularly within its Distributed Firewall and Security Services Platform. Firewall policy groups can leverage dynamic membership criteria, such as tags, VM attributes, and workload characteristics, to drive policy enforcement in a manner analogous to ABAC. The Security Services Platform also implements RBAC with defined roles, supporting role assignments to directory-managed user accounts, which facilitates attribute-driven authorization for vDefend's own management plane.

Despite these capabilities, neither VCF nor vDefend provides a full, general-purpose ABAC engine for enterprise applications or broader data resources. The comprehensive implementation of attribute-based access control, where authorization decisions are driven dynamically by evaluated attributes of the subject, resource, action, and environment for secure information sharing across the enterprise, necessitates organizational policy definitions and may involve integration with external policy decision points. Dynamic, policy-driven access decisions for broader information sharing remain dependent on identity and access management platforms and policy engines external to VCF and vDefend.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to utilize Machine Learning (ML) to make real-time access decisions based on advanced network analytics that leverages enterprise-wide data sources.
Response:
VMware vDefend provides automated mechanisms that utilize machine learning and advanced network analytics to inform and enforce real-time network-level access decisions.

vDefend Network Detection and Response (NDR) employs machine learning and advanced analytics to identify security threats by continuously analyzing network traffic patterns and correlating signals from multiple detection sources across the environment. vDefend Advanced Threat Prevention (ATP) Network Traffic Analysis (NTA) uses machine learning algorithms and advanced statistical techniques to establish baselines of normal network activities, monitoring for anomalous or malicious traffic. When such activity is detected, IDS/IPS policies can automatically drop or reject the offending traffic, making real-time enforcement decisions informed by ML analysis.

Security Intelligence further leverages a proprietary machine-learning algorithm to classify compute entities and analyze traffic flows, providing ML-based firewall rule recommendations that can be published as Distributed Firewall rules, translating ML-driven traffic analysis into enforceable access policies. It also offers real-time application flow discovery and visibility, providing the analytical foundation for informed access control decisions.

While vDefend's machine learning capabilities operate at the network layer, contributing directly to real-time access decisions by analyzing network traffic patterns, detecting anomalies, and enforcing or recommending firewall rules based on observed behavior, the customer remains responsible for defining and implementing the specific access policies and publishing the ML-driven recommendations as Distributed Firewall rules. The platform provides the intelligence and mechanisms, but the ultimate policy configuration and operationalization of these real-time access decisions at the infrastructure level require customer action.

Description: Mechanisms exist to develop access profile rules for sensitive/regulated Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD) access based on User, Data, Network, Environment & Device attributes.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for developing access profile rules based on user, network, and application attributes. Role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced across VCF components, including vCenter, NSX, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation, allowing for granular permissions tied to user identities and roles. NSX further supports attribute-aware network policy through context profiles and Layer 7 access profiles, enabling firewall rules to leverage attributes such as App ID, Custom URL, Domain Name, URL Category, and URL Reputation. VMware vDefend enhances this capability with its Gateway Firewall and Distributed IDS/IPS, which allow for the creation of security profiles attached to firewall rules, applying differentiated inspection and enforcement based on network and application attributes.

While VCF natively provides capabilities for user, network, and application-level attributes, the development of comprehensive access profile rules incorporating all five attribute categories—User, Data, Network, Environment, and Device—requires integration with external identity governance and policy management systems. Specifically, data classification and device posture attributes depend on external tooling. The organization is responsible for defining the specific access profile rules, configuring the native VCF and vDefend mechanisms, and integrating external systems to achieve full attribute-based access control across all specified categories.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement and govern processes and documentation to facilitate an organization-wide response capability for cybersecurity and data protection-related incidents.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the technical infrastructure and operational tools that support various phases of incident response.

VCF Operations includes a dedicated interface for creating, managing, and tracking incidents, complete with metadata such as status, impact level, urgency, and affected entities. It integrates with existing ITSM workflows, allowing incidents to flow into systems like ServiceNow with structured attributes.

For detection, VCF correlates events into faults, enables configurable notification rules, and leverages vCenter Server alarms for automated alerting. Recovery capabilities include vSphere HA for automatic VM restarts and Proactive HA for workload evacuation.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities by providing structured data protection incident response through documented, repeatable recovery plans, non-disruptive testing, and automated failover orchestration. It detects data protection incidents via event logging and alarms, and generates history reports for post-incident review.

VMware vDefend further contributes by offering integrated threat detection, investigation, correlation, and remediation within the network security layer. Its Security Services Platform correlates individual threat events into multi-stage attack campaigns, aggregates detection data across sites, and assigns impact scores for prioritization. Security Intelligence identifies suspicious traffic and potential tampering. For remediation, vDefend Intelligent Assist provides AI-guided response workflows to create targeted or comprehensive remediation policies, which undergo human review and approval before immediate network-level containment via the Distributed Firewall.

These platforms collectively provide robust technical mechanisms for incident detection, tracking, investigation, containment, recovery, and remediation.

However, these platforms do not govern the overarching organization-wide incident response processes, define incident response policies, or maintain comprehensive incident response documentation.

The establishment of an organization-wide response capability, including the definition of policies, procedures, human accountability, and the integration of these technical capabilities into a holistic governance framework, remains the responsibility of the customer.

VMware Cloud Foundation, in conjunction with VMware Live Recovery and VMware vDefend, provides robust technical mechanisms supporting multiple phases of incident handling. Automated event detection is delivered through VCF Operations, which correlates infrastructure events into faults, ranks them by severity, and triggers notifications based on state changes. vCenter Server alarms further enhance detection by executing scripts, sending SNMP traps, emails, or API calls when thresholds are exceeded. vDefend's Security Intelligence actively detects and prioritizes suspicious traffic events, contributing to both detection and analysis.

For analysis, VCF Operations offers a Troubleshooting Incidents page to manage incidents, identify root causes, and track resolution, complemented by Diagnostic Findings that match log patterns against a library of rules. Log Assist streamlines support by generating log bundles with diagnostic findings, and VCF Operations integrates with ServiceNow for structured incident triage.

Containment capabilities are provided by vCenter Server through VM power state changes and network reconfiguration. vDefend extends this with targeted quarantine policies that automatically isolate VMs based on security events while preserving forensic access, and its Intelligent Assist automates remediation strategies for containment and eradication.

Recovery is comprehensively addressed by vSphere HA, which automatically restarts VMs on healthy hosts, and Proactive HA, which evacuates workloads from degrading components. vSphere Fault Tolerance ensures zero-downtime protection through synchronous replication. VMware Live Site Recovery provides structured incident handling for disaster recovery, orchestrating automated failover through recovery plans that define workload recovery sequences, network reconfiguration, and custom scripts, supporting both planned migrations and disaster recovery failovers. Recovery plan testing allows for validation of these procedures without impacting production.

Despite these extensive technical capabilities, the full scope of incident handling requires customer-defined organizational preparation, process governance, and the establishment of manual incident report intake mechanisms, which fall outside the platform's direct scope. While vDefend automates many remediation actions, certain recovery activities, particularly those involving cleanup of objects generated during Intelligent Assist remediation, may necessitate human intervention. The comprehensive recovery phase ultimately relies on the customer's overarching organizational processes and complementary tooling.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to support the incident handling process.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust set of automated mechanisms that support incident handling processes across the infrastructure layer.

The platform includes an integrated incident management interface for creating, tracking, and resolving incidents, complete with configurable priority and impact settings. Alert automation detects critical conditions and triggers automated responses, such as sending notifications, executing predefined remediation actions, and creating incidents in external ticketing systems via the Service-Now Notification Plug-In. Operators can leverage a library of recommendations with automated actions to resolve specific alert conditions. Furthermore, VCF Operations supports automated job scheduling for maintenance tasks, logging all action executions with detailed results for post-incident review.

vCenter Server alarms offer another layer of automated incident support, allowing administrators to configure alarms that trigger automated actions like script execution, SNMP traps, email notifications, and API calls, with multi-level triggers and escalation paths to scale responses based on severity.

For containment and recovery, vSphere High Availability delivers automated failover with admission control policies, while Proactive HA integrates with hardware vendor monitoring to detect degraded components and evacuate virtual machines before failures occur.

VMware Live Recovery extends this support to disaster recovery scenarios by providing automated workflows for failover, test recovery, cleanup, reprotect, and planned migration operations, all accessible programmatically through a scripting API for integration with broader automation frameworks. Recovery plans within VLR execute automated step sequences, including VM power-on ordering, network reconfiguration, and custom command execution without manual intervention, and validate recovery plan states prior to execution.

VMware vDefend contributes significant automated capabilities for network security incident handling, encompassing detection, triage, remediation, and event tracking. Its IDS/IPS continuously monitors east-west traffic for attack signatures and anomalies, automatically generating alerts.

Malware Prevention performs automated file and process analysis via network sandboxing, providing detailed verdicts. Security Intelligence runs automated flow clustering to identify suspicious traffic patterns, categorizing events by MITRE ATT&CK tactics. The Security Services Platform automatically correlates related events, linking multi-stage attacks across different indicators.

Intelligent Assist accelerates incident triage by providing context and guiding remediation strategy selection, creating Distributed Firewall rules with human review and approval, and supporting policy handoff between teams. It also offers event management capabilities for tracking investigation status and suppressing false positives, while collecting and storing related IDS events to build a complete picture of an attack.

While the platform provides the foundational technology for automated detection, alerting, tracking, response, and recovery, the comprehensive incident handling process requires the customer to define organizational procedures, establish detailed response plans, integrate with external SIEM solutions, and ensure personnel are adequately trained to leverage these automated capabilities effectively.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement and govern an insider threat program.
Response:
VMware vDefend delivers advanced security intelligence and Network Detection and Response (NDR) capabilities that are instrumental in identifying potential insider threat indicators. The platform performs comprehensive traffic flow analysis, anomaly detection, and behavioral analysis to surface suspicious activities such as unusual lateral movement or data exfiltration patterns across the network. All IDS/IPS alerts are meticulously logged, providing a critical evidence trail for post-incident investigations into potential insider activity. Additionally, the Intelligent Assist feature supports the selection of appropriate remediation strategies, enabling either targeted or comprehensive responses to identified suspicious behavior.

While these technical mechanisms significantly contribute to the implementation aspects of an insider threat program, the establishment and governance of a holistic program, including the definition of organizational policies, operational procedures, and assigning human accountability for threat response and management, remain the customer's responsibility.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to dynamically reconfigure system components as part of the incident response capability.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides extensive automated mechanisms to dynamically reconfigure system components as part of incident response. vCenter Server supports programmatic reconfiguration of virtual machines through its API, allowing automated changes to compute, storage, and network settings. vCenter alarms can trigger automated actions, including script execution and API calls, to initiate reconfiguration workflows. VCF Operations detects conditions through alert automation and can trigger remediation actions, leveraging Automation Orchestrator with both out-of-the-box and custom workflows to reconfigure infrastructure components. Configuration templates and drift tracking further enable identification and reversion of unintended changes.

For storage, vSAN automatically regulates reconfiguration pace and performs reprotection. vSphere HA and Proactive HA provide automated workload migration and restart on healthy hosts, dynamically redistributing workloads in response to incidents. VMware Live Recovery dynamically reconfigures network, compute, and storage resources during recovery operations, utilizing tools like DR IP Customizer and inventory mappings for automated failover. VMware vDefend provides automated mechanisms to dynamically reconfigure network security components through Intelligent Assist, which automatically generates new security policies (Distributed Firewall, IDS/IPS) for remediation of detected threats, and Security Intelligence, which generates recommended firewall policies based on observed traffic.

While the platform provides robust automated mechanisms for dynamic reconfiguration across compute, storage, network, and network security, the customer is responsible for defining specific incident response policies, configuring the triggers and parameters for automated workflows within VCF Operations and Orchestrator, and approving the deployment of security policy changes generated by vDefend's Intelligent Assist. The integration of these technical capabilities into a comprehensive and effective incident response capability requires customer-defined processes and human oversight.

Description: Mechanisms exist to identify classes of incidents and actions to take to ensure the continuation of organizational missions and business functions.
Response:
VMware vDefend's Security Intelligence categorizes detected suspicious traffic events by impact type, including Critical, High, Medium, and Low, enabling security teams to prioritize incident triage based on severity. The Network Detection and Response (NDR) component assigns impact scores to campaigns, with higher scores indicating higher priority for triage and response. Detection events are further classified into impact levels, including Informational for scores ranging from 1 to 24. IDS/IPS and Malware Prevention alerts provide additional contextual signals that contribute to incident classification within the platform.

While vDefend provides robust mechanisms for identifying and classifying incidents, the broader incident management and categorization process, including the definition of specific actions to take and the overall strategy for ensuring the continuation of organizational missions and business functions, remains an organizational responsibility governed by customer-defined procedures.

Description: Mechanisms exist to coordinate with approved third-parties to achieve a cross-organization perspective on incident awareness and more effective incident responses.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust technical mechanisms to facilitate coordination with external organizations for incident awareness and response. VCF Operations includes a comprehensive suite of configurable outbound notification plug-ins, such as Standard Email, SNMP Trap, Webhook, Slack, and ServiceNow. These plug-ins are designed to forward critical alert data to external ticketing systems, chat platforms, and monitoring tools utilized by partner organizations or managed security service providers. Granular notification rules allow administrators to precisely filter alerts based on criticality and impact, ensuring that relevant information reaches the appropriate external parties. VCF Operations for Networks further extends these capabilities by sending alerts to platforms like Slack, ServiceNow, and PagerDuty, enhancing coordination channels for network-specific incidents. The Management Pack for ServiceNow offers bidirectional integration, enabling VCF Operations to create ServiceNow incidents from alerts and automatically suspend corresponding VCF alerts when an incident is closed in ServiceNow.

For deeper incident analysis and cross-organization perspective, VCF components support syslog forwarding to external log aggregation and SIEM platforms. ESX hosts, vCenter, vSphere Supervisor, and VCF Operations HCX can all forward logs to external syslog servers, with vSphere Supervisor leveraging Fluent Bit for structured log forwarding in RFC 5424 format. These log feeds provide external partners and service providers with the raw event data necessary for comprehensive cross-organization analysis. Secure outbound communication is supported through webhook notification plug-ins, which include certificate validation and authentication. Payload templates are customizable per plug-in type, simplifying integration with diverse external receiving systems.

While VCF delivers the essential technical infrastructure for external coordination, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing the formal processes for approving third-party organizations. This includes configuring the specific integration points, establishing the cross-organizational incident response policies and procedures, and ensuring that external systems are properly configured to receive, process, and act upon the shared alert and log data.

Description: Mechanisms exist to automatically disable Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS), upon detection of a possible incident that meets organizational criteria, which allows for forensic analysis to be performed.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for automatically disabling or isolating technology assets upon incident detection, while ensuring forensic analysis capabilities are preserved.

vCenter alarms can be configured to trigger automated actions such as powering off, suspending, or isolating virtual machines based on defined conditions. VCF Operations extends this with automated remediation tied to alert definitions, allowing for automatic execution of actions like Power Off VM or Suspend VM, and enabling custom workflows via Automation Orchestrator.

Orchestrator policies can suspend a VM, take a snapshot for forensic preservation, and then isolate the workload from the network without manual intervention.

For forensic integrity, vCenter supports VM snapshots that capture memory, disk, and configuration state, and vSAN offers immutable snapshots that are read-only and cannot be modified or deleted, safeguarding evidence.

VCF Automation for VM Apps further supports programmatic invocation of actions like Power Off, Suspend, and Create Snapshot through its API.

VMware vDefend complements these capabilities by providing automated network-level disabling and isolation.

The Distributed IDS/IPS, when configured in Detect and Prevent mode, automatically blocks or rejects malicious traffic based on signature detection. IDS/IPS rate filters provide automatic escalation to blocking for repeated malicious activity.

The Distributed Firewall and Gateway Firewall include an Emergency rule category for pre-configured quarantine rules that take highest precedence, effectively isolating compromised workloads at the network layer while preserving them for forensic analysis.

Intelligent Assist automates the creation of remediation policies, including DFW rules in the Emergency section, in response to detected security events. The Malware Prevention Service automatically blocks malicious file transfers.

These vDefend capabilities ensure that network access can be automatically disabled or restricted upon incident detection.

While the platform provides these robust automated mechanisms, the organization is responsible for defining the specific criteria that constitute a possible incident, configuring the alarms, alerts, policies, and rules within vCenter, VCF Operations, and vDefend, and establishing the automated actions that align with their incident response plan and forensic requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to define specific Indicators of Compromise (IOC) to identify the signs of potential cybersecurity events.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for defining and utilizing Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) to identify potential cybersecurity events. VCF Operations supports the creation of custom alert definitions, leveraging both metric-based and log-based symptoms to flag suspicious activities, incorporating Smart Early Warning conditions and log event patterns.

NSX enhances this by integrating with threat intelligence feeds, such as the Malicious IP Feed from NTICS, enabling the application of known malicious IP addresses in network policies for automatic blocking. NSX also employs vDPI for deep packet inspection, matching payloads against defined signature patterns.

VMware vDefend further extends these capabilities with full IDS/IPS functionality, including the critical ability to import custom and third-party threat intelligence signatures. Its custom signature bundle management allows organizations to upload, validate, and manage tailored IOC definitions, while NSX Advanced Threat Prevention combines IDS/IPS with network sandboxing and Network Traffic Analysis, aggregated through Network Detection and Response correlation engines.

Furthermore, VCF components like vCenter, NSX, and ESX hosts facilitate the operationalization of IOCs by forwarding syslog data to external SIEM platforms, where advanced IOC matching and correlation typically occur.

While the platform offers robust technical mechanisms for IOC definition and detection, the overarching organizational process for formally defining, sourcing, vetting, and curating an Indicators of Compromise program remains a governance activity that falls outside the direct scope of VMware Cloud Foundation's automated capabilities.

Description: Mechanisms exist to perform digital forensics and maintain the integrity of the chain of custody, in accordance with applicable laws, regulations and industry-recognized secure practices.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides technical capabilities for digital forensic investigations and evidence preservation.

This includes VM snapshots to record memory, disk, and settings state, complemented by vSAN immutable snapshots that create read-only, tamper-proof copies of data. VCF Automation exposes API-driven resource actions like Suspend and Create Snapshot, enabling programmatic preservation within incident response workflows. Detailed audit trails across VCF components, including VCF Operations, VCF Automation, and NSX, capture platform interactions, CRUD operations, login/logout events, and API/UI/CLI actions, providing attributable, timestamped evidence. For centralized preservation, VCF components support TLS-encrypted syslog forwarding with certificate verification to external SIEM platforms, establishing an independent, tamper-resistant record of events.

VMware Live Recovery further supports forensic efforts through audit events for DR operations, support bundle generation for diagnostic data, and historical replication data for anomaly detection. VMware vDefend enhances these capabilities by capturing and storing packet capture (PCAP) files for IDS/IPS and NDR detections, including raw network packets and traffic replay. Malware Prevention generates detailed analysis reports with downloadable screenshots and PCAP files from sandboxing. vDefend's Network Detection and Response correlates individual detections into campaigns, reconstructing attack sequences and scope. Distributed and Gateway Firewalls provide per-rule logging with labels for network access patterns, and Security Intelligence retains IDS/IPS events and flow data for investigative windows. A dedicated Auditor role within the Security Services Platform ensures evidence integrity by allowing review without alteration.

While these platforms provide robust technical mechanisms for evidence generation and preservation, the organization retains direct responsibility for establishing and maintaining formal chain-of-custody procedures, including evidence labeling, transfer records, and ensuring legal admissibility. Compliance with applicable laws, regulations, and industry-recognized secure practices for evidentiary handling and incident response processes remains an organizational obligation.

Description: Mechanisms exist to document, monitor and report the status of cybersecurity and data protection incidents to internal stakeholders all the way through the resolution of the incident.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides technical instrumentation for cybersecurity incident situational awareness, including a Troubleshooting Incidents page to manage, start, and view incidents, with basic status tracking for resolution. It enables alert-driven detection through metric event symptoms and alert definitions with configurable impact levels. VCF integrates with ServiceNow to create structured incident records and synchronize updates, and with SIEM/log management systems via encrypted syslog forwarding for continuous monitoring and data correlation.

VMware Live Recovery contributes by providing situational awareness for disaster recovery, monitoring protection status, site connectivity, and recovery readiness, including replication health metrics.

VMware vDefend delivers network-layer situational awareness through Security Intelligence, detecting and categorizing suspicious traffic by severity, mapping to MITRE ATT&CK, and visualizing entities and traffic flows to assess blast radius and lateral movement. It tracks detected versus prevented events, correlates individual detection events into campaigns for a higher-level view of threat activity, and maintains historical records of IDS/IPS events for analysis. vDefend also logs IDS/IPS alerts for post-incident investigation and generates downloadable reports of detected anomalies.

While these platforms provide robust technical foundations for documenting and monitoring various aspects of incidents, they do not automate the organizational processes required for comprehensive incident management. The customer is responsible for defining and implementing procedures for notifying internal stakeholders, coordinating cross-functional response teams, assigning incident ownership, tracking incident resolution status through a dedicated ticketing system, and producing management reports on overall incident posture. These workflow and communication responsibilities necessitate organizational procedures and often require integration with external incident management (ITSM) or Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) tooling.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to assist in the tracking, collection and analysis of information from actual and potential cybersecurity and data protection incidents.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides automated mechanisms that assist in tracking, collecting, and analyzing information related to cybersecurity incidents. VCF Operations serves as the primary platform, offering centralized monitoring with automated alert generation based on metric event symptoms and threshold violations, presenting these alerts in a dedicated data grid. The Troubleshooting Incidents page allows operators to manage, start, and view incident summaries. VCF Operations collects and organizes data across the infrastructure stack, tracking collectors, objects, and metrics, and aggregates log data from VCF components for centralized analysis. VCF Automation audits policy enforcement and tracks recurring jobs, while the vSphere Web Services API provides programmatic access to historical event and task collections. VCF Operations integrates with external SIEM platforms via syslog forwarding to feed collected data into broader incident analysis workflows.

VMware Live Recovery provides automated mechanisms for data protection incidents through its event logging framework, generating timestamped events for DR operations and configurable alarms. The VLR REST API offers programmatic access to recovery plan history records, capturing per-step results and timing data. The VCF Operations Management Pack extends this by collecting replication health metrics and surfacing replication issues.

VMware vDefend contributes automated mechanisms for cybersecurity incidents at the network layer. Security Intelligence continuously collects network traffic flow data, detects suspicious events, and categorizes them by technique. It provides IDS/IPS event data in a visualization canvas, identifies missing or deleted infrastructure components for change tracking, and offers network detection and response (NDR) capabilities for threat event detection and reporting. The Asset Collections View and detailed traffic flow data graphs provide visual analysis of communication patterns.

While these platforms provide robust automated mechanisms for collecting and analyzing incident-related information, they do not maintain full incident case management records, perform automated root cause analysis workflows, or manage the complete incident response lifecycle. Organizations must integrate these capabilities with external SIEM, SOAR, or dedicated incident management platforms to achieve end-to-end incident tracking and analysis, and to define the overarching policies and processes for incident response.

Description: Mechanisms exist to periodically review incident response activities for the existence of recurring incidents.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation Operations provides a Troubleshooting Incidents page where operators can manage incidents, initiate investigations, and view summaries that highlight top root causes, facilitating the identification of patterns and recurring issues. During investigations, operators can flag metrics, mark root causes, and document observations in a structured manner, supporting subsequent review of past incidents and their outcomes. The timeline tool offers a graphical view to compare triggered symptoms and events over time, aiding in the identification of recurring root causes. Additionally, automated reports can be scheduled to track resources and potential risks, and Automation Central logs the status of failed recurring jobs.

VMware vDefend further enhances these capabilities by continuously monitoring network traffic and detecting suspicious events, with a Suspicious Traffic Summary providing a rolling view of detection activity over configurable periods. Network Detection and Response (NDR) aggregates and correlates detection data into campaigns, enabling analysts to identify recurring threat actor or attack patterns. The IDS/IPS Security Dashboard and Timeline view offer trending analysis and visual identification of recurring intrusion attempts and event clusters. Rule Analysis can be scheduled for recurring execution, generating reports that allow comparison across analysis periods to identify persistent policy anomalies.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for identifying recurring incidents and supporting incident history review, the customer is responsible for establishing and implementing the organizational processes, policies, and human accountability necessary to define the periodicity, conduct the actual periodic reviews of incident response activities, and integrate the insights gained into their incident response refinement procedures.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to timely-report incidents to applicable:
 (1) Internal stakeholders; 
 (2) Affected clients & third-parties; and
 (3) Regulatory authorities.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to assist in the reporting of cybersecurity and data protection incidents.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide extensive automated mechanisms that significantly assist in the reporting of cybersecurity and data protection incidents.

VCF Operations delivers automated alerting and reporting, generating alerts from metric events and threshold violations, which can be configured for automated notifications and forwarded to external SIEM, SOAR, or ticketing systems via syslog and webhooks. It also supports scheduled automated reports in PDF or CSV formats and aggregates log data for broader analysis. vCenter Server further enhances this with automated alarms supporting SNMP traps, email notifications, and API calls, alongside a reports API for downloading pre-check and interoperability reports.

VMware Live Recovery automatically generates detailed recovery plan history reports after each DR operation, capturing execution results and status, which are exportable via the UI or programmatically through its REST API. SNMP traps can also be configured for DR events.

VMware vDefend provides automated detection, analysis, and reporting capabilities, continuously monitoring traffic for anomalies and security posture changes. It generates downloadable CSV reports for detected anomalies, exports segmentation recommendations, and correlates individual threat events into campaigns. Malware Prevention offers dashboards and exportable Process Analysis reports in XML, JSON, and PDF formats, all contributing essential security event data and threat intelligence.

While these platforms provide robust automated data generation, alerting, and initial reporting, they do not independently produce, format, or distribute official incident reports. The formal incident reporting lifecycle, including incident classification, specific notification routing to stakeholders or regulators, and the ultimate generation and distribution of formal incident reports, remains an organizational responsibility. These critical steps are typically handled by external incident management or Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) platforms that consume the rich data, alerts, and exportable reports provided by VCF, VLR, and vDefend.

Description: Mechanisms exist to report sensitive/regulated data incidents in a timely manner.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a comprehensive infrastructure for the timely detection and initial notification of events that may involve sensitive or regulated data. The platform continuously monitors the environment, generating alerts based on configurable thresholds and event definitions.

These alerts can be delivered through multiple outbound channels, including SNMP traps, webhooks, a ServiceNow plug-in for automated incident creation, a Slack plug-in, and email, with notification rules ensuring relevant events reach the appropriate teams without delay. All VCF components support syslog forwarding to external log aggregation and SIEM platforms, providing security operations teams with a centralized view of events.

VCF Operations for Logs further ingests and indexes these events, enabling real-time search, correlation, and alert definition. The platform also supports incident investigation workflows, allowing operators to flag metrics, mark root causes, and track incident status, which aids in assembling the necessary information for incident reports.

While VCF provides the essential detection, correlation, and delivery mechanisms that feed into incident reporting workflows, the ultimate responsibility for defining and executing the organizational processes for actual notification to regulators, affected individuals, or contractual counterparties remains with the customer.

Description: Mechanisms exist to report system vulnerabilities associated with reported cybersecurity and data protection incidents to organization-defined personnel or roles.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for identifying and reporting system vulnerabilities associated with cybersecurity incidents. VCF Operations Diagnostics scans the platform for known issues, including VMSA-based security exposures, providing administrators with visibility into vulnerabilities potentially related to an active incident. VCF Operations generates alerts based on these findings, which can be routed to organization-defined personnel through configurable channels such as SNMP traps, webhooks, ServiceNow, Slack, and email, allowing specific direction of vulnerability-related alerts to security teams or incident responders. The troubleshooting incidents capability within VCF Operations further supports the correlation of vulnerability information with active incidents. VMware vDefend enhances this capability by detecting vulnerabilities in real-time across distributed and gateway enforcement points, providing specific context such as attack type, affected product, CVSS scores, and CVE identifiers within event records. The Security Services Platform Threat Event Monitoring dashboard offers a consolidated view of security issues, enabling security teams to prioritize and investigate vulnerabilities. Security Intelligence reports identify vulnerable workloads and provide actionable recommendations, while the blast radius view connects vulnerability exposure to incident impact.

While the platform provides the technical tools for detecting, correlating, and routing vulnerability information, the customer is responsible for defining the specific organization-defined personnel or roles, establishing the formal organizational process for reporting vulnerabilities, maintaining a vulnerability disclosure workflow, and coordinating response across teams. This includes configuring the notification channels provided by VCF Operations and integrating them into a comprehensive incident management and reporting framework.

Description: Mechanisms exist to provide cybersecurity and data protection incident information to the provider of the Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) and other organizations involved in the supply chain for TAAS related to the incident.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Mechanisms exist to report any serious incident involving the organization's Technology Assets, Applications, Services and/or Data (TAASD) to relevant authorities in the locality where the incident occurred, in accordance with mandatory reporting:
 (1) Requirements; and
 (2) Timelines.

Description: Mechanisms exist to provide incident response advice and assistance to users of Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) for the handling and reporting of actual and potential cybersecurity and data protection incidents.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to increase the availability of incident response-related information and support.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides automated capabilities that significantly increase the availability of incident response-related information and support. VCF Operations includes automated alert management with configurable definitions, symptoms, and recommendations, generating instructions and, where possible, automated actions for resolution. The Troubleshooting Incidents page offers a centralized view for managing and tracking incidents. Integrations with ServiceNow via the Notification Plug-In automatically create incidents with structured data, and the ITSM Plug-in creates support tickets and sends email notifications, ensuring incident information flows without manual intervention.

Log Assist automatically handles intermittent issues and links log bundles to support cases, while syslog forwarding from all VCF components to external SIEM systems provides continuous availability of security event data.

VMware vDefend further enhances this by automatically generating intrusion events from IDS/IPS, displaying them on security dashboards for immediate visibility into the threat landscape. Network Detection and Response (NDR) automates the correlation of individual security events into coherent intrusion campaigns, reducing manual effort and surfacing coordinated threats. Intelligent Assist provides AI-driven incident triage and remediation support, explaining threat context and suggesting remediation policies, with all actions subject to human review. vDefend also exports events directly to syslog, streaming structured event data to external SIEMs and log aggregation platforms.

VMware Live Recovery contributes by providing automation support for disaster recovery operations through recovery plan orchestration and VCF Operations orchestrator workflows, automating failover sequences and routine DR tasks, which are critical components of incident support.

While the platform provides extensive automated mechanisms for information availability and support, the broader incident response program, including staffing, defining escalation procedures, developing playbooks, and ensuring human review and approval for automated remediation actions, remains an organizational responsibility.

Description: Mechanisms exist to establish a direct, cooperative relationship between the organization's incident response capability and external service providers.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust native integration capabilities to establish direct, cooperative relationships with external service providers for incident response. VCF Operations integrates with external ITSM platforms like ServiceNow through a dedicated plug-in, automatically creating incidents from VCF alerts with structured data that maps to the external provider's incident management workflow. This integration supports a bidirectional relationship, allowing VCF's monitoring to interact directly with the external ticketing platform. VCF Automation further extends this by enabling the creation of support tickets from automation workflows and supporting extensibility actions for integration with various enterprise ITSM platforms.

For security monitoring and incident notification, all VCF components support native syslog forwarding to external SIEM systems, providing continuous telemetry to external security service providers. VCF Operations for Networks also supports SNMP trap destinations for alert notification. These outbound notification channels, including webhooks, email, and Slack, enable organizations to establish and maintain cooperative incident response relationships with managed security service providers, SOC teams, and other external partners. Additionally, Log Assist facilitates cooperative troubleshooting by linking log bundles directly to support cases and attaching diagnostic findings for external providers.

While VCF delivers the foundational technical mechanisms for these integrations, the organization is responsible for configuring these capabilities to align with their specific external service providers. This includes defining the precise data mappings, alert routing rules, and establishing the operational processes and agreements that govern the cooperative incident response relationship with each external entity.

Description: Mechanisms exist to respond to sensitive/regulated data spills.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical mechanisms to respond to sensitive or regulated data spills.

At the virtualization layer, VCF offers containment through VM snapshots for evidence preservation and NSX network segments and security groups to isolate affected workloads, with VCF Automation supporting the creation of network profiles for quarantining contaminated systems.

VCF Operations centralizes event data and compliance monitoring, aggregating logs for enterprise SIEM platforms and continuously monitoring infrastructure against policies to detect deviations.

For data protection and recovery, vSAN native snapshots provide local recovery points, while vSphere HA and Fault Tolerance ensure workload continuity during remediation.

Complementing this, VMware vDefend contributes through Security Intelligence, which actively monitors traffic flows to detect unauthorized data movement and leaked flows not covered by existing Distributed Firewall rules. Its Policy Recommendations engine analyzes these leaks and generates modified firewall rules, guiding administrators to implement lockdown protections and contain the spill by restricting communication for affected workloads.

While these platforms provide critical technical tools, the comprehensive response to data spills necessitates organizational policies and procedures. This includes defining legal notification protocols, establishing evidence chain-of-custody, conducting data classification reassessment, managing user communication, and performing broader forensic investigations and regulatory reporting, all of which remain the customer's responsibility.

Description: Mechanisms exist to formally assign personnel or roles with responsibility for responding to sensitive/regulated data spills.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure incident response training material provides coverage for sensitive/regulated data spillage response.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure that organizational personnel impacted by sensitive/regulated data spills can continue to carry out assigned tasks while contaminated Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) are undergoing corrective actions.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for maintaining operational continuity and minimizing disruption to personnel during data spill remediation.
