Description: Mechanisms exist to conduct ongoing “technical debt” reviews of hardware and software technologies to remediate outdated and/or unsupported technologies.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust, integrated lifecycle management capabilities through SDDC Manager and vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM). This functionality ensures that all VCF infrastructure components, including ESXi hosts, vCenter Server, NSX, and vSAN, are maintained at supported versions and patch levels. The platform's design inherently identifies and facilitates the remediation of outdated or unsupported technologies within the VCF stack, thereby directly addressing technical debt for its own managed hardware and software components.

While VCF effectively manages the technical debt of its underlying infrastructure, the organization remains responsible for establishing comprehensive policies and processes for conducting ongoing technical debt reviews across all hardware and software technologies within their broader IT environment. This includes applications, operating systems deployed on virtual machines, and any physical infrastructure not directly managed by VCF. The customer must define the scope of these reviews, set remediation timelines, and assign accountability for addressing identified technical debt outside the VCF infrastructure.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement security functions as a layered structure minimizing interactions between layers of the design and avoiding any dependence by lower layers on the functionality or correctness of higher layers.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements security functions as a robust, layered architecture, ensuring each layer operates independently and lower layers do not depend on the functionality or correctness of higher layers.

At the infrastructure layer, ESX hosts leverage TPM-based measured boot and UEFI Secure Boot, establishing a hardware-rooted chain of trust that functions independently of any software layers above it. The hypervisor and storage layers, through vSAN data-at-rest encryption and VM encryption, protect data and virtual machine contents at the infrastructure level without reliance on application-level security. Network security, provided by NSX, utilizes software-defined segments and a distributed firewall that enforces policies at the hypervisor level, independent of physical network configurations or guest workload cooperation. Identity and access management, communications, and compliance layers further reinforce this design, with the VCF Security Baseline and Operations dashboard providing an independent verification layer above the controls they measure.

For organizations deploying VMware vDefend, this layered independence is further extended within the network security domain.

The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces Layer 2-7 policies at the vNIC, operating without dependence on guest operating system or application-layer correctness. The Deep Packet Inspection engine identifies applications independently of lower-level packet filtering, basing Layer 7 policy decisions on observed behavior. Threat detection, including IDS/IPS and Advanced Threat Prevention, functions as a separate layer, inspecting traffic and file content independently of firewall policies. The segmentation planning layer provides analysis and assessment capabilities that operate above and independently verify the effectiveness of the underlying enforcement layers.

This comprehensive design ensures that a failure or misconfiguration in a higher layer, such as the DPI engine or IDS/IPS signatures, does not compromise the integrity of lower-level firewall enforcement.

Description: Mechanisms exist to partition systems so that partitions reside in separate physical domains or environments.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for partitioning systems into separate physical domains and environments. Workload domains serve as the foundational construct, establishing isolated logical pools of compute, network, and storage infrastructure. Each workload domain supports independent lifecycle management, dedicated identity providers, and can leverage distinct storage backends, including configurations where the vCenter appliance operates on infrastructure physically separate from the resources it manages.

At the network layer, VCF's distributed switch models enable physical air-gapped separation of network traffic, isolating workload traffic to dedicated network fabrics, and extending this to storage traffic with the Storage and Workload Separation model.

Within a workload domain, vSAN fault domains provide rack-level failure isolation by grouping hosts, ensuring data copies are maintained across distinct physical boundaries. For broader geographic separation, clusters can be stretched across availability zones, allowing virtual machines to migrate between physically distinct locations during outages. Furthermore, DRS affinity and anti-affinity rules enforce VM placement, preventing co-location of designated workloads on the same physical hosts, thereby ensuring their separation at the host level.

VMware vDefend augments these physical partitioning capabilities by providing robust network-layer enforcement that isolates workloads into separate security zones and segments, effectively creating distinct environments. The vDefend Distributed Firewall operates at the vNIC level, enforcing microsegmentation policies that partition workloads irrespective of their underlying physical placement. This allows for strict communication restrictions between security zones, such as preventing production environments from interacting with development or testing environments, even when sharing physical compute infrastructure.

The Security Services Platform offers segmentation planning tools to design and implement these partitioning strategies, defining hierarchical scopes (region, zone, environment, application, tier) that map to an organization's physical and logical domain structure, and generating corresponding firewall policies. Segmentation monitoring further ensures that new workloads are correctly placed within their designated partitions.

For multi-site deployments, vDefend Distributed Firewall with Federation extends consistent partitioning enforcement across multiple physical locations and regions, maintaining a unified security posture as workloads traverse geographically distinct domains.

Description: Mechanisms exist to separate user functionality from system management functionality.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation inherently separates user functionality from system management functionality through its foundational architecture and robust access controls. The core separation is established by the management domain and workload domain architecture. The management domain hosts all essential management components, including SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX Manager, and dedicated ESX hosts. Workload domains are deployed independently, providing isolated pools of compute, network, and storage for customer workloads. This structural boundary is enforced by preventing workload domains from sharing the management domain's NSX cluster, and by the dependency order requiring management components to be operational before workload domain components.

For Kubernetes-based workloads, the Supervisor platform further isolates management and workload planes, utilizing dedicated vSphere Zones for the Supervisor control plane and separate vSphere Namespaces for workloads. Network separation is maintained by requiring management and workload networks to reside on distinct subnets, with communication between these layers mediated by a Supervisor management proxy.

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is consistently enforced across all VCF components to ensure management and user separation. NSX RBAC provides granular permissions scoped to individual projects, preventing users from accessing system-level management functions. Specific roles such as Network Admin, Network Operator, Security Admin, and Security Operator are confined to their respective project scopes. VCF Automation defines distinct roles, where only the Assembler Administrator possesses the authority to view and modify infrastructure-level resources like cloud accounts and projects. The management domain model also supports the integration of dedicated identity providers for workload domains, enabling separate authentication mechanisms for management and user-facing environments.

VMware vDefend contributes to application partitioning by enabling the logical organization of application workloads into structured tiers, such as web servers, databases, and application servers, across various hierarchy scopes. This capability facilitates the separation of functional tiers at the network layer through microsegmentation policies. However, the separation of user functionality from system management functionality at the identity and access control level is a core platform capability handled by vCenter/VCF SSO and platform-level RBAC, rather than vDefend's primary scope. The platform provides all necessary mechanisms; customers are responsible for configuring and assigning appropriate roles and permissions based on their operational requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement a separate execution domain for each executing process.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements robust, multi-layered process isolation, establishing separate execution domains across the infrastructure stack for virtual machines, containers, and automation environments. At the hypervisor layer, vSphere isolates each virtual machine with dedicated virtual CPU, memory, and storage resources. The CPU scheduler incorporates hyperthreading controls and CPU affinity settings to mitigate side-channel risks, while memory isolation is enforced through Transparent Page Sharing controls and memory reservation policies. Network isolation at the VM level is achieved via virtual switches and distributed port groups, each with distinct security policies, and port-level configuration overrides are restricted by default.

VCF workload domains extend this isolation to the infrastructure level, providing complete separation of compute, storage, and networking resources across security zones. Within these domains, DRS anti-affinity rules can place specific VMs on separate physical hosts for enhanced isolation, and fault domains provide infrastructure-level isolation within clusters. For containerized workloads, vSphere Namespaces create resource-bounded environments backed by vSphere resource pools, enabling administrators to set resource limits, storage policies, and role-based access controls per namespace, effectively isolating teams and their resources. Network isolation between namespaces is enforced through NSX integration and Private VLANs (PVLANs). VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) delivers CNCF-conformant Kubernetes clusters integrated with the vSphere Supervisor, maintaining consistent security policies for both VM and container workloads.

VCF Automation organizations operate in isolated environments where users access only allocated services and resources, with projects utilizing security groups and firewall rules for network isolation, and NSX Virtual Private Clouds providing self-contained network topologies with independent permission models and IP address management. VM-level isolation settings in VCF Operations, such as controls to disable unauthorized device modification and connection changes, further restrict the execution environment of individual virtual machines.

VMware vDefend further contributes to process isolation by enforcing network-level separation between execution domains and providing in-guest process monitoring. The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation at the virtual network interface card (vNIC) level, creating network boundaries between workloads operating in separate execution domains. This applies granular rules to each workload's network interface, preventing lateral communication unless explicitly permitted, thereby complementing hypervisor-level isolation with a defense-in-depth network layer. Security Services Platform extends this with environment-level segmentation planning, defining block policies between environments like development and production, which translate into Distributed Firewall rules for complete traffic isolation.

Within guest virtual machines, the Guest Introspection framework provides process-level visibility through a context API, generating events based on I/O activities. Malware Prevention leverages this framework to monitor process activities and memory buffers, detecting both file-based and fileless malware, capturing detailed process information including SHA256 hashes, parent process IDs, command line arguments, and full process trees. This monitoring helps detect compromises or attempts to breach isolation boundaries within an execution domain.

Description: Mechanisms exist to isolate security functions from non-security functions.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes robust mechanisms for isolating security functions from non-security functions through architectural, administrative, and network-level separation. The foundational management domain and workload domain architecture physically separates security-critical management components, such as SDDC Manager, vCenter, and NSX Manager, from customer workloads, each residing in their own isolated resource pools and network segments. This structural boundary is reinforced by comprehensive role-based access control (RBAC) across all components, implementing distinct roles for security functions, gating cryptographic operations by specific privileges, and separating security administration from network operations within NSX.

VCF Operations further provides a dedicated Compliance capability, offering security teams a distinct view of the environment's security posture.

At the network layer, VCF Automation and NSX leverage security group isolation, NSX Projects for multi-departmental configuration separation, and on-demand security groups with pre-configured firewall rules to enforce isolation boundaries.

VMware vDefend significantly enhances this isolation through advanced network-layer segmentation and policy enforcement. The Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation at the virtual network interface card (vNIC) level, enabling granular firewall rules that isolate security management components from general workload traffic and prevent unauthorized lateral movement.

The Security Services Platform extends this by enabling environment-based segmentation, allowing administrators to define classifications like production and management, and apply block policies between environments that should not communicate. Environment Protection assessments provide continuous visibility into the enforcement of these separation policies.

Furthermore, vDefend's zero-trust architecture model mandates explicit authorization for all network communication, ensuring that security functions are isolated from non-security functions by default unless specific access is explicitly granted, thereby reducing the risk of unauthorized interaction.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement underlying hardware separation mechanisms to facilitate process separation.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) inherently provides robust hardware separation mechanisms to facilitate process isolation.

The ESX hypervisor establishes hardware-backed isolation between virtual machines, ensuring each VM operates independently with dedicated virtual processors, memory, and devices. This prevents processes within one VM from accessing the address space of another through hardware-enforced CPU and memory isolation. Further granular control over inter-VM interactions and device access is available through specific VM isolation settings.

For workloads demanding the highest level of hardware-rooted process separation, VCF integrates AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization with Encrypted State (SEV-ES), enabling Confidential vSphere Pods to leverage hardware encryption to protect CPU registers from hypervisor access and detect malicious state modifications.

Additionally, VCF supports hardware trust features via Trusted Platform Modules (TPM) and virtual TPMs (vTPM), extending hardware-rooted trust into guest operating systems, and offers network-level separation through distributed switch models, including physically air-gapped configurations for critical traffic isolation.

Customers are responsible for configuring and deploying workloads to effectively utilize these inherent hardware separation capabilities, including enabling features like AMD SEV-ES for confidential workloads, configuring appropriate VM isolation settings, and implementing network segmentation strategies to align with their specific security requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to maintain a separate execution domain for each thread in multi-threaded processing.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust foundation for maintaining separate execution domains by enforcing isolation at the virtual machine level. The ESX hypervisor assigns each virtual machine its own virtual CPUs, memory, and devices, managing vCPU-to-physical-core placement and preventing interference between VMs.

VCF Operations provides visibility into hyperthreading status, allowing administrators to mitigate side-channel risks by disabling hyperthreading or using CPU affinity settings.

Resource pools further enhance isolation by guaranteeing CPU and memory resources through reservations, limits, and shares, protecting VMs from contention.

VCF Automation prevents CPU overallocation by tracking virtual CPU thread allocations and blocking provisioning to fully utilized hosts, while VCF Operations monitors CPU Co-Stop Rate to identify scheduling conflicts.

For workloads demanding hardware-enforced execution isolation, confidential vSphere Pods leveraging AMD SEV-ES prevent CPU register information leakage, even to the hypervisor.

While VCF provides comprehensive isolation at the hypervisor and virtual machine layers, the direct responsibility for maintaining separate execution domains for each thread within a guest operating system and its applications remains with the guest operating system and the application design itself.

Description: Mechanisms exist to isolate, or logically separate, any application, service and/or process running with system privileges.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust, multi-layered mechanisms to isolate and logically separate applications, services, and processes running with system privileges.

At the hypervisor layer, each virtual machine operates within its own isolated address space, leveraging hardware-assisted isolation boundaries enforced by the ESX hypervisor to prevent unauthorized resource access. For containerized workloads, vSphere Supervisor utilizes namespaces to establish resource boundaries, segregating vSphere Pods into dedicated folders with VM-level resource isolation and enforcing default firewall rules.

Network-level traffic separation is achieved through the Workload Separation Distributed Switch Model, which physically separates management-plane traffic from workload traffic.

Role-based access control (RBAC) is implemented across VCF, NSX, and VCF Operations, restricting user and service interaction with privileged objects through predefined and custom roles scoped to minimum necessary privileges. Furthermore, VCF Automation ensures each organization operates in an isolated environment, with network profiles supporting security-group-based isolation for deployed workloads.

VMware vDefend enhances this isolation by providing a Distributed Firewall that enforces granular network-level segmentation at the virtual NIC within the ESX hypervisor. Its microsegmentation capabilities allow for logical separation of privileged services from general workloads using security groups based on workload attributes, with policies restricting east-west traffic. The Distributed Firewall organizes rules into policy categories, supporting both broad zone-based segmentation and fine-grained application ringfencing, with Security Intelligence and Security Services Platform offering visibility and recommendations to maintain and verify these isolation boundaries consistently across federated environments.

While the platform provides these inherent mechanisms, the effective implementation and ongoing enforcement of isolation for specific applications, services, and processes running with system privileges require customer configuration. Organizations must define and apply appropriate RBAC roles, create and manage security groups, and establish granular firewall policies to align with their specific security requirements and operational models.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms to prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources. At the memory layer, ESX supports memory eager zeroing, which clears memory contents on the host to prevent one workload from reading residual data left by a previous workload. When virtual machines are deallocated, their memory pages are scrubbed before being made available to other workloads. For workloads requiring stronger guarantees, confidential vSphere Pods utilize AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization-Encrypted State (SEV-ES) to keep guest OS memory encrypted, protecting it against access from the hypervisor itself and preventing CPU registers from leaking information between execution domains.

At the storage layer, VCF supports virtual disk provisioning formats such as Thick Provisioned Eager Zeroed disks, which zero out any remaining data on the physical device upon creation, and Thin Provisioned disks, which zero data on demand at first write. vSphere virtual machine encryption encrypts virtual machine files, virtual disk files, core dump files, NVRAM, swap, and snapshot files, rendering residual data unreadable even if the underlying storage is shared. vSAN data-at-rest encryption encrypts everything in the vSAN datastore, protecting all files and virtual machines stored there from unauthorized access at the storage layer, including core dumps generated on ESX hosts.

VCF also provides several settings at the VM isolation layer that restrict information channels between VMs and the hypervisor, such as preventing unauthorized disk wiping operations, device modification, device connection, VIX messaging between VMs and the host, and VM Monitor Control interactions.

Network isolation prevents information transfer between workloads across shared network infrastructure through VLAN segmentation, which separates traffic types onto isolated network paths. The Workload Separation Distributed Switch Model places workload traffic on dedicated physical NICs, preventing shared network resources from becoming an information transfer path. NSX shared resources are made available to projects in read-only mode, preventing users in one project from modifying resources shared from another context.

VMware vDefend further prevents unauthorized and unintended information transfer across shared network resources through microsegmentation, traffic flow control, and zero-trust policy enforcement. The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces security policies at the vNIC level, preventing workloads sharing the same physical host, network segment, or cluster from communicating unless explicitly authorized by policy. This ensures isolation persists even when workloads share the same physical switch, host, or VLAN. The Security Services Platform extends this isolation model through environment-based segmentation and application-level policy recommendations, allowing administrators to configure block policies to prevent traffic between environment pairs that should never communicate. Gateway Firewall provides a complementary enforcement point at the perimeter of network segments, controlling north-south traffic and preventing information from leaking across zone boundaries through shared routing infrastructure. vDefend Malware Prevention adds a file-level control by inspecting files transferred to Windows workload VMs for malicious content, providing visibility into file transfer activity that could indicate unauthorized information movement.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to prevent the execution of unauthorized software programs.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements multiple automated mechanisms to prevent the execution of unauthorized software across the infrastructure stack. At the hypervisor layer, ESX leverages UEFI Secure Boot to verify digital signatures of all boot components, ensuring only trusted software loads. Trusted Platform Module (TPM) integration enables host attestation, confirming hosts boot into a known-good configuration. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) enforces a desired-state model for host software, detecting and remediating drift from approved images, while vSphere Configuration Profiles extend this to host settings. For virtual machines, VCF supports UEFI Secure Boot at the VM level and provides vTPM for secure key storage and measured boot. Content libraries enforce OVF security policies, requiring strict certificate verification for templates, ensuring only approved publishers can deploy VMs. VCF Operations orchestrator restricts file system access by default through `js-io-rights.conf`, preventing unauthorized scripts or binaries from running. For container workloads, VCF integrates with Harbor registry and VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) validates TLS certificates when fetching images, requiring registry certificates to be signed by a trusted certificate authority.

VMware vDefend further enhances these capabilities through its Malware Prevention Service, which provides automated detection and prevention of both file-based and fileless malware on protected workloads. vDefend intercepts and analyzes a broad range of executable file types using static and dynamic analysis, blocking malicious files from executing. For fileless attacks, it integrates with Microsoft's Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) to intercept and analyze scripts running directly in process memory, monitoring indicators of compromise to detect attacks without disk artifacts. Administrators can manage detection outcomes using the Allowlist feature, which permits overriding verdicts for legitimate software while maintaining automated prevention for genuinely unauthorized programs.

The comprehensive suite of controls, from boot integrity and desired state enforcement to runtime malware prevention and content validation, provides robust automated prevention against unauthorized software execution.

Description: Mechanisms exist to determine the Mean Time to Failure (MTTF) for system components in specific environments of operation.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust monitoring, trending, and proactive health management capabilities essential for predictable failure analysis of infrastructure components.

VCF Operations continuously collects and correlates metrics over time, identifying trends that may indicate potential failures. System uptime is meticulously tracked per host, and the integrated capacity analytics engine leverages historical utilization data with predictive statistical models to project future resource demands and identify components approaching their capacity limits. Furthermore, VCF Operations correlates events from all monitored systems, surfacing them as faults that represent conditions impacting the availability of objects within the environment.

vSAN, a core component of VCF, includes Proactive Health Management (PHM) with configurable hardware failure event check intervals, allowing administrators to customize the frequency of these checks. vSAN continuously monitors component health, providing immediate alerts regarding the type and severity of component failures. Upon failure, vSAN marks components as absent or degraded based on severity and initiates appropriate durability responses for both planned events, such as entering maintenance mode, and unplanned failures like disk or host failures and network disconnects.

These integrated capabilities establish a comprehensive infrastructure for data collection, trend analysis, and proactive alerting, which are foundational for failure prediction.

While VCF provides the necessary data collection, trend analysis, and proactive alerting infrastructure, it does not calculate Mean Time to Failure (MTTF) as a discrete, explicit metric. Organizations are responsible for leveraging VCF's extensive monitoring data as inputs to their own established reliability engineering processes to perform the formal determination and calculation of MTTF.

Description: Mechanisms exist to manage the usable lifecycles of Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides integrated lifecycle management tools that span the entire usable lifecycle of technology assets, applications, and services, from initial deployment through updates, upgrades, and decommissioning. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages ESX hosts at the cluster level using a desired-state image model, encompassing the ESX base image, firmware add-ons, vendor add-ons, and third-party solution components. vLCM continuously checks host compliance against this specification and remediates any drift. Hardware Support Manager (HSM) plugins extend this capability to vendor-specific firmware and driver management, ensuring hardware components remain at validated versions alongside software. Complementary vSphere Configuration Profiles provide a desired-state mechanism for host configuration settings, complete with drift detection and remediation.

For VCF core components, the VCF Operations UI, starting with VCF 9, centralizes lifecycle management. The upgrade planner enables administrators to select target versions, download upgrade bundles, and execute pre-upgrade prechecks that validate component connectivity, version compatibility, and environment readiness. SDDC Manager orchestrates the actual upgrade process across vCenter, NSX, ESX, and vSAN, performing version-specific prechecks. The vCenter patching API also includes its own pre-check operation for upgrade readiness. For Kubernetes workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) offers self-service lifecycle management of VKS clusters, where updating a Supervisor triggers rolling updates of deployed VKS clusters, including Kubernetes software versions, VM configurations, and supporting infrastructure. VKS supports running different Kubernetes versions in separate clusters for staged rollouts, and the VCF CLI provides a dedicated command for upgrading workload clusters. VCF Lifecycle Management further includes scheduling and planning tools to facilitate update windows and coordinate changes, minimizing disruption to running workloads.

Description: Mechanisms exist to enable systems to fail to an organization-defined known-state for types of failures, preserving system state information in failure.
Response:
The platform provides extensive mechanisms to ensure systems fail to a predictable, known state while preserving critical system state information.

vSphere Fault Tolerance maintains continuous availability for protected VMs, ensuring workloads remain in a known-good state identical to the moment before failure. vSphere High Availability automatically restarts VMs on surviving hosts, leveraging admission control to guarantee resources for recovery. vSAN fault domains ensure data resilience, rebuilding affected components to their policy-defined state. VCF Operations clusters tolerate node or fault domain loss, continuing in a degraded but functional state. VMware Live Site Recovery is designed to fail to a known, deterministic state during disaster recovery, handling split-brain scenarios by explicitly flagging inconsistent states for administrator resolution.

VMware vDefend contributes to fail-secure behavior by maintaining security enforcement continuity; the Distributed Firewall ensures policies travel with workloads, and its default-deny posture establishes a fail-secure model. The Gateway Firewall provides stateful failover, pre-synchronizing flow state tables to preserve TCP connections through switchover events. Recovery plan history, event logs, and point-in-time replication snapshots preserve system state information, providing administrators with the context needed for informed resolution decisions. The Security Services Platform supports backup and restore operations that preserve configuration and security policy state, ensuring the platform returns to a known configuration. NSX Federation further ensures cross-site policy continuity, preserving the security posture in a known state during site-level failures.

While the platform provides these foundational capabilities, the organization is responsible for defining the specific known-states, configuring the various resilience mechanisms (such as HA admission control policies, vSAN storage policies, DR recovery plans, and firewall rules), and establishing the operational procedures for managing and responding to system failures to align with their defined requirements.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement fail-safe procedures when failure conditions occur.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms to implement fail-safe procedures across its compute, storage, networking, and lifecycle management layers.

vSphere HA automatically restarts virtual machines upon host failures, utilizing admission control policies to reserve necessary resources. For critical workloads, vSphere Fault Tolerance maintains synchronized secondary VMs for instantaneous failover. vSAN enforces storage policies, automatically rebuilding data components on other hosts following failures and extending protection through fault domains. VCF Operations monitors the environment, generating alerts with recommended actions for various conditions, including network intent violations. Lifecycle management operations incorporate precheck validations to prevent issues during upgrades and patching, and require post-recovery verification. VMware Live Recovery further enhances fail-safe capabilities for disaster recovery through controlled recovery plan execution, configurable concurrent power-on limits, and admission control to prevent resource exhaustion at recovery sites. It ensures recovery operations continue even if the protected site fails during failover and prevents premature failover declarations with configurable site-down detection thresholds. VMware vDefend contributes to platform resilience by integrating with vSphere HA for host-level failure recovery of Security Services Platform deployments and enforcing a healthy-state prerequisite before backup and restore operations.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms for fail-safe operations, customers are responsible for defining specific recovery plans within VMware Live Recovery, configuring admission control policies, and establishing appropriate site-down detection thresholds.

Additionally, customers must define and implement operational procedures for responding to alerts and recommendations generated by VCF Operations, ensuring timely investigation and remediation of identified conditions.

Broader fail-safe security controls, such as default-deny firewall postures during outages or kernel-level enforcement continuity, are inherent to the underlying NSX/vDefend distributed firewall architecture and require appropriate configuration and policy definition by the customer.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement non-persistent system components and services that are initiated in a known state and terminated upon the end of the session of use or periodically at an organization-defined frequency.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for implementing non-persistent system components and services. VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) supports ephemeral pod workloads that initiate from a defined container image, ensuring a known state, and terminate upon task completion, with ephemeral storage disappearing at the end of the pod lifecycle. For infrastructure, vSphere Auto Deploy enables stateless ESX hosts that boot from a server-provided image and host profile, establishing a known-good configuration at each initiation. This is further enhanced by vSphere Lifecycle Manager, which enforces desired-state image management, detecting and remediating any configuration drift. Additionally, VM templates and instant clones facilitate the rapid provisioning of virtual machines from a known-good base image, allowing for customization and subsequent termination after their intended use. VCF Automation can orchestrate the deployment and lifecycle management of these non-persistent virtual machines.

While the platform offers comprehensive technical capabilities to support non-persistent patterns, the architectural decision to implement these patterns for specific workloads, define the "known state," and establish the frequency or conditions for termination rests with the organization deploying on VCF. The platform provides the necessary tools; the customer is responsible for their strategic application and the associated policy and process definitions.

Description: Mechanisms exist to ensure that software and data needed for system component and service refreshes are obtained from trusted sources.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive technical mechanisms to ensure that software and data required for system component and service refreshes originate from trusted sources. SDDC Manager centrally manages the lifecycle of VCF components, obtaining software updates from VMware-hosted online depots or through UMDS for air-gapped environments, with all updates validated against Broadcom-maintained repositories. vSphere Lifecycle Manager enforces a desired state model, validating host compliance and remediating drift, while ESX enforces code signing for hypervisor components and Secure Boot validates the boot chain. For containerized workloads, VCF integrates with Harbor, which supports image signing and content trust. Certificate management operations fetch updates from configured trusted certificate authorities, and vSphere Content Libraries support security policies for validating VM templates and other distributed content.

While these robust mechanisms are inherent to the platform, the customer is responsible for defining and configuring the specific trust policies and sources relevant to their environment. This includes establishing and maintaining trusted certificate authorities, configuring and enforcing image signing policies within Harbor, and setting content validation policies for vSphere Content Libraries. The customer must also ensure these mechanisms are appropriately configured, monitored, and integrated into their organizational processes to fully align with their definition of trusted sources for all software and data refreshes.

Description: Mechanisms exist to validate information output from software programs and/or applications to ensure that the information is consistent with the expected content.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) incorporates multiple mechanisms to validate information output from its management and automation components, ensuring consistency with expected content.

VCF Operations widgets provide an Output Filter capability, including tag-based filtering, which restricts displayed data based on selected criteria, allowing administrators to verify that information aligns with the expected scope. The VCF Operations orchestrator includes a workflow validation tool and an input form designer that can validate workflow inputs using external actions, ensuring that workflow execution yields results consistent with expectations. Within VCF Automation, the custom form designer facilitates the review of non-validatable actions for appropriate input validation, and approval policies can be configured to introduce a human review layer for changes to deployed catalog instances. Furthermore, vSphere Lifecycle Manager validates software specifications before application, with the Image Manager performing checks prior to saving draft specifications, and administrators can validate host compliance against a desired state, ensuring the software configuration output matches the intended state.

While VCF provides these robust platform-level validation and filtering mechanisms, the customer is responsible for defining specific validation criteria, establishing the expected content, and implementing the organizational processes and review procedures tailored to the applications and workloads running on the VCF environment.

Description: Mechanisms exist to limit the dissemination of Personal Data (PD) to organization-defined elements identified in the Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and consistent with authorized purposes.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to implement security safeguards to protect system memory from unauthorized code execution.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust, multi-layered security safeguards to protect system memory from unauthorized code execution.

At the hardware level, ESX supports Secure Boot, validating the boot chain, and integrates with TPM for measured boot and host attestation. Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT) establishes a hardware-based root of trust for the hypervisor.

For workloads requiring advanced memory protection, VCF supports AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV/SEV-ES) and Intel Trust Domain Extensions (TDX) to encrypt VM memory and CPU register state, preventing unauthorized access from the hypervisor or other VMs. Confidential vSphere Pods extend this hardware memory encryption to containerized workloads, and vSGX can be configured for hardware-protected memory enclaves.

The hypervisor itself enforces code signing, isolates each virtual machine in its own address space, and offers eager zeroing of VM memory to prevent data leakage.

Furthermore, Harbor registry integration supports image signing for containerized workloads, and vSphere Configuration Profiles ensure host-level security settings, including memory protection, remain in their desired state.

VMware vDefend significantly contributes to memory protection through its Malware Prevention capabilities, specifically designed to detect and respond to unauthorized code execution within system memory at runtime.

This service includes advanced fileless malware detection, identifying attacks that operate in memory without leaving disk artifacts. It intercepts in-memory scripts via Microsoft's Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI), covering PowerShell, VBScript, and JScript, to detect direct injection and execution of malicious content within process memory.

By continuously monitoring process activities, parent-child relationships, and memory buffer content, vDefend identifies potential in-memory script executions and tracks Indicators of Compromise, providing visibility and assigning verdicts for unauthorized code execution attempts within protected workloads.

These comprehensive hardware, hypervisor, and runtime protections collectively ensure system memory is safeguarded against unauthorized code execution.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize honeypots that are specifically designed to be the target of malicious attacks for the purpose of detecting, deflecting and analyzing such attacks.
Response:
VMware vDefend provides robust capabilities that significantly enhance the effectiveness of honeypot strategies.

Its Security Intelligence and Network Detection and Response (NDR) features deliver continuous traffic flow analysis and advanced anomaly detection, crucial for identifying lateral movement and suspicious connections directed at decoy assets.

The Distributed Firewall's microsegmentation capabilities are instrumental in isolating honeypot segments, establishing stringent policy boundaries to contain and observe malicious activity.

Furthermore, vDefend's Security Intelligence and integrated IDS/IPS actively monitor for anomalous activities, such as suspicious SSH and RDP usage within the datacenter, providing critical insights into attack methodologies.

While vDefend offers comprehensive network visibility and policy enforcement, it does not natively deploy or manage the honeypot infrastructure itself. The organization is responsible for the design, deployment, and ongoing management of the honeypot systems, including defining their specific purpose, configuring their deceptive characteristics, and integrating them into the broader security architecture. The effectiveness of the overall deceptive technology control objective relies on the customer's implementation of the honeypots, leveraging vDefend's capabilities to secure, monitor, and analyze interactions with these decoy systems.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize honeyclients that proactively seek to identify malicious websites and/or web-based malicious code.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize a diverse set of technologies for system components to reduce the impact of technical vulnerabilities from the same Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a virtualization abstraction layer that decouples workloads from the underlying physical hardware, enabling the deployment of diverse guest operating systems and applications on hardware from multiple OEMs without altering the software stack. The platform is certified to operate on hardware from various server vendors. SDDC Manager integrates with vendor-specific management interfaces, such as Dell iDRAC, HPE iLO, and Cisco UCS Manager, to deliver unified lifecycle management across heterogeneous physical infrastructure. This capability allows organizations to source server hardware from different OEMs and manage it through a single platform, effectively mitigating concentration risk associated with any single hardware vendor.

VCF Lifecycle Management meticulously tracks software component versions and compatibility across the environment, ensuring that updates to one component do not introduce conflicts with others. Furthermore, vSAN system baselines identify the optimal available firmware and driver releases, maintaining hardware in a supported and current state.

The strategic decision to procure and deploy hardware from diverse Original Equipment Manufacturers remains an organizational procurement and architecture decision. While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the essential platform abstraction and multi-vendor management capabilities that make a heterogeneous hardware strategy practical to operate, the actual implementation of this diversity is the responsibility of the customer.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize virtualization techniques to support the employment of a diversity of operating systems and applications.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation is fundamentally designed around virtualization, providing the core mechanisms to support a wide array of operating systems and applications. Each virtual machine operates within an isolated environment, leveraging its own virtual hardware independent of the underlying physical infrastructure.

vSphere, as a foundational component, natively supports a broad spectrum of guest operating systems, encompassing various Linux distributions, Windows versions, and other platforms. Virtual machines are the central, independent elements within this environment, utilizing virtual disks for their operating systems, program files, and data, with inherent capabilities for migration, snapshotting, and cloning.

Further enhancing this diversity, the Virtual Machine Service introduces virtual machine classes, enabling the definition of standardized, reusable hardware configurations tailored for different workload types.

VCF Operations HCX extends this capability by facilitating OS-assisted migration of workloads from disparate hypervisors, such as KVM and Hyper-V, seamlessly integrating these diverse operating systems into the VCF ecosystem.

Moreover, VMware Kubernetes Service operates alongside traditional virtual machines under a unified control plane, allowing organizations to manage both VM-based and containerized applications with consistent infrastructure policies, thereby supporting a comprehensive range of application architectures.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize concealment and misdirection techniques for Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) to confuse and mislead adversaries.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to introduce randomness into organizational operations and assets.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Automated mechanisms exist to change the location of processing and/or storage at random time intervals.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to distribute processing and storage across multiple physical locations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation inherently provides robust mechanisms for distributing processing and storage across multiple physical locations. vSAN Stretched Clusters deliver site-level resilience by distributing both compute and storage across two distinct physical sites, leveraging vSAN as a fully distributed, scalable storage solution.

Workload mobility is facilitated through VMware vMotion and HCX, enabling the movement of running virtual machines between physical locations. VCF Automation extends these capabilities with placement policies such as SPREAD_MEMORY and compute spread policies, which distribute virtual machine resources across cloud zones and evaluate storage profiles organized under cloud-specific regions. Distributed Resource Scheduler (DRS) further ensures the spread of virtual machines across hosts within a cluster.

VMware Live Site Recovery orchestrates disaster recovery, replicating protected workloads and their data between geographically separated primary and recovery sites, maintaining distributed copies of virtual machine data. Site pairing and inventory mappings establish the necessary relationships for distributed vCenter instances, supporting bidirectional protection configurations where sites serve dual roles.

VMware vDefend further contributes to this distributed architecture by providing a federated management model where a Global Manager centrally controls security policies, IDS/IPS signatures, and firewall rules across multiple Local Managers deployed at different physical locations.

The Distributed Firewall supports customized regions spanning multiple Local Managers, ensuring security policy follows the multi-site distribution of workloads.

The Security Services Platform, which provides analytics and security intelligence, supports deployment on vSAN stretched clusters, replicating its persistent storage between two distinct sites. This ensures the security analytics and data storage layer is distributed alongside the compute infrastructure it monitors. The platform's scale-out architecture uses Container Storage Interface (CSI) with tag-based placement rules, allowing security data processing infrastructure to grow and distribute alongside workload deployments.

The Distributed IDS/IPS component can be managed across multiple Local Managers, ensuring security inspection occurs locally at each physical location.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize non-modifiable executable programs that load and execute the operating environment and applications from hardware-enforced, read-only media.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize a trusted communications path between the user and the security functions of the system.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes trusted communications paths between users and the system's security functions through robust encryption, centralized authentication, and certificate management. All management interfaces and API endpoints enforce Transport Layer Security (TLS) encryption, creating secure channels for administrators interacting with critical security functions such as authentication configuration, role-based access control, and certificate management. VCF supports FIPS-validated cryptographic modules for both internal and external connections, which can be enabled in components like VCF Operations for Networks to restrict connections to FIPS mode, further enhancing trust.

Authentication is centrally managed by VCF SSO, providing single sign-on across VCF components including VMware vCenter, VCF Operations, and VCF Operations for Logs. VCF SSO redirects users to a dedicated authentication page for credential entry and integrates with external identity providers for centralized authentication. Certificates issued by the VMware Certificate Authority authenticate solution users, ensuring the path between users and security functions is verified through a trusted certificate chain. VCF Operations supports Digest access authentication for its SOAP-based orchestrator plug-in, and mutual CHAP authentication can be enabled where applicable. Network components require authentication before establishing management connections, and all administrative accounts are password-protected.

Remote logging connections utilize TLS or LI-TLS protocols for encrypted log transmission, extending the trusted path to audit and monitoring functions. VMware vDefend further contributes to secure log-on procedures by supporting TLS-secured communications paths for remote logging, requiring CA-signed certificates to configure secure connections to remote log servers for the Malware Prevention Service, and ensuring secure transmission of malware analysis and product logs.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize system use notification / logon banners that display an approved system use notification message or banner before granting access to the system that provides cybersecurity and data protection notices.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation components consistently provide robust mechanisms for implementing system use notification and logon banners across their management interfaces. These capabilities allow organizations to display approved system use notifications, legal notices, terms of use, and cybersecurity/data protection messages before or during user authentication. NSX Manager, for instance, supports configurable security banners and login user agreement banners that display at every login when activated, designed to meet stringent compliance requirements. Similarly, VCF Operations for Networks and the VCF monitoring platform offer configurable login message banners that appear when users access the login page, with options to require explicit user consent for terms and conditions before proceeding. Furthermore, vSphere Supervisor cluster configurations support login banners that can be set during cluster setup, and CLI commands allow for configuring both pre-login and post-login banners for console access, ensuring comprehensive coverage.

While the platform provides the full technical capability to display these banners, the organization remains responsible for defining the specific content of the approved system use notification message and ensuring its activation and consistent configuration across all relevant VCF components. This includes establishing internal policies for banner content and review processes.

Description: Mechanisms exist to configure Microsoft Windows-based systems to display an approved logon banner before granting access to the system that provides cybersecurity and data protection notices.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize a truncated system use notification / logon banner on systems not capable of displaying a logon banner from a centralized source, such as Active Directory.
Response:
VCF management components are Linux-based appliances that do not participate in Active Directory Group Policy for banner distribution. Each component provides its own local mechanism to display system use notifications and logon warnings on these non-centrally-managed interfaces.

VCF Operations supports a configurable login message on the web UI login page that can require explicit user consent for terms and conditions or mandatory notices before granting access. VCF Operations for Networks provides login banners with a consent checkbox that users must acknowledge, configurable through the System Configuration settings with an enable/disable toggle. For SSH and console access, the `set-banner` CLI command configures both an SSH pre-authentication banner and a post-login banner for console users, with support for multi-line text. NSX Manager provides a login user agreement banner that displays at every login when activated, with modification restricted to users with the Enterprise Admin role to ensure change control over the notification content. Workspace ONE Access supports configuration of a government information system banner for environments that require specific legal or regulatory notice text at logon. These integrated capabilities ensure that VCF's management appliances can display necessary truncated system use notifications and logon banners.

While VCF provides the technical mechanisms for displaying these banners, the customer is responsible for defining the specific banner text, establishing consent requirements, and configuring these settings to align with their organizational policies and legal obligations.

Description: Mechanisms exist to configure systems that process, store or transmit sensitive/regulated data to notify the user, upon successful logon, of the number of unsuccessful logon attempts since the last successful logon.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize time-synchronization technology to synchronize all critical system clocks.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for time synchronization across all critical platform components. Network Time Protocol (NTP) is utilized for synchronizing ESX hosts, vCenter instances, NSX Manager and Edge nodes, SDDC Manager, and HCX, with Precision Time Protocol (PTP) available for workloads requiring sub-microsecond accuracy.

NTP configuration is a deployment prerequisite for ESX hosts, with the NTP client enabled and configured to start and stop with the host. SDDC Manager serves as the central point for NTP configuration, propagating changes across core VCF components, performing precheck validations, and rolling back failed updates to ensure consistency.

Accurate time synchronization is fundamental for TLS/SSO communication, Kerberos authentication, certificate validation, and consistent log timestamps essential for auditing and troubleshooting.

VMware vDefend components similarly incorporate and depend on robust time synchronization. Accurate clock synchronization is a prerequisite for vDefend's security policy enforcement, log correlation, certificate validation, and secure communications.

The Distributed Firewall and Gateway Firewall leverage time-based policies that require ESX host clocks to be synchronized with an NTP server for correct evaluation. The Security Services Platform, which manages and orchestrates vDefend, validates time synchronization during installation and relies on it for core functions. NDR sensor appliances are configured with NTP during deployment, and consistent timestamps across all vDefend components are critical for correlating security events and maintaining the integrity of forensic timelines.

While the platform provides the necessary mechanisms and enforces time synchronization as a prerequisite for many functions, administrators are responsible for configuring and verifying NTP server settings for components like bare metal deployments and NDR sensors to ensure consistent and accurate time across the entire security ecosystem.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize an application container (virtualization approach) to isolate to a known set of dependencies, access methods and interfaces.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for application container isolation through a virtualization approach.

The vSphere Supervisor platform enables Kubernetes workloads to run directly on ESX hosts, deploying them into vSphere Namespaces. These namespaces establish strong isolation boundaries by enforcing resource limits for CPU, memory, and storage, alongside granular user permissions, preventing cross-namespace interaction. For containerized workloads requiring VM-level isolation, the Container Runtime Executive (CRX) wraps each vSphere Pod within a VM boundary, leveraging hardware virtualization for hypervisor-enforced isolation, rather than solely relying on kernel namespaces. VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) further integrates with vSphere Namespaces, inheriting these resource quotas and access controls for workload clusters. VMware vDefend complements this by enforcing network-layer isolation policies for containerized workloads, controlling access methods, and utilizing a local container registry for platform components to support dependency isolation.

While VCF provides the foundational infrastructure-level isolation, resource boundaries, and the container runtime environment, organizations must implement additional application-layer security controls, define specific network policies, and utilize container image scanning tools to fully satisfy the requirements for isolating to a precisely known set of dependencies, access methods, and interfaces.

Description: Mechanisms exist to prevent privileged operating environments from existing within unprivileged operating environments, including physical or virtual deployments of Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS).
Response:
VMware vDefend Distributed Firewall provides the foundational mechanisms to prevent privileged operating environments from existing within unprivileged operating environments. Through workload-level microsegmentation, vDefend enforces zero-trust network policies, establishing strict isolation boundaries between distinct security domains, whether physical or virtual. The Security Services Platform continuously assesses the protection status of workloads, verifying the application of environment-specific segmentation policies and quantifying the strength of inter-environment controls, which is detailed in comprehensive policy coverage reports.

While the platform delivers the technical enforcement and assessment capabilities, the customer is responsible for defining the specific criteria for privileged and unprivileged operating environments within their deployment. This includes the meticulous crafting of segmentation policies and the diligent application of governance guidance to prevent overly permissive rules, such as ANY/ANY/ANY ALLOW, thereby ensuring least-privilege enforcement between privileged management networks and unprivileged workload segments.

Description: Mechanisms exist to provide guidelines and recommendations for the secure use of Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) to assist in the configuration, installation and use of the product and/or service.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms to deliver comprehensive guidelines and recommendations for the secure use, configuration, installation, and ongoing operation of its technology assets, applications, and services. The VMware Security Configuration Guide serves as the primary hardening reference, detailing secure practices for ESX hosts, vCenter, NSX, vSphere networking, and storage components, covering aspects such as authentication, authorization, service management, network security, and data protection. Broadcom further augments this with DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) and Security Requirements Guides (SRGs), offering detailed instructions for securing operating systems, applications, and network devices across critical security domains including access control, identification, authentication, audit, accountability, and encryption. The VCF Security Baseline integrates these recommendations into a continuous compliance management capability, monitoring infrastructure against published benchmarks, detecting violations, highlighting risks, and providing actionable remediation recommendations, with options for automated remediation to maintain compliance.

VMware vDefend complements these platform-level controls by providing its own extensive product documentation, which offers component-specific guidance for secure configuration and operational practices.

This includes detailed instructions for configuring Distributed and Gateway Firewall rules and policies, microsegmentation strategies, identity-based firewall configurations, URL filtering, and TLS inspection setup.

The Security Services Platform documentation outlines system requirements, deployment prerequisites, licensing, port specifications, and secure communication practices using certificates and TLS, alongside a guided Security Segmentation Journey workflow.

Security Intelligence documentation provides recommendations for generating and deploying firewall policies based on observed traffic patterns, including assessment modes and rule analysis reports.

Furthermore, the vDefend Advanced Threat Prevention documentation covers IDS/IPS signature management, Malware Prevention sandboxing, and Network Detection and Response setup, ensuring administrators have the necessary context for deploying these capabilities securely.

Description: Mechanisms exist to detect the presence of unauthorized Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) in use.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for detecting unauthorized technology assets, applications, and services within the virtualized environment.

VCF Operations includes a Service Discovery Adapter that identifies running services and applications within guest operating systems, grouping them into applications and monitoring their resource utilization and connections. VCF Operations for Networks further enhances this by performing flow-based application discovery, analyzing network traffic to identify communication patterns, highlight unprotected flows, and flag applications communicating with the internet, which are key indicators of unexpected or unauthorized services.

Network auto-discovery identifies network devices, and configuration drift detection monitors vCenter instances and vSphere clusters against desired-state templates, generating notifications for any deviations. Event auditing provides visibility into platform interactions and suspicious access.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities with deep network-layer visibility. Security Intelligence collects and visualizes traffic flows between VMs and workloads, enabling administrators to identify unexpected communication patterns and applications operating outside established security policies. The Security Services Platform monitors application flows, highlighting unprotected traffic and classifying applications based on their adherence to security rules. The Suspicious Traffic feature, leveraging advanced detection algorithms, identifies and flags anomalous network behaviors, correlating detection events into campaigns to pinpoint unauthorized services through deviations from established baselines.

Additionally, a properly configured Distributed Firewall indirectly detects unauthorized applications by generating rule hits against deny policies or appearing as unprotected flows, making their presence visible in monitoring dashboards within a zero-trust framework.

While these integrated capabilities provide comprehensive technical detection within the virtual infrastructure and network layers, the full scope of detecting all forms of shadow IT across an entire organization, including unauthorized SaaS usage, unapproved personal devices, or services provisioned outside the VCF environment, requires the implementation of additional organizational processes, policies, and potentially other specialized tools.

Description: Mechanisms exist to utilize integrity validation mechanisms for security updates.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements comprehensive integrity validation mechanisms for security updates across its lifecycle management and operational components.

SDDC Manager automatically verifies the file size and checksum of all downloaded upgrade bundles, whether from online depots, offline depots, or manually uploaded, prior to application. It also executes version-specific prechecks to identify potential issues before an update commences.

vCenter further enhances this through its patching API, which includes a pre-check operation to validate upgrade readiness, and its Image Manager, which performs validation before saving software specifications.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager actively enforces image compliance across clusters, ensuring hosts adhere to expected software specifications.

VCF Operations includes a pre-update validation process, enabled by default, to identify issues before updates begin, complemented by NSX's Upgrade Coordinator, which provides post-check verification and consistency checks.

For management packs and extensions, VCF Operations verifies that PAK files are signed with a VMware digital signature, confirming authenticity. Similarly, NSX validates the signature of uploaded dynamic plugins using a public key.

At the container and CLI layer, the VCF CLI supports cosign-based image signature verification for plugins, with imgpkg capable of finding and copying these signatures, and administrators able to configure trusted signing authorities.

VMware vDefend integrates with and extends these integrity validation capabilities for its own component updates.

Security Intelligence provides a CLI command to verify upgrade bundles and includes prechecks for system readiness before migration.

vDefend IDS/IPS offers signature management with a manual mode, allowing administrators to control and validate signature versions before deployment into the detection pipeline.

The Security Services Platform conducts thorough validations prior to implementing configuration changes, ensuring correctness and compatibility, and monitors deployment status for errors.

These vDefend-specific mechanisms operate within the broader VCF integrity validation framework, leveraging platform-level controls such as SDDC Manager's checksum verification and the VCF CLI's cosign-based image signature verification, collectively ensuring robust integrity validation for all security updates.

Description: Mechanisms exist to require the developers of Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services (TAAS) to identify early in the Secure Development Life Cycle (SDLC), the functions, ports, protocols and services intended for use.
Response:
VMware vDefend Distributed Firewall and Gateway Firewall capabilities enforce network-level allowlists of permitted ports, protocols, and services. This provides runtime validation, ensuring that only authorized communications occur across the infrastructure. The Security Services Platform further monitors critical infrastructure services such as DNS, LDAP, NTP, SYSLOG, SNMP, and DHCP on their specified ports and protocols. Its recommendation analytics facilitate filtering traffic flows by port ranges and protocols, which informs and refines network policy decisions.

While the platform actively enforces network authorizations at the network layer, it does not govern the Secure Development Life Cycle (SDLC) process itself. The customer is responsible for establishing and implementing the mechanisms that require developers to identify functions, ports, protocols, and services early in the SDLC.
